The superior performance of FakeFlow confirms that capturing the flow of affective information is important for fake news detection.
ProFormer uses an LSH projection layer to generate word representations on-the-fly without embedding lookup tables, reducing memory footprint from O(V·d) to O(T).
ProFormer includes a local projection attention (LPA) layer that transforms N LSH word projections into N/K representations, reducing computation quadratically by O(K²).
ProFormer achieves state-of-the-art performance among on-device models for short text classification tasks.
ProFormer attains performance comparable to BERT on long text classification tasks while requiring substantially less computational resources.
ProFormer reduces embedding memory usage by several orders of magnitude and lowers computation overhead by an order of magnitude, making it the fastest and smallest on-device transformer model.
Path Type Adaptive Pretraining sequentially trains a relation extraction model with different types of universal graph paths to prevent reliance on a single path type.

Complexity Ranking Guided Attention mechanism restricts the attention span according to the complexity of a universal graph path, encouraging extraction of features from both simple and complex paths.
Extending a state-of-the-art distant supervised relation extraction model with a universal graph degrades performance due to difficulty learning the universal graph.
The proposed training strategies improve the robustness of relation extraction over universal graphs on biomedical and NYT10 datasets.
The proposed methods achieve a new state-of-the-art result on the NYT10 dataset for distant supervised relation extraction.
A technique for quality estimation uses synthetic training data instead of human-annotated examples.
Off-the-shelf architectures can be trained for supervised quality estimation using synthetic data.
Models trained on synthetic data achieve comparable performance to models trained on human-annotated data for sentence-level quality estimation.
Models trained on synthetic data achieve comparable performance to models trained on human-annotated data for word-level quality estimation.
Historical text summarisation generates summaries of documents written in historical language forms in the corresponding modern language.
A gold-standard dataset of historical German and Chinese news summarised in modern German or Chinese is compiled.
A summarisation model based on cross-lingual transfer learning can be trained without historical-to-modern parallel data.
The model is benchmarked against state-of-the-art summarisation algorithms.
The transfer learning model outperforms standard cross-lingual summarisation benchmarks on the historical-to-modern summarisation task.
A biaffine parsing head is attached to a pretrained transformer to incorporate Universal Dependencies syntactic information.
Intermediate parsing training is applied to the transformer before fine-tuning on language understanding tasks.
Injecting explicit syntactic knowledge via intermediate parsing training yields limited and inconsistent effects on downstream language understanding performance.
The limited and inconsistent effect of intermediate parsing training is observed in both monolingual English experiments and zero-shot language transfer experiments with target-language parsing.
Analysis of transformer representation spaces before and after intermediate parsing training shows minimal alteration of the representations.
GLaRA learns new labeling rules for named entity recognition from unlabeled data using a graph-based augmentation framework.
GLaRA constructs a graph whose nodes represent candidate labeling rules extracted from unlabeled data.
GLaRA employs a graph neural network to augment labeling rules by exploiting semantic relations between rules.
GLaRA generates weak labels with the augmented rules and trains a named entity recognition model on the weakly labeled data.
GLaRA achieves a substantial improvement over the best baseline on three named entity recognition datasets when using a small set of seed rules.
A self-training method uses the output of a direct transfer parser as implicit supervision for unsupervised cross-lingual dependency parsing.
The unsupervised cross-lingual transfer method accepts any pre-trained arc-factored dependency parser.
The unsupervised cross-lingual transfer method operates without access to source language data.
The unsupervised cross-lingual transfer method supports both projective and non-projective dependency parsing.
The unsupervised cross-lingual transfer method supports multi-source transfer.
When English is used as the source language, the method achieves significant improvements over state-of-the-art transfer models on both distant and nearby languages.
Analyses demonstrate that the choice of source languages influences the effectiveness of multi-source transfer.
Analyses demonstrate that non-projective parsing provides an advantage in unsupervised cross-lingual transfer.
Hierarchical Multi-head Attentive Network combines multi-head word-level attention and multi-head document-level attention for fact-checking textual claims.
The network provides explanations at both the word level and the evidence level.
The network outperforms seven state-of-the-art baselines on two real-world fake news detection datasets.
A typological blinding technique that prevents a cross-lingual model from exploiting typological information is introduced.
The cross-lingual model employs latent weights that are learned during training to govern language sharing.
Preventing the model from exploiting typology severely reduces its performance.
Encouraging language sharing according to typological information improves the model's performance.
Strategic sentence-level perturbations are introduced as a method for evaluating neural model robustness to adversarial attacks.
A model for predicting errors during adversarial attacks is developed.
Models using ELMo embeddings are more susceptible to adversarial attacks than models using BERT embeddings.
Word-level perturbations cause more high-confidence misclassifications than sentence- and character-level perturbations.
Question type and answer length are identified as the most predictive factors of model errors in adversarial settings.
A meta-learning approach learns interactions between tasks and languages for NLP.
The impact of different sampling strategies on meta-learning is investigated.
Experiments on five tasks and six languages from the XTREME benchmark evaluate the meta-learning approach.
The meta-learned model outperforms competitive baseline models, including multi-task baselines.
Zero-shot evaluations on unseen target languages demonstrate the utility of the meta-learned model.
Reinforcement learning fine-tuning uses a reward function that explicitly captures consistency between a response and persona facts and semantic plausibility.
Fine-tuning with this reward function increases the rate of factually consistent responses compared to supervised training.
Fine-tuning with this reward function retains the language quality of responses.
Automatic and human evaluations on the PersonaChat corpus confirm the increase in factual consistency of responses.
A large-scale dataset of manually edited syntax graphs for coordinate constructions in Enhanced Universal Dependencies for English is created.
Systematic errors in existing enhanced UD English treebanks are identified, and adjunct propagation is proposed.
A principled comparison between rule-based and machine-learning methods for conjunction propagation in English is performed.
Learning propagation rules is more effective than hand-designed heuristic rules for conjunction propagation.
A neural graph-parser based edge predictor outperforms pipelines that use a basic-layer tree parser plus converters for conjunction propagation with automatic parses.
AdapterFusion is a two-stage learning algorithm that extracts task-specific adapters and composes them to combine knowledge from multiple tasks.
AdapterFusion enables non-destructive exploitation of representations learned from multiple tasks.
AdapterFusion effectively combines various types of knowledge at different model layers.
AdapterFusion outperforms full fine-tuning and multi-task learning on diverse natural language understanding tasks.
A neural end-to-end argument mining approach based on biaffine dependency parsing is introduced.
The biaffine argument mining dependency parser outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods on component identification and relation identification.
The approach results in a simple neural architecture for argument mining.
An analysis identifies that the biaffine model, the argument dependency representation, encoder choices, and added syntactic information each contribute to the model's performance.
Treating argument mining as dependency parsing is shown to be a viable and promising direction.
An ontology-free framework enables natural language queries for unseen constraints and slots in multi-domain task-oriented dialogs.
The framework employs generative question-answering with a conditional language model pre-trained on English sentences.
The framework achieves higher joint goal accuracy in zero-shot domain adaptation for dialog state tracking compared to previous state-of-the-art methods.
An evaluation framework assesses neural machine translation's ability to continuously learn new phrases.
Integration of one-shot learning methods with diverse word representations enables neural machine translation to incorporate bilingual dictionaries for continuous learning.
Simultaneously addressing one-shot learning and morphological modeling enhances translation of new rare words and phrases.
Simultaneously addressing one-shot learning and morphological modeling increases generation of correct lemmas for new words.
Jointly predicting target and polarity BIO labels improves target extraction in fine-grained sentiment analysis.
Augmenting input text with gold polar expressions improves targeted polarity classification.
Current models for predicting polar expressions exhibit poor performance, limiting the practical benefit of expression information.
Experiments on eight English datasets assess the impact of incorporating holder and expression information on target extraction and classification.
Retrieving text passages improves the performance of generative models for open domain question answering.
State-of-the-art results are achieved on the Natural Questions and TriviaQA benchmarks.
Increasing the number of retrieved passages significantly improves the method's performance.
Sequence-to-sequence generative models provide a flexible framework to efficiently aggregate and combine evidence from multiple passages.
Polarized-VAE disentangles selected attributes in a VAE latent space by using proximity measures that reflect similarity between data points with respect to those attributes.
Polarized-VAE disentangles sentence semantics and syntax.
Polarized-VAE supports transfer experiments for attribute disentanglement.
Polarized-VAE outperforms a standard VAE baseline in attribute disentanglement.
Polarized-VAE achieves performance competitive with state-of-the-art attribute disentanglement methods.
Polarized-VAE provides a general framework applicable to other attribute disentanglement tasks.
An analysis method using synthetic and naturalistic NLI datasets with clause-embedding verbs evaluates transitivity in neural NLI models.
Current neural NLI models do not consistently succeed on transitivity inference tasks, indicating limited capacity for drawing composite inferences.
Synthetic and naturalistic NLI datasets and accompanying code for transitivity analysis are publicly released.
PolyLM formulates learning sense embeddings as a language modeling problem, enabling contextualization techniques to be applied to sense embeddings.
PolyLM assumes that a word's probability in a context equals the sum of its senses' probabilities and that a single sense is usually far more plausible than other senses for a given occurrence.
PolyLM outperforms previous sense embedding techniques on word sense induction and matches the state-of-the-art specialized WSI method while using far fewer parameters.
A generalized framework for identifying resisting strategies in persuasive conversations is proposed.
The framework is instantiated on two datasets of persuasion and negotiation conversations.
A hierarchical sequence-labeling neural architecture is used to automatically infer resisting strategies.
Power roles are asymmetric in non-collaborative goal-directed conversations.
Incorporating resisting strategies improves the final conversation outcome.
Different resisting strategies have distinct impacts on conversation outcome, corroborating prior findings.
A joint model performs entity-level relation extraction from documents.
The model operates on the entity level rather than on local intra-sentence mention pairs.
The model uses a multi-task approach that incorporates coreference resolution.
The model gathers signals via multi-instance learning with multi-level representations that combine global entity and local mention information.
The model achieves state-of-the-art relation extraction performance on the DocRED dataset.
The model provides the first entity-level end-to-end relation extraction results.

Joint learning of entity-level relation extraction and coreference resolution attains comparable effectiveness to task-specific learning while being more efficient due to shared parameters and training steps.
JEANS jointly represents multilingual knowledge graphs and text corpora in a shared embedding space.
JEANS employs an entity grounding process to combine each knowledge graph with a monolingual text corpus.
JEANS uses an embedding learning process to encode knowledge graphs and text of each language into a single embedding space.
JEANS uses a self-learning based alignment process to iteratively induce correspondences between entities and lexemes across embeddings.
JEANS improves entity alignment performance with incidental supervision from text and outperforms state-of-the-art methods that rely solely on internal knowledge graph information.
A 600K word-pair corpus for English-Indic transliteration, mined from public sources, constitutes the largest transliteration corpus for Indian languages.
A large-scale evaluation of neural machine transliteration for English-Indic language pairs is performed.
A detailed analysis of multilingual transliteration between English and Indic languages is conducted.
An improved multilingual training recipe for Indic language transliteration is proposed.
The impact of language family, transliteration direction, and word origin on transliteration quality is analyzed.
A top-down discourse parsing method frames the task as a sequence labeling problem that iteratively segments a document into discourse units.
The top-down method eliminates the decoder and reduces the search space for splitting points.
Both traditional recurrent models and modern pre-trained transformer models are applied to the top-down sequence labeling discourse parsing task.
A novel dynamic oracle is introduced for top-down parsing.
An LSTM model using the top-down sequence labeling approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on RST parsing according to the Full metric.
Adv-OLM generates textual adversarial examples using an occlusion language model to rank words for substitution.
Adv-OLM employs word replacement strategies to substitute ranked words in black-box attacks.
Adv-OLM outperforms existing attack methods on multiple text classification tasks.
An ablation-based methodology assesses the extent to which MRC datasets evaluate understanding of explicit discourse relations.
Seven MRC skills that require understanding of different discourse relations are defined.
Ablation methods verify whether these discourse-relation skills are required to succeed on an MRC dataset.
Performance drops of neural MRC models on original versus modified datasets measure the degree to which a dataset requires discourse-relation skills.

Experiments on three large-scale MRC datasets with BERT-base and ALBERT-xxlarge show that relative performance changes for all discourse-relation skills are small, indicating most questions do not require discourse structure understanding.
LASAGNE combines a transformer architecture with Graph Attention Networks for multi-task neural semantic parsing in conversational question answering over knowledge graphs.
LASAGNE includes an entity recognition module that detects, links, and ranks all relevant entities in the question context.
LASAGNE outperforms existing baselines on a standard dataset for complex sequential question answering across all question types.
LASAGNE is the first approach to employ a transformer extended with Graph Attention Networks for multi-task semantic parsing in conversational question answering over knowledge graphs.
A zero-shot passage retrieval approach uses synthetic question generation to create domain-specific question-passage relevance pairs.
The synthetic question generation system is trained on general-domain data and applied to documents in a targeted domain.
Combining synthetic question-generated data with a simple hybrid term-neural model improves first-stage retrieval performance.
The approach enables building neural passage retrieval models without large supervised training corpora.
The technique can approach the accuracy of supervised models in certain domains.
SANDI automatically selects images from an image collection and aligns them with story text paragraphs.
SANDI combines visual tags, user-provided tags, and background knowledge to inform image-text alignment.
SANDI employs an Integer Linear Program to compute semantically meaningful alignments between images and text.
Experiments demonstrate that SANDI achieves high-quality semantic fit between selected images and story text.
Two spoken word recognition models instantiate distinct hypotheses about orthographic influences on auditory word recognition.
The models reproduce human-like behavior in spoken word recognition.
The models generate testable hypotheses regarding the source of orthographic effects in spoken word recognition.
Two novel data augmentation techniques generate new training examples for hypernymy detection.
One augmentation technique combines hypernym transitivity and intersective modifier-noun composition to generate additional vector pairs that assume a hypernymy relationship.
Another augmentation technique uses generative adversarial networks to generate vector pairs that assume a hypernymy relationship.
Two complementary strategies extend an existing hypernymy dataset by leveraging linguistic resources such as WordNet.
The proposed data augmentation and dataset extension strategies substantially improve hypernymy classifier performance across multiple datasets and vector spaces.
Classifiers that leverage textual and listening patterns detect extraneous content in podcast descriptions and audio transcripts.
The classifiers improve ROUGE scores for podcast summarization.
The classifiers reduce extraneous content generated in podcast summaries.
An unsupervised graph-based ranking model for extractive summarization of long scientific documents is introduced.
The model uses a two-level hierarchical graph representation of the source document.
The model exploits asymmetrical positional cues to determine sentence importance.
The model outperforms strong unsupervised baselines in automatic metrics and human evaluation.
The model achieves performance comparable to many state-of-the-art supervised approaches trained on large datasets.
Patterns in discourse structure serve as a strong signal for determining importance in scientific articles.
A human-human negotiation dialogue dataset in a job interview scenario with multiple possible solutions and a utility function is released.
A dialogue act-based breakdown detection method that focuses on dialogue flow and is applicable to various corpora is introduced.
The dataset is evaluated on a breakdown detection task for human-human negotiation support.
The dialogue act-based method achieves comparable detection performance to text-based approaches on existing corpora and outperforms them on the new dataset.
A fine-tuned GPT-2 is used to automatically construct large-scale weakly-labeled data.
A semi-supervised framework jointly trains NLG and NLU models with parameter updates weighted by estimated label quality.
The quality-weighted weak supervision framework is effective in low-resource scenarios with very few training instances.
The framework outperforms benchmark systems on the E2E and Weather datasets when using the full training data.
Apple’s bi-directional LSTM architecture for language identification in short strings was reproduced.
The reproduced bi-LSTM model outperforms current open-source language identifiers.
The model’s language identification errors are primarily caused by confusion between related languages.
A tree-Wasserstein distance regularized label distribution learning algorithm is introduced for hierarchical text classification.
Neural networks are used to predict the entire label hierarchy, with similarity between predictions and true labels measured by the tree-Wasserstein distance.
The proposed method incorporates label hierarchy structure during training.
The proposed method requires less computation time and memory than the Sinkhorn algorithm.
A self-supervised method builds sentence embeddings by combining diverse explicit syntactic structures of a sentence.
Individual representation functions are learned jointly for different syntactic frameworks.
An original contrastive multi-view framework induces explicit interaction between models during training.
Combining dependency, constituency, and sequential structures yields superior performance on standard sentence embedding benchmarks.
A deep neural model of spoken word recognition retrieves word embeddings from spoken forms.
A set of controlled experiments using word-aligned read speech data in German evaluates the influence of speech variability on model performance.
The model is more sensitive to dialectical variation than to gender variation in speech signals.
Recognition performance of word cognates from related languages reflects the degree of linguistic relatedness between those languages.
A GAN model generates diverse texts conditioned on sampled latent codes without requiring pre-training.
The generator employs a Gumbel-Softmax distribution for word sampling.
The objective function incorporates a reconstruction loss to enforce conditioning on the latent code.
The discriminator iteratively inspects incomplete partial texts to distinguish real from fake using the standard GAN objective.
The model attains competitive performance on the COCO Image Captions dataset compared to baseline models that require pre-training.
A tutorial teaches the basics of reviewing natural language processing research.
The tutorial enables experienced researchers to revise their reviewing procedures.
Emoji-based source task parameters are transferred to sentiment target tasks via transfer learning.
Transfer learning is most beneficial when the target sentiment task is balanced and contains high emoji content.
Monolingually learned emoji source tasks capture culturally specific emoji usage, leading to performance gains over baseline.
The effects of emoji content, label distribution, and monolingual versus multilingual source training on transfer efficacy are analyzed.
A multilingual email zoning benchmark comprising 625 emails in Portuguese, Spanish, and French is introduced.
OKAPI is a multilingual email segmentation model that uses a language-agnostic sentence encoder.
OKAPI generalizes well to unseen languages.
OKAPI achieves performance competitive with existing English email zoning benchmarks.
OKAPI attains state-of-the-art performance on English domain adaptation tasks.
ALANNO is an open-source annotation system for NLP tasks that supports active learning.
ALANNO facilitates annotation management in a multi-annotator setup.
ALANNO supports a variety of active learning methods and underlying models that are easily configurable and extensible.
The impact of anaphoric "one" words on English‑Hindi neural machine translation performance is evaluated.
English‑Hindi neural machine translation exhibits significantly lower performance on sentences containing anaphoric "one" words compared to sentences with regular, non‑anaphoric words.
Noun‑class anaphoric "one" words are more challenging for English‑Hindi neural machine translation than determinative‑class anaphoric "one" words.
ALAMBIC is an open-source dockerized web-based platform for annotating text data through active learning for classification tasks.
ALAMBIC integrates the entire workflow from data import to customization of the active learning process and data annotation.
ALAMBIC provides progress indications of the trained model and enables downloading the model for downstream tasks.
ALAMBIC's architecture allows easy integration of additional model types, features, and active learning strategies.
CoTEVer annotates the factual correctness of generated explanations and collects revision data for incorrect explanations.
Data collected with CoTEVer can be used to improve the faithfulness of explanations.
A crowd-sourcing platform consisting of a mobile app and web API gamifies the collection of multiple-domain question-answering data for low-resource languages.
The platform was released for Icelandic, enabling the construction of a question-answering dataset comparable in size and answer coverage to large high-resource language datasets.
The platform is open source and includes instructions for localization and deployment to gather question-answering data for other low-resource languages.
FISH extracts and highlights crucial textual signals from financial statements.
FISH integrates pre-trained BERT representations and a fine-tuned BERT highlighting model in a two-stage classify-then-highlight pipeline.
Human evaluation shows that FISH provides accurate financial signals.
Revita is an AI-based language-learning platform that functions as an intelligent online tutor for multiple languages from lower-intermediate to advanced levels.
Revita incorporates a system of linguistic constructs to represent domain knowledge.
The system of linguistic constructs defines the types of exercises and the content of feedback.
The system of linguistic constructs enables detailed modeling and evaluation of learner progress.
The system of linguistic constructs was developed in collaboration with experts in language pedagogy.
Complex questions are decomposed into simpler subquestions to enable use of single-span extraction reading-comprehension models.
Final answers are derived from subquestion answers using a predefined reasoning template.
The approach is applied to subtraction-based arithmetic questions.
The approach is evaluated on a subset of the DROP dataset.
The approach achieves competitive performance with state-of-the-art methods.
The approach provides interpretability and requires minimal supervision.
Separable negative log-likelihood losses for structured prediction are not necessarily Bayes consistent.
Minimizing separable negative log-likelihood losses for structured prediction may not result in a model that predicts the most probable structure under the data distribution.
Yu Sheng is a web-based poetry generation system that incorporates a human-in-the-loop framework allowing users to customize the composition process.
Two methods are introduced and models are trained to enable constrained generation and fine-grained polishing of classical Chinese poetry.
Yu Sheng demonstrates stronger generation and polishing performance for poetry compared to vanilla models.
Web scraping is used to extract keywords for articles from the Repository Analytics and Metrics Portal (RAMP).
Extracted keywords are mapped to Library of Congress Subject Headings (LCSH) names to create a gold‑standard dataset.
Predictive models are trained to assign Biology‑related LCSH concepts to scholarly articles by formulating the task as multi‑label classification.
The proposed automatic annotation approach is shown to be viable for predicting LCSH for scholarly articles.
CSTOP, a dataset of 5800 Spanish-English code-switched utterances with semantic parses, is released.
Two data augmentation methods are proposed for zero-shot and few-shot code-switched semantic parsing: translate-and-align fine-tuning and generation-plus-match-and-filter augmentation.
Pre-trained cross-lingual language models demonstrate superior code-switched generalization when only English training data is available.
Combining the few-shot setting with the proposed augmentation methods substantially narrows the accuracy gap between zero-shot and full-data semantic parsing performance.
A content-based recommender system detects complementary products using Siamese Neural Networks.
Siamese Convolutional Neural Network and Siamese Long Short-Term Memory models are implemented and compared for complementary product detection.
An extension of the Siamese Neural Network approach enables handling millions of products in seconds and substantially reduces training time complexity.
Siamese LSTM predicts complementary products with high accuracy using only product titles.
A BERT-based full ranking model outperforms a BERT-based re-ranker and BM25 on the fully labeled Cranfield collection.
The BERT-based full ranking model retrieves documents that the initial ranker does not retrieve.
BERT-based rankers are empirically evaluated on a collection with complete relevance judgments (Cranfield).
An adaptation engine fine‑tunes a word‑embedding model to capture the semantic meaning of recipe ingredients in an unsupervised manner.
The adaptation engine aligns ingredient embeddings to external databases to enrich recipe data.
The adaptation engine modifies a recipe to satisfy specific user preferences, such as reducing caloric intake, while preserving cuisine style and essence.
The adaptation engine adjusts the remaining components of a recipe to maintain reproducibility after ingredient modifications.
FreCORE and SweCORE are register-annotated corpora for French and Swedish.
Deep pre-trained language models achieve strong performance on French and Swedish register classification and surpass previous state-of-the-art in English and Finnish.
Zero-shot cross-lingual transfer from the English CORE corpus matches or exceeds previously published monolingual models for register classification.
Lightweight monolingual classification with minimal training data reaches or exceeds zero-shot cross-lingual performance for register classification.
Analysis of classification results reveals that certain registers remain challenging for cross-lingual transfer.
Generative and discriminative approaches to unsupervised parsing are systematically overviewed.
Relative strengths and weaknesses of generative and discriminative unsupervised parsing approaches are analyzed.
Statistical and recent neural unsupervised parsing methods are covered to illustrate historical and recent development of the field.
Emerging research topics such as BERT-based approaches and visually grounded learning for unsupervised parsing are discussed.
small-text provides pool-based active learning for single- and multi-label text classification in Python.
small-text includes numerous pre-implemented state-of-the-art query strategies, including GPU-accelerated ones.
small-text offers standardized interfaces that allow combination of classifiers, query strategies, and stopping criteria.
small-text integrates scikit-learn, PyTorch, and Hugging Face transformers as optional extensions, enabling optional GPU usage.
Using small-text, the SetFit training paradigm matches transformer fine-tuning in classification accuracy while achieving higher area under the curve.
OLEA is an open-source, extensible Python library for diagnostic error analysis of offensive language detection in English.
OLEA provides easy-to-use tools for error analysis of offensive language detection models.
OLEA packages analyses and datasets from prior scholarship for evaluating offensive language classifiers.
OLEA enables researchers to build effective, explainable, and generalizable offensive language classifiers.
LaRoSeDa is a large Romanian sentiment dataset containing 15,000 positive and negative reviews from the largest Romanian e-commerce platform.
Self-organizing maps replace k-means for clustering word embeddings.
Clustering word embeddings with self-organizing maps produces clusters that more closely follow Zipf’s law distribution than clusters produced by k-means.
Using self-organizing maps for clustering word embeddings improves sentiment classification results on LaRoSeDa.
Self-organizing maps for clustering word embeddings generalize to another Romanian dataset for text categorization by topic.
A few-shot data-to-text generation approach augments training data by generating new text samples through value replacement, GPT-2 generation, and automatic pairing with data samples.
Cycle consistency is employed as an objective to ensure that a data sample can be reconstructed from its generated text and that text samples can be reconstructed from data.
The weakly supervised training paradigm outperforms fully supervised sequence-to-sequence models while using only a small fraction of the training set.
Utilizing all annotated data, the model boosts the performance of a standard sequence-to-sequence model and establishes a new state-of-the-art on the E2E and WebNLG benchmarks.
A hierarchical transformer architecture processes the full sequence of clinical notes for ICD coding.
The architecture incorporates embeddings for note metadata such as position, time, and type.
Superconvergence training reduces training costs for models that use all clinical notes.
The model achieves state-of-the-art performance on ICD coding when using only discharge summaries as input.
Using all clinical notes as input yields additional performance improvements for ICD coding.
ALLECS is a lightweight web application that serves grammatical error correction systems to the general public.
ALLECS is designed to be accessible to users with slow Internet connections and mobile devices.
ALLECS provides three state-of-the-art base grammatical error correction systems using sequence-to-sequence generation and sequence tagging approaches.
ALLECS provides two state-of-the-art grammatical error correction system combination methods using edit-based and text-based approaches.
Orthographic information on the target word is encoded in higher layers of BERT representations, which largely explains BERT’s low performance on lexical semantic change detection.
Reducing the influence of orthography on BERT vectors considerably improves BERT’s performance on lexical semantic change detection.
A systematic investigation of multiple variables affecting clustering of BERT vectors for lexical semantic change detection is conducted.
ScanTextGAN generates human-like scanpaths over text.
ScanTextGAN-generated scanpaths approximate meaningful cognitive signals present in human gaze patterns.
Augmenting NLP models with synthetically generated scanpaths improves performance across multiple downstream tasks.
TULAP provides an open-source, easily deployable platform for sharing Turkish NLP datasets and tools.
TULAP supports interactive demonstrations of tools.
Tools hosted on TULAP are containerized to ensure portability.
Numerous open-access Turkish NLP resources have been made available through TULAP.
The design, implementation, and deployment of TULAP are described with use cases.
A model uses deep metric learning to fine-tune contextualized word embeddings for supervised semantic frame induction.
Fine-tuned contextualized embeddings are applied to perform semantic frame induction.
Deep metric learning fine-tuning substantially improves clustering evaluation scores for semantic frame induction on FrameNet.
The approach remains effective when the number of training instances is small.
A novel approach learns domain-specific plausible materials for vehicle repair components by probing pretrained language models with cloze-style queries.
The approach aggregates salient predictions from multiple cloze query templates.
Domain adaptation using a small high-quality or customized Wikipedia corpus improves performance of the material prediction method.
A distilled pretrained language model outperforms a classic pattern-based algorithm for resource-lean material prediction.
Exploiting the compositionality assumption addresses data sparsity for multiword vehicle repair components.
Tough Mentions Recall (TMR) metrics evaluate NER recall on subsets of tough mentions, including unseen mentions, mentions with unseen token/type combinations, and type-confusable mentions.
TMR metrics are applied to English, Spanish, and Dutch corpora using several recent neural NER architectures.
TMR metrics reveal subtle performance differences between BERT and Flair on two English NER corpora.
TMR metrics reveal a weak spot in the performance of current NER models on Spanish.
TMR metrics enable differentiation between otherwise similar-scoring systems and identification of performance patterns that would go unnoticed from overall precision, recall, and F1.
A qualitative and quantitative analysis of constituency grammar in attention heads of BERT and RoBERTa is performed.
The syntactic distance method is used to extract implicit constituency grammar from the attention weights of each head.
Certain attention heads induce specific grammar types substantially better than baselines, indicating that those heads act as proxies for constituency grammar.
Fine-tuning on sentence meaning similarity tasks decreases the average constituency grammar inducing ability of upper layers.
Fine-tuning on natural language inference tasks increases the average constituency grammar inducing ability of upper layers.
kogito is an open-source toolkit that generates commonsense inferences about situations described in text.
kogito provides a standardized API for training and evaluating knowledge models and for generating and filtering inferences.
kogito includes helper functions that convert natural language texts into a format ingestible by knowledge models, performing knowledge head extraction and knowledge head‑relation matching.
kogito enables targeted, multi‑granularity commonsense knowledge generation through an intuitive and extensible interface to natural language generation models.
SocratiQ is a dataset of over 100K (question, context) pairs for Socratic Question Generation.
An in-depth analysis of the various types of Socratic questions is provided.
Prompt-tuned models are introduced for generating Socratic questions from a given context.
Automated and human evaluations demonstrate that the Socratic Question Generation models produce realistic, type-sensitive, human-like questions.
A fine-tuning strategy incorporates similarity between main model and biased model token attribution scores within a Product of Experts loss function for debiasing.
The strategy improves out-of-distribution performance while preserving in-distribution performance on natural language inference and fact verification benchmarks.

Knowledge distillation and pruning reduce out-of-distribution robustness of compressed models relative to their pre-trained language model counterparts while maintaining similar in-distribution performance.
Compressed models overfit on shortcut samples and generalize poorly on hard samples.
A regularization strategy based on sample uncertainty improves robustness of model compression.
CLICK injects contextual knowledge from Reddit data into conversational recommender systems via contrastive learning.
CLICK captures context-level user preferences from dialogue context even when preferred items are not mentioned.
CLICK employs a relevance-enhanced contrastive learning loss that reflects fine-grained multiple recommendable items.
CLICK includes a response generation module that produces persuasive rationales for recommendations.
Experiments demonstrate that CLICK outperforms state-of-the-art methods on a benchmark conversational recommender system dataset.
EvAlign provides a visual analytics framework for quantitative and qualitative evaluation of automatic translation alignment models.
EvAlign offers visualization views that enable comparison of model predictions with baseline and state-of-the-art models.
EvAlign includes search and filter functions to inspect frequent alignment errors and their positions.
EvAlign hosts nine gold standard datasets and predictions from multiple alignment models.
EvAlign is an extendable framework that allows straightforward addition of new datasets and models.
CTC alignments improve autoregressive translation.
A Hybrid CTC/Attention model originally designed for ASR is modified to support text-to-text translation and speech-to-text translation.
Joint CTC/attention models leverage CTC's core properties to counteract key weaknesses of pure-attention models during training and decoding.
Joint CTC/attention models outperform pure-attention baselines across multiple benchmark translation tasks.
A self-supervised inference method backpropagates loss from class-balanced pseudo-label predictions and adapts the Reptile meta-learning algorithm with an inductive bias toward pre-trained weights.
The method enables self-supervised fine-tuning during inference for any classifier model, improving adaptation to target domains.

The method improves performance of BERT, Electra, and ResNet-50 on question answering, benchmark, conversation response selection, and image classification tasks without modifying the underlying models.
The method outperforms previous approaches in self-supervised fine-tuning during inference.
The method is effective in online and transfer-learning settings.
SPINDLE parses written Dutch text into λ terms for meaning composition.
SPINDLE generates high-resolution derivations of a multimodal type‑logical grammar that captures deep function‑argument structures and dependency relations.
SPINDLE incorporates a static type‑checker that asserts the well‑formedness of grammatical analyses.
SPINDLE incorporates a structurally‑aware supertagger based on heterogeneous graph convolutions.
SPINDLE incorporates a massively parallel proof search component based on Sinkhorn iterations.
Large poetry corpora for English and German are provided.
Prosodic features are annotated in smaller corpora to enable training of corpus-driven neural models.
BiLSTM-CRF models with syllable embeddings outperform a CRF baseline and BERT-based approaches for prosodic tagging.
Joint prediction of foot boundaries and syllable stress improves foot boundary learning.
Caesuras are dependent on syntax and integral to shaping the overall measure of a poetic line.
A unified framework evaluates encoding architectures, similarity functions, and training regimes for few-shot intent classification.
Cross-encoder architecture combined with episodic meta-learning consistently yields the best few-shot intent classification performance.
Episodic training substantially outperforms conventional non-episodic training for few-shot intent classification.
Splitting episodes into support and query sets has a limited and inconsistent effect on few-shot intent classification performance.
RoPPT introduces a target-oriented parse tree structure for metaphor detection.
RoPPT focuses on semantically relevant information in metaphor detection.
RoPPT achieves state-of-the-art performance on several main metaphor datasets.
RoPPT demonstrates effectiveness in context denoising compared to popular denoising and pruning methods.
ConEntail is a framework for universal zero- and few-shot classification that uses supervised contrastive pretraining.
ConEntail formulates classification as a nested entailment meta-task, interpreted as "Does sentence a entail [sentence b entail label c]".
ConEntail leverages 57 annotated classification datasets for supervised contrastive pretraining.
ConEntail achieves consistent performance gains as the amount of pretraining data increases.
ConEntail outperforms discriminative and generative baselines in both zero-shot and few-shot classification settings.
COVID-VTS is a benchmark for fact-checking multi-modal information in short-duration COVID-19 videos from real and machine-generated sources.
TwtrDetective incorporates cross-media consistency checking to detect token-level malicious tampering across modalities and generates explanations.
An efficient and scalable approach automatically generates misleading video posts via event manipulation or adversarial matching.
TwtrDetective outperforms several state-of-the-art models in fact extraction and verification on short video platforms.
ITMT provides a graphical user interface for user-in-the-loop training and curation of state-of-the-art topic extraction libraries, including recent neural-based methods, targeting domain experts.
ITMT enables domain experts to train and curate topic models without requiring deep knowledge of parameter optimization.
A comparative analysis demonstrates how ITMT’s functionalities and implementation aspects differ from other topic modeling analysis tools.
MMBias is a visual and textual bias benchmark that includes thousands of images and phrases covering 14 population subgroups.
Evaluation with MMBias shows that CLIP, ALBEF, and ViLT exhibit meaningful bias favoring certain groups.
A post-processing debiasing method for large pretrained vision–language models mitigates bias while preserving model accuracy.
XLGen casts extreme multi-label classification as a text-to-text generation task using pre-trained models.
Cluster-guided label generation hierarchically generates lower-level labels based on label cluster information.
Frequency-based label ordering and decoding ensemble methods are identified as critical factors for improving XLGen.
XLGen with cluster guidance outperforms classification and generation baselines on tail labels and improves overall performance across four XMC benchmarks.
Human evaluation shows that XLGen can generate unseen but plausible labels.
Instruction bias is present in 14 recent NLU benchmarks, with concrete patterns in annotation instructions propagated to the collected data.
Instruction bias leads to overestimation of model performance on NLU benchmarks.
Models struggle to generalize beyond biases originating from crowdsourcing instructions.
Pattern frequency and model size influence the magnitude of instruction bias.
Concrete recommendations for creating future NLU benchmarks are derived to mitigate instruction bias.
IterX performs iterative document-level extraction of complex relations represented as N-tuple templates mapping named slots to text spans.
IterX uses imitation learning to cast template extraction as a Markov decision process, removing the need for predefined template orders.
IterX achieves state-of-the-art performance on the SciREX 4-ary relation extraction benchmark.
IterX achieves state-of-the-art performance on the MUC-4 template extraction benchmark.
IterX establishes a strong baseline on the BETTER Granular task.
NLP Workbench provides a web-based platform that enables non-expert users to obtain semantic understanding of large-scale corpora using state-of-the-art text mining models.
NLP Workbench integrates entity linking, sentiment analysis, semantic parsing, and relation extraction functionalities from latest pre-trained models and open source systems.
NLP Workbench features an extensible design that allows researchers and developers to replace existing models or integrate new ones.
NLP Workbench employs a microservice architecture that facilitates allocation of acceleration hardware and parallelization of computation to improve efficiency.
Bayesian matrix factorisation is used to predict performance of multilingual NLP tasks.
The Bayesian matrix factorisation approach outperforms state-of-the-art performance prediction methods on several NLP benchmarks, including machine translation and cross-lingual entity linking.
The approach eliminates the need for hyperparameter tuning.
The approach provides uncertainty estimates for its predictions.
SSMT pipeline translates videos between English and Indian languages by cascading speech-to-text, preprocessing, machine translation, subtitle generation, text-to-speech, and video rendering modules.
SSMT pipeline allows human users to edit intermediate module outputs and incorporates the edited results in subsequent processing to improve translation quality.
SSMT pipeline is the first fully integrated system for English-to-Indian-language video translation.
SSMT pipeline achieves a high mean opinion score for English-to-Hindi video translation when human intervention is used.

An architecture performs domain adaptation for semantic parsing using only a small amount of metadata about a new domain, without any new training data (zero-shot) or with very few examples (few-shot).
A concept encoder encodes intent and slot tags from a new domain within a sequence-to-sequence semantic parsing model.
A decoder-focused pretraining approach using Wikidata makes sequence-to-sequence models concept aware for low-resource semantic parsing.
The model achieves superior performance compared to prior approaches in few-shot settings on the TOPv2 and SNIPS datasets.
Few-shot and zero-shot evaluations of compositional semantic parsing are conducted on the TOPv2 dataset.
Learn is a unified tool that applies question generation and selection in classroom settings.
Learn can generate questions from existing course materials such as reference textbooks.
Learn can select the highest-quality generated questions.
Learn can adapt question difficulty to individual student knowledge.
Learn can generate new questions based on the effectiveness of previously used questions for student learning.
Learn’s modular, composable architecture allows instructors to use only the sub-tools required for a given course, supporting integration across diverse teaching styles.
Learn was adopted in classes at the University of Pennsylvania with a large enrollment of students.
TwiRGCN modulates messages passed through KG edges during convolution based on relevance of the edge's temporal period to the question.
TwiRGCN includes a gating device that predicts whether the answer to a complex temporal question is a KG entity or a time and uses this prediction to guide the scoring mechanism.
TwiRGCN outperforms state-of-the-art models on the TimeQuestions dataset across diverse question types.
TwiRGCN improves accuracy for the most difficult ordinal and implicit temporal question types.
DREEAM guides attention modules of document-level relation extraction systems to assign high weights to evidence using evidence information as a supervisory signal.
DREEAM employs a memory-efficient design for evidence retrieval in document-level relation extraction.
DREEAM uses a self-training strategy to learn evidence retrieval from automatically-generated evidence without requiring evidence annotations.
DREEAM achieves state-of-the-art performance on the DocRED benchmark for both document-level relation extraction and evidence retrieval.
DREEAM is the first approach to employ self-training for evidence retrieval in document-level relation extraction.
Adaptive Appraisal (A²) introduces task adapters and sentiment adapters built on language models for appraisal tagging.
Appraisal tagging is formulated as a sequence tagging problem.
Adaptive Appraisal (A²) achieves higher performance than baseline adapter-based models and other neural classification models in cross-domain and cross-language settings.
A generative neuro-symbolic approach discovers inference chains that connect argument pairs using the Commonsense Transformer (COMET).
The approach is evaluated on three datasets for both two-label (attack/support) and three-label (attack/support/neutral) relational argument mining tasks.
The approach achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods on all three datasets.
A thin-deep encoder can construct robust low-dimensional sentence embeddings for 109 languages.
Distillation methods that incorporate knowledge from a teacher model further improve lightweight language-agnostic sentence embeddings.
Lightweight language-agnostic sentence embedding models achieve effective performance on Tatoeba, United Nations, and BUCC benchmarks.
Lightweight language-agnostic sentence embedding models are released on TensorFlow Hub.
BERT-based measures moderately align with human intuitions about compound semantics, especially when using contextualized representations.
Lexeme meaning dominance is more predictable from BERT representations than semantic transparency.
Higher, more contextualized layers of BERT best represent compound meaning, contrary to results reported for standard words.
Shapley Values are applied to attention heads of multilingual transformers to identify heads that cause cross-lingual interference.
Pruning attention heads identified as language-specific reduces interference and improves target-language performance on sentence classification.
Pruning attention heads identified as language-specific reduces interference and improves target-language performance on structural prediction.
Attention visualizations reveal distinctions between language-agnostic and language-specific attention heads.
Four popular active learning schemes do not outperform random selection on multi-source unlabeled pools for natural language inference.
Uncertainty-based active learning strategies acquire collective outliers that hinder learning and generalisation.
Removing collective outliers enables active learning strategies to recover and surpass random baselines.
Collective outliers differ in form across data sources, and hard-to-learn data is not universally detrimental.
Difficulty-stratified testing based on dataset cartography reveals that active learning strategies are differentially impacted by example learnability and difficulty.
A three-hot embedding and decoding scheme models Korean syllables using jamo-level representations.
The three-hot scheme reduces embedding parameter count to less than one percent of that required by a syllable-level model.
The three-hot scheme avoids tripling the sequence length required by jamo-level models.
The three-hot scheme addresses a theoretical flaw in a prior three-hot modeling approach.
Reducing embedding parameters by several orders of magnitude does not degrade translation quality compared to a baseline syllable model.
Stochastic Bucket-wise Feature Sampling (SBFS) enables processing of long videos with a constant memory footprint.
Locformer combines SBFS with a consistency loss to process videos up to 18 minutes long.
SBFS improves the performance of prior temporal moment localization methods.
Locformer attains state-of-the-art results on the Charades-STA and YouCookII benchmarks.
SBFS provides substantial speed-up at test time and markedly reduces memory usage.
A similarity-based model classifies utterances as empathetic without using dialogue context.
The context-agnostic model attains performance comparable to existing methods on standard empathy benchmarks.
The context-agnostic model surpasses state-of-the-art models on empathetic rationale extraction.
A set of dialogue-context-altering examples reveals that current empathy identification systems label utterances as empathetic irrespective of context.
Current empathy identification systems rely primarily on the surface form of responses rather than contextual suitability.
GLADIS provides an acronym dictionary containing millions of acronyms and long forms.
GLADIS includes a pre-training corpus comprising a large collection of sentences.
GLADIS comprises three datasets that cover general, scientific, and biomedical domains.
AcroBERT is a language model pre-trained on the GLADIS corpus for general acronym disambiguation.
Evaluation of AcroBERT on GLADIS reveals challenges for acronym disambiguation and highlights the benchmark's utility.
A taxonomy of possible reasons leading to annotators’ disagreement in subjective tasks is proposed.
A portion of a Twitter offensive language detection dataset is manually labeled according to the proposed taxonomy, revealing the distribution of disagreement categories.
Experiments assess the impact of different types of annotator disagreement on offensive language classification performance.
Classification accuracy of tweets is evaluated separately for each disagreement category to determine how well they can be classified as offensive or not.
Incorporating data with specific disagreement types into the training set is shown to affect classification performance.
Offensive language detection is implemented as a multi-task framework that uses disagreement classification as an auxiliary task.
A selective in-context data augmentation method uses pointwise V-information to filter synthetic utterances for intent detection.
The method fine-tunes a pre-trained language model on a small seed of training data before synthesizing new utterances for given intents.
The method achieves state-of-the-art performance on three intent detection datasets in few-shot settings.
The method matches state-of-the-art performance on intent detection datasets in full-shot settings.
A systematic analysis of seven standard hate‑speech detection benchmarks is performed using a fine‑grained, linguistically‑grounded definition of implicit and subtle messages.
State‑of‑the‑art neural network architectures are evaluated on implicit hate‑speech and subtle hate‑speech classification tasks.
State‑of‑the‑art models achieve satisfactory performance on explicit hate‑speech detection.
State‑of‑the‑art models fail to detect implicit and subtle hate‑speech, indicating that detection of these forms remains unsolved.
PiC is a dataset of approximately 28,000 noun phrases with contextual Wikipedia pages and three tasks for training and evaluating phrase embeddings.
Training on PiC improves the accuracy of ranking models.
Span selection models trained on PiC achieve near-human accuracy on semantic search given a query phrase and a passage.
Span selection models trained on PiC learn to capture the common meaning of a phrase regardless of its actual context.
State-of-the-art models perform poorly at distinguishing two senses of the same phrase in different contexts.
State-of-the-art models perform poorly at estimating similarity between two different phrases in the same context.
A question generation method combines rule-based and neural sequence-to-sequence models.
Semantic role labeling is used to convert training examples into semantic representations for sequence-to-sequence training.
The method automatically generates multiple questions from an input sentence covering different views of the sentence.
The method achieves a significant improvement over state-of-the-art neural question generation approaches.
A systematic study evaluates the robustness of automatically learned discrete prompts by applying designed perturbations to AutoPrompt and measuring performance on two NLI datasets.
Automatically learned discrete prompts remain relatively robust to perturbations of NLI inputs.
Automatically learned discrete prompts are highly sensitive to shuffling and deletion of prompt tokens.
Automatically learned discrete prompts generalize poorly across different NLI datasets.
CLiMP is a benchmark comprising 1000 minimal pairs for each of 16 syntactic contrasts covering nine major Chinese linguistic phenomena.
CLiMP labels achieve high human agreement.
Evaluation of eleven Chinese language models on CLiMP shows that classifier–noun agreement and verb complement selection are the syntactic phenomena with the strongest model performance.
Models perform poorly on the ba construction, binding, and filler‑gap dependencies.
Chinese BERT outperforms LSTM and 5‑gram models on CLiMP.
LingMess categorizes mention-pair coreference decisions into six linguistically motivated types.
LingMess learns a dedicated trainable scoring function for each mention-pair type.
LingMess significantly improves the accuracy of the pairwise coreference scorer.
LingMess significantly improves overall coreference performance on the English OntoNotes corpus and five additional datasets.
A dataset of English tweets by US Congress members annotated for interpersonal emotion is released, providing supervision for interpersonal group relationship (IGR) labels.
Interpersonal group relationship (IGR) can be predicted by modeling the relationship between speaker and target using fine-grained interpersonal emotions as an anchor.
Neural models achieve substantially higher performance than chance at identifying IGR from utterances.
A shared encoding between IGR and interpersonal perceived emotion yields performance gains on both tasks.
Analyses show that subtle emotional signals in utterances are indicative of different biases.
MEDA adds raw toxicity scores as meta-data to pretraining samples.
INST adds instructions indicating toxicity to pretraining samples.
INST reduces toxicity generation in language models while preserving accuracy on benchmark NLP tasks.
INST improves bias detection performance as measured by AUC.
The augmentation strategies maintain performance when scaling the number of training samples and model parameter count.
Instruction Clarification Requests are annotated in the CoDraw multimodal collaborative dialogue dataset.
CoDraw-iCR (v1) provides a large spontaneous corpus of Instruction Clarification Requests.
Two tasks are formalized: determining when to make an Instruction Clarification Request and recognizing Instruction Clarification Requests in dialogue.
Baseline models are provided for the tasks of predicting when to make an Instruction Clarification Request and recognizing Instruction Clarification Requests.
Instruction Clarification Requests in CoDraw are shown to be lexically and semantically diverse and are produced self‑motivatedly by players to solve the task.
FastKASSIM measures utterance- and document-level syntactic similarity by pairing and averaging the most similar constituency parse trees using tree kernels.
FastKASSIM is more robust to syntactic dissimilarities than the previous standard document-level syntactic similarity metric.
FastKASSIM runs significantly faster than its predecessor on large document collections.
Syntactically similar arguments on r/ChangeMyView tend to be more persuasive.
Syntax predicts authorship attribution in the Australian High Court Judgment corpus.
ISLR models predict both sign labels and phonological attributes such as handshape from pose estimations of a signer.
Predicting phonological attributes alongside sign labels improves sign recognition accuracy on the WLASL benchmark.
The accuracy improvement from phonological auxiliary predictions is observed across multiple underlying model architectures.
New metrics evaluate belief-updating methods with emphasis on logical consistency of beliefs.
The SLAG training objective improves performance of hypernetwork-based belief-updating methods.
The belief graph visualizes relationships between stored model beliefs in language models.
Update methods can correct incorrect factual beliefs in language models and substantially increase consistency among beliefs.
Learned optimizers can surpass off-the-shelf optimizers for belief updating in challenging settings.
A reinforced sequence training approach performs subjective bias correction.
The approach balances bias neutralization with fluency and semantics preservation via reinforcement learning.
The approach is cross-trained over multiple sources of bias to improve robustness to unseen biased writing styles.
The approach fine-tunes a large pre-trained transformer model to achieve state-of-the-art performance on bias text correction.
Experiments demonstrate that the approach yields significant improvements over alternative methods in subjective bias correction.
Pento-DIARef is a diagnostic dataset of puzzle-piece images and referring expressions generated by the Incremental Algorithm.
The dataset is designed to evaluate whether neural models can learn the underlying regularity of the Incremental Algorithm from extensional examples.
A model that incorporates a vision detection component and a targeted data generation scheme learns to produce referring expressions with near‑perfect BLEU@1 and sentence accuracy on Pento-DIARef.
Simpler baseline models fail to achieve near‑perfect performance on Pento-DIARef.
ComSearch compresses the equation search space by excluding mathematically equivalent equations.
The compressed search space enables enumeration of all possible equations and generation of high-quality data.
A ranking model denoises pseudo labels that contain incorrect mathematical logic (false-matching problem).
A flexible framework integrates two existing supervised math word problem solvers to generate pseudo labels, achieving state-of-the-art performance in weakly supervised math word problem solving.
Label regularization techniques, including knowledge distillation, do not improve fine-tuning performance of pre-trained language models compared to regular fine-tuning.
Pre-training of language models acts as a regularizer, eliminating the need for additional label regularization during downstream fine-tuning.
The ineffectiveness of label regularization techniques is observed across both natural language processing and computer vision tasks.
A large-scale experimental study comprising more than 600 trials on BERT, RoBERTa, and GPT evaluates the impact of label regularization on fine-tuning.
A rigorous analysis of various generative language models on two dialog tutoring datasets for language learning is performed using automatic and human evaluations.
Current generative language models can model tutoring in constrained learning scenarios with few concepts and teacher strategies, but perform poorly in less constrained scenarios.
Human quality evaluation indicates low performance of both models and ground-truth annotations on equitable tutoring, measured by learning opportunities and dialog engagement.
An expert annotator user study finds a large number of model reasoning errors in a substantial proportion of conversations.
Event Linking is defined as a task that links an event mention in an article to the most appropriate Wikipedia page.
A dataset for Event Linking is created, comprising an automatically gathered training set from Wikipedia and two evaluation sets: an in-domain Wikipedia set and an out-of-domain real-world news set.
Two state-of-the-art entity linking models are retrained and evaluated on the Event Linking task, revealing challenges specific to event linking.
EVELINK, an event-specific linking system, is proposed and achieves competitive performance on the Event Linking task.
A detailed analysis of Event Linking is performed to elucidate task characteristics and the limitations of current models.
Path Spuriousness (PS) metric quantifies the degree to which a reasoning path is spurious.
A reinforcement learning model uses a reward that balances answer accuracy and path reasonableness.
The model improves an agent's ability to avoid spurious paths while maintaining performance comparable to state-of-the-art.
A contrastive learning fine-tuning method using a small set of word translation pairs exposes cross-lingual lexical knowledge in multilingual sentence encoders.
Multilingual sentence encoders can be rewired into effective cross-lingual lexical encoders via contrastive learning fine-tuning.
Probing shows that multilingual sentence encoders store cross-lingual lexical knowledge in their parameters.
The contrastive fine-tuning method improves performance on bilingual lexical induction, cross-lingual lexical semantic similarity, and cross-lingual entity linking benchmarks.
The method provides a tool for harnessing covert multilingual lexical knowledge hidden in multilingual sentence encoders.
Role identification of entities in harmful memes is defined as detecting hero, villain, and victim roles for each referenced entity.
VECTOR is a multi-modal framework that incorporates entity-based contextual information into visual-semantic representations for meme role identification.
VECTOR outperforms the best baseline and the top competing shared-task submission on the HVVMemes dataset for entity role identification.
Comparative analyses across unimodal and multimodal models are conducted to evaluate performance on meme role identification.
Challenges of semantic role labeling within memes are highlighted based on experimental findings.
A question-guided generation framework models events in complex scenarios as answers to participant-focused questions.
The framework generates each next event by answering one of three question types: what else a participant did, what else happened to a participant, or what else happened.
Participants and questions can be sampled or supplied by a user, enabling controllable exploration of event scenarios.
Empirical evaluation shows that the question-guided generation provides better coverage of participants than standard event language modeling.
Empirical evaluation shows that the question-guided generation yields more diverse events within a domain while maintaining comparable perplexity for modeling event sequences.
Empirical evaluation shows that the question-guided generation offers more effective control for interactive schema generation.
A BERT-based probing method predicts typicality scores more accurately than prior static embedding and contextual language model approaches.
Integrating WordNet Information Content similarity with disambiguation achieves performance comparable to the best BERT-based method for typicality prediction.
Combining the WordNet similarity approach with BERT predictions yields enhanced typicality predictions.
Disambiguation of polysemous words during contextualization improves typicality prediction performance.
A methodological framework for probing and characterizing recall of memorized sequences in transformer language models is introduced.
Criteria for detecting model inputs that trigger memory recall are defined, and idioms are identified as inputs that typically satisfy these criteria.
An English idiom dataset is constructed for comparing transformer model behavior on memorized versus non-memorized inputs.

Analysis of hidden representations reveals that memorized predictions follow a two-step process: early layers promote the predicted token to the top of the output distribution, and upper layers increase model confidence.
The methodology is applied to memorized factual statements, demonstrating its utility beyond idioms.
A dataset of 1,522 unique relations from Wikidata, DBPedia, and YAGO is collected for verbalizing relations.
Pretrained language models for data-to-text generation often produce semantically inaccurate outputs when relation labels are ambiguous or incomplete.
Models trained with a large variety of relation labels are robust in verbalizing novel, unseen relations.
Diverse clear and meaningful relation labels improve generalization of data-to-text generation systems to novel domains.
A prompting technique elicits GPT-3 to produce acceptability judgments for the Article + Adjective + Numeral + Noun construction.
The prompting technique is validated against the CoLA corpus of acceptability judgments.
GPT-3 acceptability judgments for the AANN construction are compared with crowdsourced human judgments on a subset of sentences.
GPT-3 judgments are broadly similar to human judgments and generally align with literature constraints, while some cases show divergence between GPT-3, humans, and the literature.
Rationale models are introduced to explicitly learn to ignore adversarial attack tokens.
Rationale models can ignore the majority of attack tokens.
The approach improves robustness over baseline models on multiple datasets for BERT and RoBERTa.
The approach outperforms data augmentation with adversarial examples alone.
The method can close the performance gap between clean and attacked test sets, reducing the effect of adversarial attacks.
The StatCan Dialogue Dataset provides 19,379 English and French conversation turns between Statistics Canada agents and online users seeking over 5,000 complex data tables.
Two tasks are defined on the dataset: automatic retrieval of relevant tables from an ongoing conversation and automatic generation of appropriate agent responses at each turn.
Strong baseline models are established for both the table retrieval and response generation tasks.
Temporal split experiments reveal that all models suffer a significant performance drop when generalizing from validation to future test conversations.
Response generation models struggle to decide when to return a table.
A benchmark evaluates the prediction of out-of-domain language model performance using a few target-domain examples.
Feature attribution factors are used to rank relative model out-of-domain performance.
Factors that reveal model agreement with pathological heuristics are introduced to indicate poorer generalization capabilities.
Attribution-based factors improve ranking of model out-of-domain performance on textual entailment, paraphrase recognition, and a synthetic classification task.
Few-shot accuracy on target-domain examples provides a strong baseline for predicting out-of-domain performance without prior knowledge of the domain shift.
CoLaCTC merges vocabulary labels for CTC regularization using simple heuristic rules such as truncation, division, or modulo operations.
Modulo-based coarse labeling in CoLaCTC compresses the CTC label space to 256, providing training speedup while maintaining comparable or better translation quality than standard CTC regularization.
CoLaCTC generalizes to CTC regularization regardless of whether source transcripts or target translations are used as labels.
Synthetic clinical case corpus can be generated using auto-regressive French language models that are partially or fully pretrained on generic texts and fine‑tuned on clinical cases.

Generation process variants include fine‑tuning on annotated clinical text versus plain clinical text with post‑hoc annotation, and selecting generated texts based on model parameters and filtering criteria.
Clinical named entity recognition models trained on the synthetic clinical corpus can be evaluated on a gold‑standard clinical corpus.
Evaluation results demonstrate that synthetic text is useful for training clinical NER systems.
The first systematic evaluation of QA-based factuality metrics for error localization is conducted.
QA-based factuality metrics fail to correctly identify error spans in generated summaries.
Exact match baselines outperform QA-based factuality metrics for error span localization.
Errors in generated summaries are inherited by the question generation module, causing error propagation in downstream localization.
Human-in-the-loop question generation does not substantially improve error localization performance of QA-based frameworks.
Performance strongly improves over earlier unsupervised pretrained BERT implementations for predicting noun compound compositionality.
Data preprocessing, embedding computation, and compositionality estimation choices are identified as beneficial for BERT-based compositionality prediction.
BERT-derived representations capture distinct linguistic roles of heads and modifiers in noun compounds.
Frequency, productivity, and ambiguity of noun compounds affect BERT-based compositionality prediction performance.
Initial layers of BERT contain the most relevant information for predicting noun compound compositionality.
MTS-Dialog is a dataset of 1,700 doctor-patient dialogues paired with clinical notes.
Automatic summarization of doctor-patient conversations is shown to be feasible using the MTS-Dialog dataset.
Existing language models, data-augmentation methods, and guided summarization techniques are evaluated for generating clinical notes from doctor-patient dialogues.
Evaluation metrics based on n-gram matching, contextual embeddings, and fact extraction are compared for measuring accuracy and factual consistency of generated clinical notes.

Expert-based evaluation using natural-language-generation criteria and task-specific criteria such as critical omissions is conducted, and the correlation between automatic metrics and expert judgments is analyzed.
A perturbation method for unsupervised semantic analysis is proposed to investigate individual word contributions to sentence semantics.
A new evaluation metric is developed to adapt sentence compression datasets for automatic evaluation of sentence embedding models' ability to capture main semantics.

Unsupervised discourse relation recognition is shown to serve as a general inference task that more effectively aggregates information to essential contents than several state-of-the-art unsupervised sentence embedding models.
The Global-Local Centrality (GLC) model selects salient context from all sub-topics in dialogue summarization.
GLC constructs global-level centrality to identify vital sub-topics in a dialogue.
GLC constructs local-level centrality to select the most important context within each sub-topic.
GLC collects sub-topics based on utterance representations and aligns each utterance with a sub-topic.
GLC outperforms strong baselines on three public dialogue summarization datasets.
Analysis shows that GLC can precisely identify vital contents from sub-topics.
A dataset of conversational question answering over knowledge graphs, containing natural language questions annotated with SPARQL parses and execution-result answers, is released.
Two semantic parsing approaches for converting conversational natural language questions into SPARQL queries over knowledge graphs are introduced.

Large vocabularies, conversation context modeling, multi-entity query prediction, and test-time generalization are identified as key challenges for conversational semantic parsing over knowledge graphs.
The released dataset provides a testbed for evaluating and developing conversational semantic parsers.
DAM encapsulates bias mitigation functionalities into separate adapter modules that can be integrated into a language model on-demand without modifying the core model parameters.
DAM provides a stand-alone debiasing functionality that can be switched on and off, preserving the original model.
DAM improves or maintains bias mitigation effectiveness on classification tasks involving gender, race, and age protected attributes.
DAM avoids catastrophic forgetting when mitigating multiple protected attributes simultaneously.
DAM maintains task performance comparable to the original model while providing debiasing.
StyLEx learns from human-annotated explanations of stylistic features.
StyLEx jointly learns to perform style classification and predict stylistic features as model explanations.
StyLEx provides human-like stylistic lexical explanations without sacrificing sentence-level style prediction performance on in-domain and out-of-domain datasets.
Explanations generated by StyLEx achieve higher sufficiency and plausibility than baseline explanations.
