PubMed provides higher verification performance for specialized biomedical claims than other knowledge sources.
Wikipedia provides higher verification performance for everyday health concerns than other knowledge sources.
BM25 achieves higher retrieval precision than semantic search in open-domain scientific claim verification.
Semantic search achieves higher recall of relevant evidence than BM25 in open-domain scientific claim verification.
SPUQ quantifies both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties in large language models by generating input perturbations, sampling outputs for each perturbation, and aggregating the results.
An aggregation module in SPUQ generalizes the sampling‑based uncertainty approach for text generation tasks.
Experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate that SPUQ substantially improves uncertainty calibration of large language models.
Various perturbation and aggregation techniques are evaluated to assess their impact on uncertainty quantification performance.
Token-Level Prompt Decomposition (ToPro) decomposes an input sentence into single tokens and applies a single prompt template to each token for token-level sequence labeling.
ToPro-based fine-tuning outperforms vanilla fine-tuning and prompt-tuning in zero-shot cross-lingual transfer for multilingual named entity recognition and part-of-speech tagging.
ToPro achieves state-of-the-art performance on multilingual sequence labeling when used with the mT5 model.
ToPro performs much better than the current in-context learning method for multilingual large language models.
ToPro can serve as a novel and simple benchmarking method for sequence labeling tasks.
PAIRS provides parallel AI-generated images of people that share identical backgrounds and visual content while varying in gender (man, woman) and race (Black, white).
PAIRS enables systematic examination of gender and racial bias in large vision–language models by querying the models with controlled image variations.
Large vision–language models generate significantly different responses depending on the perceived gender or race of the person depicted in the input image.
The paper presents the largest and most comprehensive study of sociodemographic prompting to date.
Socio-demographic prompting influences model sensitivity, performance, and robustness across seven datasets and six instruction-tuned model families.
Incorporating sociodemographic information can improve zero-shot learning for subjective NLP tasks.
The effects of sociodemographic prompting vary substantially with model type, model size, dataset, and prompt formulation.
Socio-demographic prompting should be used cautiously when employed for data annotation or studying LLM alignment.
MLCopilot is a framework that leverages large language models to develop machine learning solutions for novel tasks.
MLCopilot enables large language models to comprehend structured inputs for solving novel machine learning tasks.
MLCopilot enables large language models to perform thorough reasoning to solve novel machine learning tasks.
With dedicated design, large language models can observe existing machine learning task experiences and reason effectively to deliver promising results for new tasks.
Solutions generated by MLCopilot can be used directly to achieve competitive performance on new machine learning tasks.
CFKGR links knowledge graph completion and counterfactual reasoning.
Benchmark datasets for CFKGR comprising diverse hypothetical scenarios with plausible changes and retained facts are created.
COULDD adapts existing knowledge graph embeddings given a hypothetical premise.
Knowledge graph embeddings learn patterns in the graph without explicit training.
Knowledge graph embeddings adapted with COULDD detect plausible counterfactual changes that follow learned patterns.
Knowledge graph embeddings adapted with COULDD are mostly unable to recognize changes that do not follow learned inference rules.
ChatGPT outperforms knowledge graph embeddings in detecting plausible changes to the graph but exhibits poor knowledge retention.
LLM Stereotype Index (LSI) is an extensible benchmark grounded on the Social Progress Index for holistic bias evaluation of large language models.
LSI evaluates bias across tasks of varying complexity to assess the breadth and depth of bias protection in large language models.
ChatGPT exhibits strong inherent prejudice with respect to nationality, gender, race, and religion according to LSI evaluations.
GPT-4 exhibits strong inherent prejudice with respect to nationality, gender, race, and religion according to LSI evaluations.
Bias in large language models becomes increasingly apparent as task complexity increases.
GPT-4 more effectively hides biases, but when biases are displayed they are more significant than in other models.
SIB-200 is a large-scale open-sourced benchmark dataset for topic classification in 205 languages and dialects.
SIB-200 provides the first publicly available evaluation dataset for natural language understanding in many of the covered languages.
Sentence-level topic annotations were created for English and extended to the remaining 204 languages using the Flores-200 corpus.

Evaluation of multilingual language models on SIB-200 in full-supervised, cross-lingual transfer, and prompting settings reveals a large performance gap between high-resource and low-resource languages.

Languages unseen during pretraining, languages from under-represented families such as Nilotic and Atlantic-Congo, and languages from Africa, the Americas, Oceania, and Southeast Asia exhibit the lowest performance on the topic classification dataset.
TESS is a fully non-autoregressive text diffusion model.
TESS employs a new form of self-conditioning.
TESS applies the diffusion process on the logit simplex space rather than the learned embedding space.
TESS outperforms state-of-the-art non-autoregressive models on natural language understanding and generation tasks.
TESS requires fewer diffusion steps with minimal performance drop.
TESS is competitive with pretrained autoregressive sequence-to-sequence models.
Metrics for assessing the quality of discovered latent concepts are proposed.
K-Means-based concept discovery enhances efficiency while maintaining the quality of latent concepts.
Latent concept discovery is scaled to large language models and phrasal concepts using the improved efficiency of K-Means-based clustering.
Clustering algorithms for latent concept discovery are compared using the proposed quality metrics.
Text-Guided Image Clustering generates textual descriptions of images using captioning and visual question-answering models and clusters the generated text to form image groups.
Prompting visual question-answering models injects task- or domain-specific knowledge into the clustering process.
A counting-based cluster explainability method provides keyword-based explanations for image clusters.
Text representations derived from generated captions and visual question-answering answers often outperform image features for clustering across diverse datasets.
Keyword-based explanations derived from the counting-based method describe clusters more accurately than suggested by cluster accuracy alone.
Fréchet Distance is proposed as a metric for evaluating information retrieval systems with sparse relevance labels.
The metric measures the distance between the distribution of judged relevant items and the distribution of retrieved results.
Empirical evaluation on MS MARCO V1 and TREC Deep Learning query sets demonstrates the metric's effectiveness for offline IR evaluation.
The approach extends evaluation methodology to generative information retrieval systems in real-world scenarios.
Multilingual sentiment lexicons are used in pretraining to reduce reliance on low-resource language texts.

Pretraining with multilingual sentiment lexicons, without sentence-level sentiment data, yields superior zero-shot sentiment analysis performance across 34 languages compared to models fine-tuned on English sentiment data and large language models such as GPT‑3.5, BLOOMZ, and XGLM.
The lexicon-based pretraining approach generalizes to unseen low-resource languages and code-mixed scenarios involving high-resource languages.
An algorithm finds the shortest string for a weighted nondeterministic automaton over non-idempotent semirings that admit an order satisfying a monotonicity condition.
The algorithm uses the backwards shortest distance of an equivalent deterministic automaton as a heuristic for A* search performed over a companion idempotent semiring.
The algorithm is proven to return the shortest string.
When determinization is performed on the fly, the algorithm visits only a small fraction of the potentially exponential number of deterministic automaton states.
Contrastive Decoding reduces hallucinations in large multilingual machine translation models.
Contrastive Decoding is applied to neural machine translation by maximizing the log-likelihood difference between the full model and a version with reduced encoder contribution.
An alternative implementation of Contrastive Decoding dynamically weights the log-likelihood difference based on the maximum token probability to lessen its effect when the model is confident.
The reduction of hallucinations is evidenced by increased mean COMET scores on sentences that originally generated hallucinations.
Evaluation on Small (418M) and Medium (1.2B) M2M models across 21 low- and medium-resource language pairs demonstrates the method's effectiveness.
A gradient-based inference-time controller can steer a pretrained multilingual translation model to control attributes such as formality or grammatical gender.
The inference-time controller enables zero-shot attribute control for languages without attribute-annotated data.
In zero-shot conditions, the performance gap between inference-time control and finetuning-based control narrows, especially for new and distant target languages.
Inference-time control exhibits stronger domain robustness than finetuning in zero-shot settings.
Inference-time control complements finetuning-based control for attribute manipulation.
Human evaluation on Bengali confirms the effectiveness of inference-time attribute control in low-resource settings.
PEARL is a prompting framework that reasons over long documents by decomposing questions into a sequence of actions and executing them on the document.
PEARL consists of three stages—action mining, plan formulation, and plan execution—each implemented via zero-shot or few-shot prompting of large language models.
PEARL outperforms zero-shot prompting and chain-of-thought prompting on a challenging subset of the QuALITY dataset.
Ablation experiments show that each stage of PEARL is critical to its performance.
The parsing process for top-down and bottom-up RST discourse parsing strategies is converted into prompts for large language models.
Llama 2 is fine‑tuned with QLoRA to perform RST discourse parsing.
Llama 2 with 70 billion parameters using the bottom‑up prompting strategy achieves state‑of‑the‑art performance on RST‑DT, Instr‑DT, and GUM corpora.
A parser trained on the GUM corpus generalizes to RST‑DT, attaining performance comparable to parsers trained directly on RST‑DT.
A multi-relational model derives word embeddings from natural language definitions by leveraging their recursive semantic structure.
The model automatically extracts relations linking defined and defining terms from dictionaries.
Learning word embeddings is formalized via a translational framework in hyperbolic space to capture the global semantic structure of definitions.
The framework imposes desired structural constraints while preserving semantic mapping for controllable and interpretable traversal.
Hyperbolic word embeddings outperform Euclidean embeddings.
The multi-relational approach achieves competitive results compared to state-of-the-art neural models while being intrinsically more efficient and interpretable.
ConstraintChecker is a plugin that provides and checks explicit relational constraints for commonsense knowledge base reasoning with large language models.
ConstraintChecker uses a rule-based module to generate a list of constraints for a new knowledge instance.
ConstraintChecker uses a zero-shot learning module to verify whether a knowledge instance satisfies all generated constraints.
ConstraintChecker aggregates constraint-checking results with the output of the main prompting technique to produce the final answer.
ConstraintChecker consistently improves performance of prompting methods on commonsense knowledge base reasoning benchmarks.
Gradient-Based Red Teaming (GBRT) automatically generates diverse prompts that are likely to cause language models to output unsafe responses.
GBRT trains prompts by scoring language model responses with a safety classifier and backpropagating through the frozen safety classifier and language model.
A variant of GBRT adds a realism loss to improve the coherence of generated prompts.
A variant of GBRT fine-tunes a pretrained model to generate prompts instead of directly learning prompts.
GBRT finds prompts that trigger unsafe responses more effectively than a strong reinforcement learning-based red teaming approach.
GBRT remains effective at finding unsafe prompts even when the language model has been fine-tuned to produce safer outputs.
A framework enables system-level use of natural language feedback to formalize design decisions in a human-in-the-loop process.
Metric design for tasks can be derived from system-level natural language feedback.
Language model prompt design can be used to refine model responses based on system-level natural language feedback.
System-level natural language feedback improves search query generation.
System-level natural language feedback improves dialog response generation.
Combining system-level and instance-level feedback yields additional performance gains.
Human-written instance-level feedback leads to more grounded refinements than GPT-3.5-generated feedback.
MultiMUC is a multilingual parallel corpus for template filling that includes translations of the MUC-4 benchmark into Arabic, Chinese, Farsi, Korean, and Russian.
Automatic translations for MultiMUC are generated using a strong multilingual machine translation system.
Original English annotations are manually projected into each target language in MultiMUC.
Human translations are provided for key portions of the development and test splits in each language of MultiMUC.
Baseline evaluations on MultiMUC are performed with state-of-the-art template filling models for MUC-4 and with ChatGPT.
MUC-4 and the supervised baselines are released to facilitate further work on document-level information extraction in multilingual settings.
Code-switched language identification is reformulated as a sentence-level multi-label tagging problem.
New evaluation metrics are defined that better reflect desired performance for code-switched language identification.
Three architectures for sentence-level multi-label code-switched language identification are investigated.
Scaling code-switched language identification to a larger set of languages and using simpler architectures enables faster inference.
Empirical evidence shows that existing approaches are inadequate for code-switched language identification.
Precise Outline-conditioned Generation is defined as generating stories based on specific, sentence-level outlines.
The WPOG and CDM datasets are constructed for Precise Outline-conditioned Generation.
Strong baselines based on fine-tuned BART and GPT-2 models and zero-shot evaluation of ChatGPT and Vicuna are provided for Precise Outline-conditioned Generation.
An issue of imbalanced utilization of outline information is identified across fine-tuned and zero-shot models in Precise Outline-conditioned Generation.
An explicit outline utilization control approach is proposed to address imbalanced outline utilization.
A novel framework that leverages the task duality between summarization and generation is proposed for Precise Outline-conditioned Generation.
The proposed approaches alleviate imbalanced outline utilization and enhance the quality of Precise Outline-conditioned Generation in both fine-tuning and zero-shot settings.
FACTOR is a scalable approach that transforms a factual corpus into a benchmark evaluating a language model's propensity to generate true facts versus similar incorrect statements.
Wiki-FACTOR, News-FACTOR, and Expert-FACTOR are benchmarks created with FACTOR for assessing factuality in Wikipedia, news, and expert domains.
FACTOR benchmark scores increase with language model size and improve when the model is augmented with retrieval.
FACTOR benchmark scores and perplexity do not always agree on model ranking.
When benchmark scores and perplexity disagree, benchmark scores better reflect factuality in open-ended generation as measured by human annotators.
A methodology to quantify bias in abstractive opinion summarisation models is established.
Bias in abstractive summarisation models is traced from pre-trained models through various adaptation methods.
Most abstractive summarisation models exhibit intrinsic bias.
Tuning a smaller number of parameters during adaptation results in less bias than standard fine-tuning.
Diversity of topics in fine-tuning training data is critical for bias mitigation.
A systematic evaluation of 16 language models on 10 English probing datasets compares template-based and template-free approaches in both general and biomedical domains.
Template-free and template-based probing often rank models differently, except for the top domain-specific models.
Accuracy scores decrease substantially when using template-free prompts compared to template-based prompts.
Perplexity is negatively correlated with accuracy in template-free probing, while it is positively correlated with accuracy in template-based probing.
Models predict the same answers more frequently across prompts in template-based probing than in template-free probing.
Hyperparameter sensitivity is formalized with similarity-based sensitivity and performance-based sensitivity metrics.
Experiments on two character-level sequence-to-sequence tasks quantify hyperparameter sensitivity of transformers and LSTMs.
Transformers are slightly more sensitive to hyperparameter choices than LSTMs according to both similarity-based and performance-based sensitivity metrics.
Transformers are not more sensitive to batch size than LSTMs.
MAFIA enables simultaneous debiasing of pretrained language models across multiple bias dimensions.
MAFIA constructs diverse counterfactual data augmentation for multiple bias dimensions using structured knowledge and a large generative model in a semi-automated manner.
Extensive evaluation across multiple tasks and languages demonstrates the efficacy of MAFIA for multi-bias debiasing.
Importance-Aware Data Augmentation (IADA) augments document-level neural machine translation training data using token importance estimated from hidden state norms and training gradient norms.
IADA outperforms several existing data augmentation approaches for document-level neural machine translation.
IADA yields statistically significant BLEU improvements at both sentence and document levels compared to strong document-level neural machine translation baselines.
A simple approach uses multilingual machine translation models for multilingual and low-resource grammatical error correction.
Multilingual machine translation models can perform grammatical error correction without any fine-tuning.
Fine-tuning multilingual machine translation models improves grammatical error correction quality.
The multilingual model outperforms similar-sized mT5-based models on grammatical error correction.
The multilingual model competes favorably with larger models on grammatical error correction.
MultiBully-Ex is a benchmark dataset of code-mixed cyberbullying memes that provides multimodal explanations highlighting visual and textual evidence.
A CLIP projection based multimodal shared‑private multitask model generates visual and textual explanations for meme cyberbullying detection.
Training with multimodal explanations improves the generation of textual justifications for meme cyberbullying decisions.
Training with multimodal explanations enhances the identification of visual evidence supporting meme cyberbullying decisions.
A dataset with orders of magnitude more distinct entity types and descriptions than existing NER datasets is constructed from an entity linking benchmark.
Large-scale label interpretation learning scales up the number and granularity of entity types to provide a strong semantic prior for few-shot named entity recognition.
Scaling up label interpretation learning yields strong zero- and few-shot NER performance across in-domain, cross-domain, and cross-lingual settings.
Task arithmetic enables modular cross-lingual transfer using full model fine-tuning.
The proposed approach achieves strong performance on multilingual benchmarks for both high-resource and low-resource languages.
Perturbation rules that transform German sentences into colloquial forms covering 18 distinct language phenomena were crafted.
Synthetic test sets for four task-oriented dialogue datasets were created using the perturbation rules.
Intent recognition accuracy degrades modestly on colloquial German varieties.
Slot detection performance drops substantially on colloquial German varieties.
A transfer experiment from Standard American English to synthetic Urban African American Vernacular English corroborates the observed robustness patterns.
A pipeline extracts, isolates, and aligns spatial terms in basic locative constructions from parallel text.
Shannon entropy measures cross-linguistic variability of spatial term use across eight languages.
Variability in functional spatial terms differs significantly from variability in geometric spatial terms.
Latent variable modeling supports a division of spatial terms into geometric and functional classes.
A corpus-based empirical test demonstrates that geometric and functional spatial terms exhibit different cross-linguistic variability.
VOLTAGE is a contrastive learning based OCR methodology that uses auto-glyph feature recommendation for cluster-based labeling.
VOLTAGE augments labeled data through image transformations and generative adversarial networks.
VOLTAGE is evaluated on the Takri script and additional low- and high-resource Indic scripts, demonstrating universal applicability.
Baseline and ablation studies are conducted to assess the contributions of VOLTAGE's components.
Downstream use cases for the Takri script are built to demonstrate the usefulness of VOLTAGE.
A new dataset of late antique and medieval Hebrew poetry annotated for metaphor is introduced.
The dataset provides expert annotations that distinguish figurative from literal language use.
Baseline results for metaphor detection on the dataset are reported.
A novel framework is introduced to measure semantic sensitivity of natural language inference models.
The framework generates adversarial examples that preserve semantics while altering surface form using conditional text generation.
The framework evaluates NLI models by requiring them to predict a symmetric equivalence entailment between original and adversarial inputs.
Empirical evaluation shows that semantic sensitivity leads to performance degradation in both in-domain and out-of-domain settings.
Ablation analysis demonstrates that semantic sensitivity causes major inconsistency in model predictions across models, datasets, and inference variations.
A transformer-based architecture inspired by computational biology is presented for automated cognate detection.
The method performs better than existing methods beyond a certain amount of supervision.
Performance improves steadily with increased supervision, demonstrating the efficacy of utilizing labeled information.
Using multiple sequence alignments as input and an end-to-end architecture with a link prediction head reduces computation time while yielding superior performance.
A large instruction dataset comprising over two million diverse instructions across a broad set of topics is created.
LaMini-LM, a collection of encoder-decoder and decoder-only models of varying sizes, is fine‑tuned on the instruction dataset.
LaMini-LM models are evaluated on fifteen NLP benchmarks using automatic metrics and human assessment.
A new benchmark dataset for hallucination‑inducing question answering is introduced to assess model hallucination.
LaMini-LM models achieve performance comparable to strong baselines while being substantially smaller.
GSM8K-Zero is a math QA dataset composed of questions that can be answered without any calculations.
Large language models, including Llama-2 models and Claude-2, generate lengthy and unnecessary calculations when answering questions from GSM8K-Zero.
Experiments reveal underlying reasons why large language models produce redundant calculations and reasoning.
Geo-Encoder integrates Chinese geographical semantics into re-ranking pipelines using a chunk-argument bi-encoder architecture.
Geo-Encoder employs off-the-shelf tools to segment text into geographical spans that serve as chunking units.
Geo-Encoder includes a multi-task learning module that learns an attention matrix to weight chunk contributions to geographic representations.
Geo-Encoder introduces an asynchronous update mechanism that directs the model to focus on specific chunks during training.
Geo-Encoder achieves substantial improvement in Hit@1 performance over MGEO-BERT on the GeoTES dataset.
HiGen encodes dynamic text representations for hierarchical text classification using a text-generation-based framework with language models.
HiGen employs a level-guided loss function to capture relationships between text and label name semantics.
HiGen incorporates a task-specific pretraining strategy that adapts the language model to in-domain knowledge and improves performance for classes with limited examples.
ENZYME is a new dataset for hierarchical text classification consisting of PubMed articles annotated with Enzyme Commission numbers.
HiGen achieves superior performance compared to existing approaches on ENZYME, WOS, and NYT datasets while efficiently handling data and mitigating class imbalance.
Measuring the performance gap between zero-shot evaluations on human-translated and machine-translated target texts across multiple languages serves as an indicator of translation errors.
Translation inconsistencies disproportionately impact low-resource languages in the XNLI benchmark.
Manual reannotation of Hindi and Urdu test instances reveals poor agreement with the original English labels, confirming translation errors.
Multimodal argument mining classifies argumentative fallacies in political debates.
The first corpus for multimodal fallacy classification in political debates is released.
Incorporating the audio modality improves fallacy classification performance.
Framing fallacy classification as a multimodal task captures paralinguistic aspects of fallacious arguments.
A joint embedding architecture that incorporates input labels and definitions classifies offensive speech via a Prototypical Network.
Meta-learning approaches are employed to leverage the diversity of offensive speech corpora for reliable and efficient detection.
The model attains a large fraction of maximal performance while using only a small fraction of the available training data across multiple datasets.
A case study of training strategies for combating resource scarcity in offensive speech classification is provided.
An interactive task-based evaluation of neural, supervised, and classical topic models is conducted.
Topic models are combined with a classifier to assist humans in content analysis and document annotation.
The Contextual Neural Topic Model achieves the highest performance on cluster evaluation metrics and human evaluations in the interactive task.
LDA performs competitively with two neural topic models in simulated experiments and user studies, despite lower coherence scores.
Automated coherence metrics are shown to be insufficient for assessing topic modeling capabilities, and selecting appropriate neural topic models can surpass classical models on practical tasks.
Style-News uses publisher metadata to model publisher templates that include text type, political stance, and credibility.
Style-News includes a style-aware neural news generator that can produce news conditioned on a specific publisher.
Style-News trains style and source discriminators to identify the publisher of a news article and to distinguish human-written from machine-generated news.
Style-News evaluates generated news with metrics for language fluency, content preservation, and style adherence, achieving higher scores than prior methods.
Style-News’s discriminative model achieves higher accuracy in publisher prediction and neural fake news detection than state-of-the-art baselines.
AutoAugment is adapted to improve rule-based text augmentation methods.
The adapted AutoAugment method boosts existing augmentation methods.
Enhanced rule-based augmentation methods improve cutting-edge pretrained language models.
The Defining Issues Test is applied to evaluate moral judgment and reasoning of large language models in Chinese, Hindi, Russian, Spanish, Swahili, and English.
ChatGPT, GPT-4, and Llama2Chat-70B are evaluated for multilingual moral judgment and reasoning using the Defining Issues Test.
Moral reasoning ability, as measured by post-conventional scores, is substantially lower for Hindi and Swahili than for English, Chinese, Russian, and Spanish across the evaluated models.
Moral judgments of large language models vary considerably across languages.
Introduces the notion of source sentences as input sentences that contain essential information for the generated summary.
Provides annotations of source sentences for reference summaries and PEGASUS-generated system summaries on CNN/DailyMail and XSum document-summary pairs.
Formulates the task of automatic source sentence detection and establishes a strong baseline for it.
Demonstrates that a perplexity-based method performs well for source sentence detection in highly abstractive summarization settings.
Demonstrates that similarity-based methods perform robustly for source sentence detection in relatively extractive summarization settings.
A parser architecture combines strictly left-to-right generative language models with tree-decoding modules to achieve strict incrementality across languages.
Incremental constituent parsers are evaluated for their ability to generate full parse trees from prefix representations alone.
A comparative analysis is performed between strictly incremental parsers, non-incremental parsers, and partially incremental parsers.
Corpus-Steered Query Expansion (CSQE) expands queries using both corpus-derived pivotal sentences and LLM-generated expansions.
CSQE leverages LLMs' relevance assessment capability to select pivotal sentences from initially retrieved documents.
CSQE improves relevance prediction between queries and target documents without requiring any training.
Experiments demonstrate that CSQE achieves strong performance, particularly for queries lacking LLM knowledge.
CharSpan introduces character-span noise augmentation into high-resource language training data for machine translation to extremely low-resource languages.
Character-span noise augmentation serves as a regularization technique that increases model robustness to lexical divergences between high-resource and extremely low-resource languages.
CharSpan enables effective zero-shot cross-lingual transfer for machine translation from extremely low-resource languages to English.
CharSpan achieves state-of-the-art performance among zero-shot methods on closely related high-resource and extremely low-resource language pairs across three diverse language families.
Extreme Fine-Tuning (EFT) fine-tunes a pre-trained model using a brief backpropagation phase followed by an iterative extreme learning machine classifier training.
Extreme Fine-Tuning (EFT) achieves faster training times than state-of-the-art approaches while maintaining comparable model performance on text classification tasks.
Contrast-Consistent Ranking (CCR) extends the binary Contrast-Consistent Search method to ranking tasks by adapting Max-Margin Loss, Triplet Loss, and Ordinal Regression objectives.
CCR probes language model representations with a logical constraint that statements and their negations map to consistent true-false poles across multiple items, yielding self-consistent rankings.
CCR achieves performance that is better than or comparable to prompting-based ranking techniques across various language models and datasets.
Prompting-based ranking techniques, including pairwise, pointwise, and listwise prompting, can produce self-inconsistent rankings even when calibrated and using constrained decoding.
FlowSeq leverages flow matching for conditional text generation.
FlowSeq generates text in a few sampling steps.
FlowSeq is trained with a novel anchor loss.
FlowSeq eliminates the need for hyperparameter optimization of the noise schedule in diffusion models.
FlowSeq achieves competitive performance on question generation, open-domain dialogue, and paraphrasing tasks.
A method distills generation of motivational interviewing-style reflections from GPT-4 into smaller language models.
GPT-4 generates reflections with near-perfect success using zero-shot prompting, outperforming prior methods.
Fine-tuned GPT-2-small achieves high success in generating reflections on a hold-out test set.
Fine-tuned GPT-2-XL achieves higher success than GPT-2-small in generating reflections on a hold-out test set.
GPT-4 functions as a zero-shot classifier to evaluate the quality of distilled reflection models, attaining substantial inter-rater reliability.
Three data curation methods—sample removal, caption replacement, and image replacement via a text-to-image generation model—are introduced for training image captioning models.
Evaluation on Flickr30K and COCO with BLIP and BEiT-3 shows that these curation methods improve image captioning performance.
Rendezvous (RVS) introduces a task and dataset of English geospatial instructions for reaching a target location using map knowledge.
RVS exhibits a richer use of spatial allocentric relations than previous text-based navigation benchmarks.
RVS requires resolving more spatial relations simultaneously compared to previous text-based navigation benchmarks.
CEV-LM is a lightweight, semi-autoregressive language model that utilizes constrained edit vectors to control the shape of generated text.
CEV-LM controls three complementary metrics—speed, volume, and circuitousness—that quantify the pacing and complexity of generated text.
CEV-LM provides more targeted and precise control of speed, volume, and circuitousness while preserving semantic content.
CEV-LM requires less training data than existing control approaches for text generation.
CEV-LM contains fewer parameters than existing control approaches for text generation.
Current machine translation systems exhibit inherent limitations in generating gender-neutral translations.
Prompting GPT-4 for gender-neutral translation demonstrates potential while also encountering challenges.
A manual comparative analysis of machine translation systems and GPT-4 for gender-neutral translation is performed.
Non-Autoregressive Proxy models predict scalar-valued sequence-level attributes directly from encoder representations, bypassing autoregressive decoding.
Non-Autoregressive Proxy models predict metrics such as BERTScore for machine translation and word error rate for automatic speech recognition.
Non-Autoregressive Proxy models outperform ensembles for out-of-distribution detection in machine translation while providing faster inference.

Non-Autoregressive Proxy models generate performance predictions for downstream tasks such that data filtering and resource optimization predictions surpass predictive uncertainty methods and are highly inference efficient.
VlogQA is a Vietnamese spoken-language machine reading comprehension dataset comprising 10,076 question-answer pairs derived from 1,230 YouTube transcript documents covering food and travel topics.
VlogQA provides a resource for studying machine reading comprehension on spoken Vietnamese, addressing a gap where existing corpora focus on formal written texts.
Baseline deep-learning models achieve the highest reported F1 and exact match scores on VlogQA, indicating strong performance on spoken-language MRC.
An analysis of challenges and opportunities associated with using real-world spoken data for Vietnamese machine reading comprehension is presented.
Tree-of-Experts is a prompting method that improves the generation of valid Winograd Schema Challenge instances.
WSC+ is a dataset of 3,026 LLM-generated sentences for the Winograd Schema Challenge.
The Winograd Schema Challenge framework is extended with new ambiguous and offensive categories to assess model overconfidence and bias.
Analysis shows that LLMs do not consistently outperform other models when evaluating their own generated Winograd Schema questions.
GPT-4 attains substantially lower accuracy on WSC+ than human performance.
A contrastive learning method injects English Wiktionary as supervision to improve token-level contextual representations.
The method achieves a new state-of-the-art result on the original Word-In-Context (WiC) test set in an unsupervised setting.
Two new WiC test sets are introduced and the fine-tuning method yields substantial improvements on them.
Modest improvements are observed for the semantic frame induction task after applying the fine-tuning method.
The fine-tuning approach is adaptable to any language that has a large Wiktionary.
Targeted Paraphrasing via RL (TPRL) learns a policy to generate challenging adversarial samples that improve language model performance.
TPRL uses FLAN-T5 as a generator and proximal policy optimization to produce adversarial examples.
TPRL’s reward combines classifier confusion with a Mutual Implication score to preserve the original text meaning.
TPRL discovers natural adversarial attacks on four diverse NLP classification tasks, as demonstrated by automatic and human evaluation.
TPRL outperforms strong baselines and generalizes across classifiers and datasets.
TPRL generates diverse and influential adversarial examples by combining language modeling and reinforcement learning.
An annotated dataset with fine-grained stereotype scores for sentences is created.
Pre-trained language models are trained on the annotated dataset to predict sentence-level stereotype scores.
Analyses reveal connections and differences between stereotypes and social issues such as hate speech, sexism, sentiments, and disadvantaged and advantaged groups.
Fine-grained stereotype scores are demonstrated to be a relevant and competitive dimension for research on social issues.
Semantic graph smoothing enhances pretrained sentence embeddings to yield more discriminative sentence representations.
An unsupervised approach learns discriminative sentence representations.
Enhanced sentence representations improve text clustering performance.
Enhanced sentence representations improve text classification performance.
Evaluation on eight benchmarks demonstrates consistent improvements for supervised and unsupervised document categorization tasks.
A speaker contribution-based dialogue act modeling framework predicts therapist interventions and client emotions at the utterance level in psychotherapy dialogues.
Fine-tuned language models and lightweight adapter solutions are evaluated on a Hebrew psychotherapy dialogue dataset for intervention prediction and emotion recognition.
Lightweight adapter solutions achieve performance comparable to fully fine-tuned models for therapist intervention prediction and client emotion recognition.
Analysis of model predictions reveals ambiguities in categorical clinical coding that can inform refinement of the coding schema.
Model-predicted utterance-level emotions show a positive correlation with client self-reported emotions.
VEIL vets extracted image labels from in-the-wild captions for weakly-supervised object detection without using bounding boxes.
A Caption Label Noise dataset containing annotated types of label noise in captions is introduced.
A classifier is trained to predict whether an extracted label is actually present in the image.
The classifier generalizes across dataset boundaries and across categories.
The classifier improves weakly-supervised object detection performance compared to using unvetted labels.
Extract-then-Evaluate extracts key sentences from long documents and evaluates summaries by prompting large language models.
Extract-then-Evaluate reduces evaluation costs for long document summary evaluation.
Extract-then-Evaluate achieves higher correlation with human evaluations than prior large language model–based evaluation methods.
Practical recommendations for optimal document length and sentence extraction methods improve cost-effectiveness and accuracy of large language model–based text generation evaluation.
Language-agnostic speaker replication is defined as replicating a speaker’s voice irrespective of the language spoken.
Multi-level attention aggregation probes and amplifies speaker-specific attributes in a hierarchical manner for language-agnostic speaker replication.
The model achieves substantial speaker similarity across seen and unseen speakers and languages.
The model generalizes to out-of-domain cases in language-agnostic speaker replication.
A novel unsupervised pre-training method retrieves and ranks questions.
The method leverages knowledge distillation from a basic question retrieval model.
The method introduces a new pre-training task and objective for learning to rank questions by relevance to a query.
The method achieves state-of-the-art performance on QRC and Quora-match datasets.
Combining re-ranking and retrieval models improves performance.
Interviews with multiple researchers working with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities on language technology projects in Australia are reported.
A set of recommended practices for NLP researchers to increase attention to the process of engagements with Indigenous communities is presented.
Ethical considerations in building NLP technologies for Indigenous languages are analyzed, emphasizing that projects should primarily serve Indigenous communities.
A hypothesis-driven statistical framework analyzes self-rationalising models.
The framework implements a hypothesis about task solving using a Bayesian network and translates internal states into natural language with templates.
The framework compares Bayesian network explanations to LLM-generated free-text explanations using automatic and human evaluations.
Demonstration with an example hypothesis and two Bayesian network realizations shows that the resulting models do not exhibit strong similarity to GPT-3.5.
HumBEL provides a human-in-the-loop approach that applies clinical techniques from Speech Language Pathology to evaluate demographic factors of language models in human‑machine conversations.
HumBEL is evaluated by a clinically licensed speech language pathologist.
Automated techniques are introduced to complement clinical evaluation of language models at scale.
GPT‑3.5 mimics the inference abilities of humans aged 6–15 while surpassing typical 21‑year‑old performance on memorization tasks.
GPT‑3.5 demonstrates limited pragmatic skills in social language use.
A set of desiderata for context use in question answering models is defined, including both previously discussed and novel criteria.
A comprehensive evaluation of question answering systems on multiple datasets is performed according to all defined desiderata simultaneously.
Systems that are less susceptible to noise are not necessarily more consistent in their answers when given irrelevant context.
Systems that are more susceptible to noise are more likely to answer correctly when the context conflicts with their parametric knowledge.
The combination of conflicting knowledge and noise can drastically reduce question answering system performance.
An evaluation method assesses language models' ability to reproduce human variability in next-word prediction by exact matching against a prerecorded dataset of alternative single-word continuations.
GPT2, BLOOM, and ChatGPT exhibit low calibration to human uncertainty in the next-word prediction task.
Expected calibration error fails to reflect calibration to human uncertainty in next-word prediction, indicating it should not be relied upon in this setting.
A fine-grained NLL loss is proposed to reduce hallucinations in text summarization and simplification.
Fine-grained data cleaning strategies based on word-level NLL among entities are proposed to reduce hallucinations.
Word-level NLL among entities provides a better signal for distinguishing factual from non-factual examples than the assumption underlying loss truncation.
Loss truncation's effectiveness is limited when noisy targets do not exhibit higher NLL loss.
An analysis of loss values for factual versus non-factual examples reveals that the underlying assumption of loss truncation is often violated.
Model confidence in the correct class on many pairs of points is strongly correlated across models in six popular language classification benchmarks.
Anchor Point Selection selects small subsets of datasets that capture model behavior across the entire dataset.
Anchor points reliably rank models across diverse language model‑prompt pairs, outperforming uniform sampling and other baselines for accurate model ranking.
A small number of anchor points can be used to estimate model per‑class predictions on all other points in a dataset with low error.
Anchor Point Maps visualize model performance across regions of the dataset distribution and facilitate comparisons of different models.
A context-aware attention framework aligns visual and textual features for multimodal hateful content detection.
The alignment enables selective focus on modality-specific features before fusing them.
Evaluation on English and non-English hateful meme datasets shows performance improvements over prior state-of-the-art systems.
GEAR is a query-tool grounding algorithm that delegates tool grounding to small language models and tool execution to large language models.
GEAR employs semantic and pattern-based evaluation at both question and answer levels to achieve generalizable tool grounding.
GEAR achieves higher precision in tool grounding than prior strategies that rely on large language model prompting.
GEAR improves downstream task accuracy while reducing computational cost compared to baseline tool-augmented methods.
GEAR demonstrates strong generalizability across novel tasks, tools, and different small language models, as shown by evaluation on multiple datasets spanning several downstream tasks.
A perturbation technique that incorporates visual similarity of complex glyphs for abugida writing systems is introduced.
A training strategy designed to improve robustness of neural machine translation systems for abugida glyphs is proposed.
The introduced perturbation technique and training strategy enable non-degenerate neural machine translation systems with improved robustness for low-resource abugida translation tasks.
The approach is evaluated on six abugida languages (Bengali, Hindi, Myanmar, Khmer, Lao, Thai), demonstrating improved robustness.
AttriSage extracts product attribute values from product descriptions using graph neural networks.
AttriSage improves attribute value extraction accuracy compared to baseline sequence tagging approaches.
GaussCSE is a Gaussian-distribution-based contrastive learning framework for sentence embedding.
GaussCSE handles asymmetric inter-sentential relations.
GaussCSE includes a similarity measure for identifying entailment relations.
GaussCSE achieves performance comparable to previous methods on natural language inference tasks.
GaussCSE can estimate the direction of entailment relations between sentences.
Comparative assessment using pairwise comparisons is introduced as a zero-shot evaluation method for natural language generation.
Comparative assessment outperforms absolute score prediction for moderate-sized open-source large language models such as FlanT5 and Llama2-chat.
Comparative assessment attains performance competitive with state-of-the-art natural language generation evaluation methods.
Large language models exhibit strong positional biases when making pairwise comparisons for natural language generation assessment.
Debiasing techniques are proposed that mitigate positional biases and improve the performance of comparative assessment.

3H-TH (3D Rotation and Translation in Hyperbolic space) captures symmetry, antisymmetry, inversion, commutative composition, non-commutative composition, hierarchy, and multiplicity relation patterns simultaneously.
3H-TH outperforms existing state-of-the-art models on accuracy, hierarchy property, and other relation patterns in low-dimensional space.
3H-TH performs similarly to existing state-of-the-art models in high-dimensional space.
Dynamic masking rate schedules adjust the masking rate throughout masked language model pretraining.
Linearly decreasing the masking rate during pretraining improves average GLUE accuracy relative to fixed-rate baselines.
Exposure to both high and low masking rate regimes provides benefits from both settings.
Masking rate scheduling yields a speedup in pretraining for BERT-base.
Masking rate scheduling achieves a Pareto improvement for BERT-large.
A two-step generation approach creates synthetic contexts and question-answer pairs to augment existing reading comprehension datasets.
Augmenting reading comprehension datasets with synthetic contexts and question-answer pairs enhances the robustness of question answering models to natural distribution shifts.
Experiments on four question answering datasets under varying distribution shifts demonstrate that synthetic data facilitates domain generalization for question answering models.
Perturbed kNN-MT generates more diverse translation candidates.
Perturbed kNN-MT expands the search space of kNN-MT to incorporate diverse words into translation candidates.
Perturbed kNN-MT addresses the overcorrection problem in translation models.
The proposed methods enable control of translation diversity by tuning the perturbation magnitude.
Experiments demonstrate that the proposed methods drastically improve candidate diversity.
Existing computational models of semantic change can extract reliable sentiment information from historical corpora.
Words that underwent semantic change exhibit greater sentiment change than historically stable words.
Sentiment information is integrated into standard models of semantic change for individual words.
Integration of sentiment information yields mixed impact on overall performance of semantic change models.
This work constitutes the first attempt to enrich standard semantic change models with sentiment information.
XLM is a Python package that streamlines implementation of small non-autoregressive language models.
XLM includes a companion package that supplies a suite of small pre-trained non-autoregressive language models for research use.
Implicit feedback signals such as user response length, sentiment, and future human reaction are used to measure the quality of machine-generated utterances.
Training social conversational agents with these implicit feedback signals improves human-evaluated response quality compared to baseline models.
Optimizing for conversation length as an implicit signal increases the generation of controversial or unfriendly content relative to baseline.
Optimizing for positive sentiment or future human reaction as implicit signals decreases the generation of controversial or unfriendly content relative to baseline.
The task of automatic assessment of sentence translation exercises is defined, grading student responses according to educator-specified rubric criteria.
A Japanese-English sentence translation exercises dataset comprising 21 questions and 3,498 student responses is created.
Fine-tuned BERT is evaluated as a baseline for the automatic assessment task and achieves strong classification performance on correct responses but weaker performance on incorrect responses.
GPT-3.5 with few-shot learning is evaluated as a baseline and performs worse than fine-tuned BERT, highlighting the difficulty of the task for state-of-the-art large language models.
BOOM jointly translates lecture audio and slides, generating synchronized translated text, localized slides that preserve visual elements, and synthesized speech.
Slide-aware transcripts improve performance on downstream summarization and question answering tasks.
Forged-GAN-BERT is a modified GANBERT-based model for classifying forged novels.
Forged-GAN-BERT incorporates data augmentation from a forged novels generator (ChatGPT) and a GAN generator.
Forged-GAN-BERT achieves higher classification performance than other transformer-based models for forged novel detection.
Different prompt categories for generating forged novels are evaluated using similarity measures such as ROUGE-1, Jaccard similarity, Overlap Confident, and Cosine similarity.
Topic models are used to select in-context translation examples based on abstract semantic relationships below the domain level.
The topic-guided example selection method improves translation quality on challenging multilingual out-of-domain tests compared to baseline example selection strategies.
Adding few-shot examples and related keywords consistently improves translation quality in LLM-based machine translation.
Balancing example diversity with source similarity is necessary for effective in-context example selection in machine translation.
The example selection pipeline becomes overly restrictive when a targeted development set is available.

Multi-reference benchmarks for Russian grammatical error correction are constructed by adding two new independent raters' corrections to each of 7,444 learner sentences, yielding three references per sentence.
The new raters make more changes than the original raters, particularly at the lexical level.
Two popular grammatical error correction approaches achieve competitive performance on both the original single-reference datasets and the new multi-reference benchmarks.
Evaluating system scores against individual annotators demonstrates the impact of using multiple references overall and for specific error types.
Using the union of multiple references raises system scores and narrows the gap between system and human performance, leading to a more realistic evaluation of GEC systems.
The multi-reference annotations are released for research use.
A set of guidelines for choosing between human-labeled data and LLM-augmented data in computational social science classification tasks is established.
Human-labeled data yields superior or comparable performance to synthetically generated data from GPT-4 and Llama-2 across ten computational social science classification tasks.
Synthetic data augmentation improves performance on rare classes within multi-class classification tasks.
Training data size influences classification performance in computational social science tasks.
Zero-shot classification using GPT-4 and Llama-2 shows strong performance but generally underperforms specialized classifiers trained on moderately sized datasets.
A synonym-based word-level adversarial attack using a BERT masked language model in a black-box setting is introduced for Arabic text classification.
Human evaluators assess grammatical and semantic similarity between generated Arabic adversarial examples and original texts.
The generated Arabic adversarial examples are transferred to multiple text classification models to evaluate cross-model vulnerability.
Fine-tuned BERT models exhibit greater susceptibility to the synonym attacks and transferred attacks compared to WordCNN and WordLSTM models.
Adversarial training restores accuracy of fine-tuned BERT models against the synonym attacks.
Verification Assistant provides a browser-based interface for submitting URLs or media files to verify multimodal content.
Verification Assistant automatically extracts content from submitted URLs or media files and routes it to a suite of backend NLP classifiers.
Verification Assistant presents actionable credibility signals, AI-generation likelihood, and verification advice in an easy-to-digest format.
Verification Assistant integrates multiple NLP services into a unified framework for disinformation detection.
Extensive evaluation protocols assess robustness of grounding language models to knowledge bases under environmental, linguistic, and modal distribution shifts.
A data augmentation method is introduced to mitigate distribution shifts for grounding language models to knowledge bases.
Small and large language models exhibit poor robustness to unseen schema, language variations, and few-shot learning when grounded to knowledge bases.
Robustness challenges in grounding language models to knowledge bases are caused by distribution shifts across environmental, linguistic, and modal dimensions.
FiMMIA is a modular framework for multimodal membership inference attacks.
Perturbation-based membership inference methods are generalized to multimodal language models.
An extended baseline pipeline for detecting distribution shifts in multimodal datasets is released.
Distribution shifts in existing multimodal datasets are identified.

Neural networks are trained to analyze target model behavior on perturbed inputs, capturing interactions between semantic domains and loss values of members and non-members in the local neighborhood of each sample.
Perturbation-based membership inference attacks are effective in multimodal settings, as demonstrated by comprehensive evaluations on various fine-tuned multimodal models.
SciTrue provides source-level accountability and evidence traceability for scientific claim verification.
SciTrue links each claim component to explicit, verifiable scientific sources.
Human evaluation demonstrates that SciTrue achieves high fidelity in summary traceability, attribution accuracy, and context alignment.
SciTrue substantially outperforms RAG-based baselines such as GPT-4o-search-preview and Perplexity Sonar Pro in attribution performance.
EvalSense is a flexible, extensible framework for constructing domain-specific evaluation suites for large language models.
EvalSense provides out-of-the-box support for a broad range of model providers and evaluation strategies.
EvalSense includes an interactive guide that aids users in selecting evaluation methods.
EvalSense includes automated meta-evaluation tools that assess the reliability of evaluation approaches using perturbed data.
EvalSense demonstrates effectiveness in a case study of generating clinical notes from unstructured doctor-patient dialogues.
An unsupervised continual learning framework for medical automatic speech recognition is introduced.
The framework adapts to new audio data while preserving prior knowledge, enabling efficient domain adaptation without extensive retraining.
Experiments on real-world medical audio demonstrate that the framework outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.
AITutor-EvalKit evaluates the pedagogical quality of AI tutors using language technology.
AITutor-EvalKit provides software for demonstration and evaluation of AI tutors.
AITutor-EvalKit enables model inspection and data visualization for AI tutors.
AITutor-EvalKit can be used to collect user feedback and annotations for AI tutors.
Targeted adaptation of general vision-language models improves e-commerce performance while preserving broad multimodal capabilities.
A novel extensive evaluation suite covering deep product understanding, strict instruction following, and dynamic attribute extraction is introduced.
An agentic AI workflow converts noisy pharmaceutical batch records into validated JSON using hybrid OCR, vision-language, and schema-guided large language models.
The workflow reduces QA review time from hours to minutes.
The workflow preserves GMP-critical structure while converting batch records to JSON.
SCILIRE creates datasets from scientific literature using a Human-AI teaming approach.
SCILIRE incorporates workflows for verifying and curating data within a Human-AI teaming framework.
SCILIRE enables an iterative workflow in which researchers review and correct AI outputs.
SCILIRE uses researcher corrections as feedback to improve future LLM-based inference.
Evaluation with intrinsic benchmarks and real-world case studies across multiple domains shows that SCILIRE improves extraction fidelity.
SCILIRE facilitates efficient dataset creation.
An interpretable model that scores subjective bias in documents using only textual content is introduced.
The model is trained on pairwise comparisons of Wikipedia article revisions where one version is more biased than the other.
The model can accurately perform pairwise comparisons for bias scoring.
Model parameters can be interpreted to identify words most indicative of bias.
The model is applied to study temporal evolution of bias in Wikipedia articles, providing explainable and validated bias scores.
The model is applied to compare bias across news sources, providing explainable and validated bias scores.
The model is applied to score bias in law amendments, providing explainable and validated bias scores.
Legal texts are found to be the least biased, news media the most biased, and Wikipedia articles exhibit intermediate bias levels.
MedRiskEval is a medical risk evaluation benchmark tailored to the medical domain.
PatientSafetyBench is a patient-oriented dataset containing 466 samples across five critical risk categories.
MedRiskEval and PatientSafetyBench are used to evaluate a variety of open- and closed-source large language models.
An initial foundation for safer deployment of large language models in healthcare is established.
PropGenie is a multi-agent framework based on large language models that provides comprehensive real estate assistance.
PropGenie coordinates eight specialized sub-agents for tasks such as search and recommendation, question answering, financial calculations, and task execution.
PropGenie integrates structured, unstructured, and multimodal retrieval-augmented generation techniques to incorporate diverse knowledge sources and advanced computational tools.
Experiments on real user queries demonstrate that PropGenie outperforms a general-purpose large language model and a domain-specific chatbot in real estate scenarios.
ESG-KG is a multi-modal knowledge graph system for automated ESG compliance assessment.
ESG-KG includes a multi-modal information extraction pipeline that processes text, tables, figures, and infographics from corporate sustainability reports.
Extracted multi-modal data are formalized into a structured knowledge graph representing ESG entities, relationships, and metrics.
The knowledge graph enables high-precision retrieval of ESG information across multiple source formats and reporting modalities.
The knowledge graph provides a verifiable evidence base that supports retrieval-augmented generation and LLM-based scoring and analysis in an automated ESG compliance system.
Stakeholder Suite maps actors, topics, and arguments in public debates.
Stakeholder Suite integrates actor detection, topic modeling, argument extraction, and stance classification in a unified pipeline.
Stakeholder Suite delivers fine-grained, source-grounded insights and adapts to diverse domains.
Stakeholder Suite achieves strong retrieval precision and stance classification accuracy.
Stakeholder Suite enables project teams to visualize influence networks, identify emerging controversies, and support evidence-based decision-making.
PEFT-Factory is a unified framework for efficient fine-tuning of autoregressive large language models using off-the-shelf and custom parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods.
PEFT-Factory includes implementations of 19 parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods.
PEFT-Factory provides 27 classification and text generation datasets covering 12 tasks.
PEFT-Factory incorporates both standard evaluation metrics and metrics specific to parameter-efficient fine-tuning.
PEFT-Factory offers a controlled and stable environment that improves replicability and benchmarking of parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods.
DeepPavlov 1.1 is an open-source NLP library that supports traditional tasks such as named entity recognition and sentiment classification.
DeepPavlov 1.1 provides tools for enhancing LLM truthfulness and reliability, including a hallucination detection model, an evergreen question classifier, and a toxicity classifier.
DeepPavlov 1.1 offers multilingual support and a flexible, easy-to-use interface.
DeepPavlov 1.1 includes an interactive online demonstration.
Diffusion-LM and DiffuSeq are adapted for machine translation tasks.
Diffusion-based machine translation models suffer from the multimodality problem that limits non-autoregressive translation performance.
Knowledge distillation from an autoregressive model enhances the performance of diffusion-based machine translation.
Diffusion-based machine translation models exhibit greater difficulty with long-range dependencies compared to other models, as revealed by analysis of translation quality across input lengths.
A benchmark evaluating diffusion models for machine translation against conventional Transformer and Levenshtein Transformer models is presented.
BornoDrishti is a unified OCR system based on a vision transformer that recognizes both printed and handwritten Bangla scripts within a single model.
BornoDrishti introduces a domain objective that learns domain-invariant representations while preserving script-specific features, eliminating the need for separate domain experts.
BornoDrishti achieves state-of-the-art performance for printed Bangla script recognition.
BornoDrishti matches or exceeds the accuracy of specialized uni-domain OCR systems despite being a single unified model.
VR-Arch allows users to modify BIM files directly within a virtual reality environment using voice commands.
VR-Arch employs a ReAct-based agentic system that selects appropriate tools according to the context of natural language queries.
VR-Arch supports complex natural language queries about BIM models and can combine queries with modifications to execute complex actions.
VR-Arch provides real-time, contextualized interaction in VR, facilitating faster and more natural architectural prototyping.
A domain-agnostic framework enables lightweight open-source LLMs to perform intent classification and generate clarifying questions for ambiguous user utterances.
An evaluation methodology jointly assesses intent classification accuracy and the timing and quality of clarifying questions.
Instruction-tuned lightweight LLMs achieve performance comparable to leading proprietary LLMs while substantially reducing inference cost.
Deployment of the framework in an e-commerce customer-care system reduces misrouting rate and improves automation rates, leading to cost savings.
An industry case study on developing an agentic system for summarizing multi-party dialogues is presented.
Robust evaluation methods for dialogue summarization that handle evolving requirements and task subjectivity are described.
Component-wise optimization enabled by task decomposition in an agentic architecture for dialogue summarization is demonstrated.
The impact of upstream data bottlenecks on the performance of dialogue summarization systems is analyzed.
Vendor lock-in caused by poor transferability of large language model prompts in dialogue summarization systems is identified.
SmartMatch integrates modern sentence encoders and lexical/fuzzy baselines with a vector database for translation memory retrieval.

SmartMatch provides a web-based UI that lets users enter query segments, switch retrieval backends and embedding models, inspect top‑k retrieved matches with similarity scores and qualitative cues, and observe end‑to‑end latency in real time.

SmartMatch includes a reproducible benchmark on multilingual translation memory data that evaluates retrieval quality using reference‑based machine translation metrics, coverage, and latency/throughput trade‑offs.
Encoder‑based retrieval in SmartMatch achieves full coverage with millisecond‑level latency and the strongest semantic‑quality scores among evaluated methods.
BM25 serves as a lightweight lexical baseline in SmartMatch that provides very low latency.
QSTN is an open-source Python framework for systematically generating responses from questionnaire-style prompts for in-silico surveys and annotation tasks with large language models.
QSTN enables robust evaluation of questionnaire presentation, prompt perturbations, and response generation methods.
Question structure and response generation methods significantly impact the alignment of generated survey responses with human answers.
Altering the presentation method reduces the compute cost required to obtain answers.
QSTN provides a no-code user interface that allows researchers to set up robust experiments with large language models without coding knowledge.
A method synthesizes realistic digital footprints using large language model agents from structured user profiles.
The method generates diverse and plausible sequences of user events and corresponding digital artifacts such as emails, messages, calendar entries, and reminders.
Intrinsic evaluation shows the generated dataset is more diverse and realistic than existing baseline synthetic datasets.
Models fine-tuned on the synthetic digital footprint dataset outperform models fine-tuned on other synthetic datasets on real-world out-of-distribution tasks.
A focused analysis of multilingual model merging from an efficiency perspective is provided.
Merging multilingual multitask models reduces initial training time substantially while maintaining quality parity.
Updating an individual language and re-merging reduces training costs compared to retraining the full multilingual model.
The merging strategy is evaluated across three independent tasks.
The approach is demonstrated on both public and proprietary industry datasets, confirming applicability to industrial use cases.
elfen extracts item-level linguistic features across eleven feature areas.
elfen supports extraction for up to 80 languages on large text datasets with limited computing resources.
elfen includes the largest collection of item-level linguistic features among Python libraries.
elfen enables linguistically informed data selection, outlier detection, and text collection comparison.
A context-aware detection framework identifies offensive query‑ad pairs that are offensive only in specific query‑ad contexts.
The framework employs a multi‑modal teacher‑student knowledge distillation architecture with a Gemini encoder‑only teacher model and a lightweight student model for low‑latency deployment.
A graph mining technique is used to discover rare offensive examples for training the detection models.
An Automated Evaluation Model based on a larger Gemini model with chain‑of‑thought reasoning evaluates detection performance in live A/B tests.
The detection framework reduces the serving of offensive query‑ad pairs compared to the baseline while preserving real‑time efficiency at massive scale.
JourneyBench benchmark assesses policy-aware agents in customer support.
JourneyBench uses graph representations to generate diverse realistic support scenarios.
JourneyBench introduces the User Journey Coverage Score metric to measure policy adherence.
Dynamic-Prompt Agent explicitly models policy control and significantly improves policy adherence compared to Static-Prompt Agent.
Dynamic-Prompt Agent enables smaller language models to achieve higher policy adherence than larger models.
Youden’s J statistic is theoretically aligned with selecting the best judge for comparing large language models.
Balanced Accuracy is an equivalent linear transformation of Youden’s J statistic.
Using Balanced Accuracy to select judges results in more robust classifier selection.
Pro-QuEST generates multiple-choice questions on specialized technical products using a prompt-chain approach with state-of-the-art large language models.
Pro-QuEST identifies key terms and topics from professional certification textbooks or product guides to inform question generation.
Pro-QuEST employs domain-knowledge guided prompts to produce multiple-choice questions.
Benchmarking results demonstrate that questions generated by Pro-QuEST can be evaluated with a range of open-source and proprietary large language models and compared to expert-created exams.
Question-type based prompts in Pro-QuEST improve the generation of questions with complexity levels approaching those of expert-designed certification exams.
User studies with Cisco personnel show high optimism for Pro-QuEST's practical usefulness in quickly compiling quizzes for self-assessment on novel product knowledge.
RegNLI formulates misbranding detection as an inference task between product claims and regulatory provisions.
RegNLI integrates a regulation-aware gating mechanism with a contrastive alignment objective to jointly optimize misbranding classification and statute mapping.
A curated dataset of FDA warning letters is used to construct structured representations of claims and statutes for training RegNLI.
RegNLI provides interpretable attention patterns that highlight critical linguistic cues for misbranding detection.
RegNLI outperforms strong baselines on the FDA-Misbrand dataset across accuracy, F1, and regulation alignment metrics.
Bayesian Optimization reduces the time required to tune Retrieval-Augmented Generation pipelines compared to grid search while maintaining comparable accuracy.
Local component-wise optimization of Retrieval-Augmented Generation pipelines offers a more practical balance for deployment than global pipeline optimization.
Performance gains from Bayesian Optimization plateau with larger evaluation budgets, indicating that moderate resource investments suffice for effective Retrieval-Augmented Generation tuning.
A systematic comparison of seven Bayesian Optimization strategies combining four surrogate models and two multi-fidelity methods is conducted across FiQA, SciFact, and HotpotQA datasets.
A framework that explores both global pipeline and local component-wise optimization of Retrieval-Augmented Generation pipelines is introduced.
Actionable guidelines for efficiently configuring and deploying high-performing Retrieval-Augmented Generation systems under real-world constraints are provided.
LLM BiasScope provides side-by-side comparison of large language model outputs with real-time bias analysis.
LLM BiasScope employs a two-stage bias detection pipeline that first detects sentence-level bias and then classifies bias types for biased sentences.
LLM BiasScope generates statistics, visualizations, and detailed breakdowns of bias types for both user prompts and model responses.
LLM BiasScope displays synchronized streaming responses for two models and highlights differences in bias distributions through a comparison view.
LLM BiasScope integrates multiple LLM providers using Hugging Face inference endpoints and the Vercel AI SDK, and supports export of analysis results to JSON and PDF.
MURSE is a framework that generates Chinese user-specific rumor-debunking passages through multi-round refinement guided by simulated user feedback.
User-specific debunking passages generated by MURSE are preferred over general debunking passages.
A hybrid system augments large language models with domain-specific knowledge graphs using a knowledge-graph-based retrieval approach.
The system implements a pipeline consisting of pre-processing, Cypher query generation, Cypher query processing, knowledge-graph retrieval, and large language model–enhanced response generation.
Evaluation on a curated dataset demonstrates high precision in retrieving correct knowledge-graph nodes.
The hybrid system surpasses a standalone large language model in accuracy and completeness according to an LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation.
The system delivers accurate responses within seconds, making it suitable for time-sensitive, precision-focused research contexts.
An approach for efficiently collecting nuanced dialectal datasets that yields high-quality, multipurpose data.
The KIND corpus is a collection of fine-grained Arabic dialect short texts manually curated through social collaboration rather than crawled from social media.
The KIND dataset includes aligned parallel corpora between Modern Standard Arabic and multiple Arabic dialects for translation and comparison studies.
The data collection approach incentivizes participation through educational gamification and competitions.
The KIND dataset provides coverage of under-represented Arabic dialect groups and fine-grained dialectal varieties.
DIALECTIC gathers factual knowledge about a startup and organizes the facts into a hierarchical question tree.
DIALECTIC synthesizes the gathered facts into natural-language arguments for and against an investment.
DIALECTIC iteratively critiques and refines the arguments through a simulated debate to surface the most convincing arguments.
DIALECTIC produces numeric decision scores that enable ranking and prioritization of investment opportunities.
DIALECTIC matches the precision of human venture capitalists in predicting startup success when evaluated via backtesting on real investment opportunities from five VC funds.

VideoMind is a training-free framework for long video understanding that orchestrates a multimodal large language model to decompose queries into sub-queries and invoke specialized modes with tailored context.
VideoMind includes a Multi-Scale Temporal Search mode that identifies and summarizes relevant video sub-snippets at varying time scales.
VideoMind includes a Single-Frame Visual Detail mode that provides precise spatial localization of objects.
VideoMind achieves state-of-the-art performance on the Video-MME, LongVideo, and MLVU benchmarks.
Synthetic data fine-tuning is evaluated for semantic search in enterprise team formation.
Two synthetic data generation strategies—augmenting real data with synthetic labels and generating synthetic employee profiles tailored to tasks—are investigated for team formation.
A pretrained text embedding model is fine-tuned using LoRA and Rank Aggregation techniques for the team formation task.
Combining both synthetic data generation strategies yields a model that outperforms existing pretrained models on a human-curated team formation dataset.
Docstring reformulation with a large language model is proposed as a method to improve code generation.
Two baseline methods for optimizing code generation via docstring reformulation are developed.
The baseline methods are evaluated on the HumanEval benchmark and on curated variants with degraded docstrings.

Performance of several open-source large language models remains largely unchanged when using LLM-reformulated docstrings instead of original or degraded inputs, demonstrating robustness to docstring details.
