In-depth analyses of the sensitivity of open-source large language models to docstring details, the potential for improvement via docstring reformulation, and the limitations of the employed methods are presented.
Compact multimodal language models serve as privacy-preserving alternatives for transcribing noisy clinical documents.
An evaluation of eight transcription systems on obstetric ultrasound reports written in regionally inflected Indian medical English is presented.
Compact multimodal models consistently outperform classical and neural OCR pipelines in transcription accuracy, noise sensitivity, numeric accuracy, and computational efficiency.
The robustness and linguistic adaptability of compact multimodal models make them viable candidates for on-premises healthcare digitization despite higher computational costs.
A question answering system deconstructs domain-specific vocabulary to improve retrieval from legal source documents.
The system parses complex legal document layouts, isolates sections and footnotes, and links them appropriately.
The system generates comprehensive long-form answers using precise domain-specific vocabulary.
A coverage metric classifies performance into recall-based coverage categories to facilitate human evaluation of recall.
A legal-domain question answering dataset is curated with expertise from law and corporate tax professionals.
Experiments and ablation studies demonstrate the usability and merit of the proposed system.
A modular, scalable end-to-end agentic pipeline extracts and selects relevant content from unstructured financial documents and generates question-answer pairs for fine-tuning small language models.
Small language models fine-tuned with synthetic question-answer data generated by the pipeline achieve competitive in-distribution performance compared to models trained on manually generated data.
Small language models fine-tuned with synthetic question-answer data generated by the pipeline demonstrate superior generalization relative to models trained on manually generated data.
The pipeline reduces manual data creation effort, thereby accelerating deployment of cost-effective small language models.
Embedding model comparison, Reciprocal Rank Fusion, embedding concatenation, and LLM-based re-ranking are evaluated as retrieval enhancements for Retrieval-Augmented Generation systems.
Zero-shot large language models outperform traditional cross-encoders in identifying high-relevance passages for Retrieval-Augmented Generation.
Context-aware embeddings, diverse chunking strategies, and model fine-tuning are investigated as methods to improve retrieval quality.
Retrieval enhancements produce consistent gains in Recall@10 and reduce the performance gap with Recall@50 across proprietary and public benchmarks.
Improved retrieval quality elevates downstream large language model response quality in a real-world customer support chatbot.
PromptLab is a web-based platform that enables collaborative prompt engineering for diverse natural language processing tasks and datasets.
PromptLab integrates AI-assisted prompt generation via OpenRouter and provides real-time validation with multiple large language models.
PromptLab includes a flexible templating system based on Jinja2, role-based project management, and peer review processes.
PromptLab offers programmatic access through RESTful APIs and supports self-hosted deployments via an easy CI/CD pipeline for institutional control.
PromptLab’s effectiveness is demonstrated through a controlled comparison study involving multiple researchers, benchmark datasets, models, and prompts.
PromptLab’s effectiveness is demonstrated through a case study in instruction tuning research where hundreds of prompts across many datasets were developed and validated by multiple team members.

A modular and interpretable framework uses LLMs to automate candidate assessment by integrating job descriptions, CVs, interview transcripts, and HR feedback into structured evaluation reports that mirror expert judgment.
Role-specific LLM-generated rubrics and a multi-agent architecture enable fine-grained, criteria-driven evaluations within the framework.
The framework produces transparent, auditable assessment reports, candidate comparisons, and ranked recommendations suitable for real-world hiring workflows.
An LLM-Driven Active Listwise Tournament mechanism ranks small candidate subsets and aggregates the listwise permutations using a Plackett–Luce model.
An active-learning loop selects the most informative candidate subsets, yielding globally coherent and sample-efficient rankings for large-scale talent acquisition.
MizanQA is a benchmark for assessing large language models on Moroccan legal multiple-choice questions with multiple correct answers.
MizanQA comprises a large set of expert-verified questions written in Modern Standard Arabic enriched with Moroccan idioms.
MizanQA reflects influences from Maliki jurisprudence, customary law, and French legal traditions.
MizanQA features variable numbers of answer options per question, creating added difficulty compared to single-answer settings.
Multilingual and Arabic-centric models are evaluated on MizanQA using zero-shot native-Arabic prompts.
Evaluation metrics for MizanQA include accuracy, a precision-penalized F1-like score, and calibration error.
Model evaluations on MizanQA reveal large performance gaps among the tested models.
Model evaluations on MizanQA reveal miscalibration, particularly when stricter precision penalties are applied.
MizanQA provides a baseline for future retrieval-augmented and rationale-focused setups.
Multimodal Auto-Completion (MAC) predicts upcoming characters in live chats using partially typed text and visual cues.
Benchmark datasets for MAC are created by adapting MMDialog and ImageChat to include multimodal context.
Leading vision-language models are evaluated against strong textual baselines, revealing trade-offs between accuracy and efficiency for multimodal auto-completion.
Router-Suggest is a router framework that dynamically selects between textual models and vision-language models based on dialog context.
A lightweight variant of Router-Suggest is designed for resource-constrained environments.
Router-Suggest achieves substantial speedup over the best-performing vision-language model.
User study shows that vision-language models provide higher user satisfaction, reduce typing effort, and improve completion quality in multi-turn conversations compared to textual models.
CLARIESG is an end-to-end system for ESG analysis over complex tables in corporate reports.
CLARIESG couples robust table extraction with a structured prompting framework for multi-table filtering, normalization, and program-of-thought reasoning.
CLARIESG outperforms standard prompting on ESG-focused multi-table benchmarks.
CLARIESG provides transparent, auditable reasoning for ESG analysis.
CLARIESG supports more reliable ESG analysis and greenwashing detection in real-world settings.
A synthetic data generation pipeline produces doctor-patient dialogues in both text and audio targeting a curated set of over 124,000 medical terms.
The pipeline generated over 1 billion audio recordings with ground‑truth transcriptions.
Fine‑tuning automatic speech recognition models with the synthetic corpus reduces overall word error rate.
Fine‑tuning automatic speech recognition models with the synthetic corpus improves transcription accuracy on medical terminology.
The pipeline provides a scalable method for vocabulary expansion and privacy preservation in medical speech recognition.
CASPER bridges discrete and continuous prompt optimization through feedback-guided gradient descent in embedding space.
CASPER uses a feedback module that generates detailed error analyses to provide optimization signals.
CASPER projects feedback-derived insights together with prompt tokens into embedding space to direct gradient descent.
CASPER incorporates fluency regularization that penalizes incomprehensible tokens, preserving prompt interpretability.
CASPER accelerates convergence by generating synthetic data that oversamples failure cases, addressing data scarcity.
CASPER improves accuracy across multiple tasks, demonstrating cross-domain generalizability on benchmarks such as WDC, DROP, GSM8K, VQA, and internal evaluations.
A feedback-aware prompt optimization framework automates generation of high-quality job postings through iterative human-in-the-loop refinement.
The framework integrates job metadata, competencies, compliance guidelines, and organization brand statements as data sources for prompt generation.
Human feedback is incorporated to continuously improve prompt quality via multi-LLM validation.
The framework is evaluated using an LLM-as-a-judge on a large collection of job postings and human evaluation on a subset across standardization, compliance, and user perception dimensions.
Evaluation demonstrates high compliance rates and strong satisfaction scores, validating the effectiveness of the feedback-aware approach for enterprise job posting generation.
PECRS formulates conversational recommender systems as a natural language processing task by representing items in natural language.
PECRS encodes items and understands user intent using a pre-trained language model.
PECRS performs item recommendation through semantic matching within the same model.
PECRS can be optimized in a single training stage without external knowledge graphs or non-textual metadata.
PECRS achieves effective recommendation and conversation performance on the ReDial and INSPIRED benchmark datasets.
TaxMorph uses large language models to transform entire taxonomies through operations such as renaming, merging, splitting, and reordering.
TaxMorph revises the full hierarchy to better match the semantics encoded by language models.
LLM-refined taxonomies consistently outperform human-curated taxonomies on hierarchical text classification benchmarks.
Human-curated taxonomies produce more easily separable clusters in embedding space than LLM-refined taxonomies.
LLM-refined taxonomies align more closely with language models' actual confusion patterns during classification.
Trove is an open-source retrieval toolkit that simplifies research experiments while preserving flexibility and speed.
Trove provides efficient data management features that load, filter, select, transform, and combine retrieval datasets on the fly.
Trove’s data management reduces memory consumption.
Trove offers a low-code unified pipeline for evaluation and hard negative mining that supports multi-node execution without code changes.
Trove’s inference pipeline incurs no overhead and inference time decreases linearly with the number of nodes.
Trove allows users to modify or replace existing components with user-defined objects, enabling arbitrary customizations.

A hybrid pipeline that combines a high-recall RoBERTa classifier trained with a precision–recall surrogate and a controlled instruction-tuned LLM performs suggestion extraction, categorization, clustering, and summarization from unstructured customer reviews.
The hybrid pipeline achieves higher extraction accuracy than prompt-only, rule-based, and classifier-only baselines.
The hybrid pipeline yields more coherent clusters than prompt-only, rule-based, and classifier-only baselines.
Human evaluations indicate that the suggestions and summaries produced by the hybrid pipeline are clear, faithful, and interpretable.
Hybrid reasoning architectures provide meaningful improvements for fine-grained actionable suggestion mining while exposing challenges in domain adaptation and efficient local deployment.
MEDASSESS-X is a deployment-oriented clinical question answering framework that applies alignment at inference time rather than through supervised fine-tuning.
MEDASSESS-X uses lightweight steering vectors to guide model activations toward medically consistent reasoning without updating model weights or requiring domain-specific retraining.
MEDASSESS-X stabilizes clinical question answering performance across both general-purpose and specialized medical large language models.
MEDASSESS-X improves accuracy and factual consistency while reducing safety error rates in clinical question answering.
A MAPE-driven data flywheel was implemented in NVInfo AI, a Mixture-of-Experts Knowledge Assistant serving enterprise users.
The data flywheel creates a closed-loop system that systematically addresses failures in retrieval-augmented generation pipelines and enables continuous learning.
Analysis of user feedback identified routing errors and query rephrasal errors as the primary failure modes in the RAG pipeline.
Fine-tuning a smaller 8B model to replace a Llama 3.1 70B model achieved high accuracy while reducing model size tenfold and improving latency.
Fine-tuning for query rephrasal improved accuracy and reduced latency in the RAG pipeline.
Human-in-the-loop feedback structured within the data flywheel transforms enterprise AI agents into self-improving systems.
SDialog unifies persona-driven multi-agent simulation with composable orchestration for controlled synthetic dialog generation.
SDialog offers multi-layer evaluation that combines linguistic metrics, LLM-as-a-judge assessments, and functional correctness validators.
SDialog includes mechanistic interpretability tools for activation inspection and causal behavior steering via feature ablation and induction.
SDialog provides audio rendering with full acoustic simulation, including 3D room modeling and microphone effects.
SDialog integrates with major LLM backends through a consistent API, supporting mixed-backend and reproducible experiments.
ScriptMind integrates automated reasoning and human cognition for LLM-based scam detection.
Crime Script Inference Task (CSIT) provides a task for scam reasoning.
Crime Script–Aware Inference Dataset (CSID) supplies structured training instances for fine-tuning small LLMs on scam detection.
Cognitive Simulation-based Evaluation of Social Engineering Defense (CSED) assesses real-time cognitive impact of scam detection systems.
An 11B small LLM fine-tuned with ScriptMind outperforms GPT-4o in detection accuracy, false-positive reduction, scammer utterance prediction, and rationale quality.
Phone scam simulation experiments show that the fine-tuned model significantly enhances and sustains users' suspicion levels, improving cognitive awareness of scams.
ReflectiveRAG is a lightweight reasoning-driven architecture for retrieval-augmented generation that improves factual grounding.

Self-Reflective Retrieval employs a small language model as a decision controller to iteratively evaluate evidence sufficiency and adaptively reformulate queries without fixed schedules or policy training.
Contrastive Noise Removal refines retrieved passages using embedding-based contrastive filtering to enforce semantic sparsity and remove redundant or tangential content.
ReflectiveRAG improves exact match and F1 performance on WebQuestions, HotpotQA (distractor setting), and InternalQA with common crawl distractors compared to strong baselines.

The combination of Self-Reflective Retrieval and Contrastive Noise Removal reduces evidence redundancy while enhancing factual accuracy and efficiency, outperforming large-scale policy optimization approaches in retrieval-augmented generation.
KV Pareto maps the trade-off frontier between total memory consumption and task accuracy for KV cache quantization, chunked prefill, and model weight quantization.
KV Pareto evaluates multiple LLM architectures (Qwen, Llama, Mistral) across various KV quantization schemes and 4-bit weight quantization.
KV Pareto identifies model-specific Pareto-optimal configurations that substantially reduce total memory usage while incurring only small accuracy loss on long-context tasks.
KV Pareto validates the identified configurations on benchmarks such as Needle-in-a-Haystack, GSM8k, MMLU, and extended context lengths up to 128k.
A general method for constructing benchmarks to assess KG-RAG under knowledge incompleteness is introduced.
BRINK benchmark systematically assesses KG-RAG methods under incomplete knowledge.
Current KG-RAG methods have limited reasoning ability when knowledge is missing.
Current KG-RAG methods often rely on internal memorization.
Current KG-RAG methods exhibit varying degrees of generalization depending on their design.
Target language typology drives translation quality of both NLLB-200 and Tower+ models, independent of data resourcedness and writing script.

Languages with certain typological properties benefit more from a wider search of the output space, indicating that alternative decoding strategies beyond standard left-to-right beam search could improve translation.
A fine-grained typological property dataset for 212 languages of the FLORES+ MT evaluation benchmark is released.
XPLAINSIM unifies three complementary approaches for explaining textual similarity.
XPLAINSIM includes a token attribution method that explains how individual word interactions contribute to the predicted similarity of any embedding model.
XPLAINSIM includes a method for inferring structured neural embedding spaces that capture explainable aspects of text.
XPLAINSIM includes a symbolic approach that explains textual similarity transparently through parsed meaning representations.
Interpretability methods are evaluated for constructing cross-lingual token alignments.
Modern information retrieval methods are investigated for handling stop words.
Empirical analysis provides insight into the distinction between relatedness and similarity in computational linguistics.
ClinicalTrialsHub consolidates data from ClinicalTrials.gov and augments it with automatically extracted trial-relevant information from PubMed research articles.
ClinicalTrialsHub automatically parses full-text research articles to extract structured trial information.
ClinicalTrialsHub translates user queries into structured database searches.
ClinicalTrialsHub provides an attributed question-answering system that generates evidence‑grounded answers linked to specific source sentences.
ClinicalTrialsHub significantly increases access to structured clinical trial data compared to relying solely on ClinicalTrials.gov.
ClinicalTrialsHub demonstrates its utility through a user study involving clinicians, clinical researchers, and PhD students in pharmaceutical sciences and nursing.
ClinicalTrialsHub undergoes systematic automatic evaluation of its information extraction and question‑answering capabilities.
ELO performs continual pretraining by detaching and training only the first and last layers of a multilingual large language model with the target language.
Detaching only the first and last layers reduces the number of trainable parameters and forward-pass computation, thereby lowering GPU memory usage and accelerating training.
After training, the newly trained layers are reintegrated and briefly fine-tuned on a small dataset to align parameters.
ELO achieves a significant training speedup compared to existing continual pretraining methods.
ELO improves target language performance while preserving source language (English) capabilities.
SELENE unifies Selective Debate Initiation and Evidence Weighted Self-Consistency for adaptive debate-on-demand reasoning.

Selective Debate Initiation predicts when debate is necessary by detecting confidence-likelihood misalignment and semantic disagreement, allowing well-aligned queries to skip debate and conserve computation.
Evidence Weighted Self-Consistency aggregates paraphrased evaluations with variance-aware, evidence-weighted voting, replacing a single-judge verdict and yielding more stable factual judgments.
The combined SELENE framework reduces token consumption substantially while improving accuracy and calibration.
Evaluations on BoolQ, CosmosQA, and an internal QnA benchmark show higher factual robustness and efficiency of SELENE, demonstrating practical scalability for real-world LLM deployments.
PortOldBERT is a historical Portuguese encoder language model.
PortOldBERT outperforms Albertina on historical data across multiple tasks.
PortOldBERT integrates historical linguistic contexts while retaining the ability to process contemporary text.
PortOldBERT is evaluated on pseudo-perplexity, named-entity recognition, part-of-speech tagging, word error rate prediction, and OCR error detection across different historical periods.
A taxonomy of error types that can occur in realistic retrieval-augmented generation systems is introduced.
A dataset of erroneous retrieval-augmented generation responses annotated with error types is curated.
An auto-evaluation method aligned with the error taxonomy for tracking and addressing errors in retrieval-augmented generation systems is proposed.
Practical advice for addressing identified error types in retrieval-augmented generation systems is provided.
A structured approach for automated novelty evaluation models expert reviewer behavior through three stages: content extraction, retrieval and synthesis of related work, and structured comparison.
The approach captures key patterns of novelty reviews such as independent claim verification and contextual reasoning.
Evaluation on ICLR 2025 submissions shows the approach aligns with human reasoning and agrees on novelty conclusions more than existing LLM-based baselines.
The approach produces detailed literature-aware analysis and improves consistency over ad hoc reviewer judgments.
A post-training adaptation strategy selectively replaces low-utility BPE tokens with more relevant tokens based on their frequency in an adaptation corpus.
An algorithm identifies the token inventory that most effectively encodes an adaptation corpus for a specified target vocabulary size.
Adapted tokenizers compress test corpora more effectively than baseline tokenizers with the same vocabulary size across multiple languages.
The adaptation method serves as a lightweight vocabulary fine-tuning mechanism for optimizing tokenization for specific domains or tasks.
A systematic analysis of poisoning risks in LLM-based prompt optimization is presented.
LLM-based prompt optimization systems are substantially more vulnerable to manipulated feedback than to query poisoning alone.
A simple fake reward attack that requires no access to the reward model is introduced, significantly increasing vulnerability of LLM-based prompt optimizers.
A lightweight highlighting defense reduces the fake reward attack success rate without degrading utility.
Prompt optimization pipelines constitute a first-class attack surface for LLM systems.
Explicit assumptions underlying intrinsic evaluation metrics for conditional language models in multilingual settings are identified and discussed.
Six intrinsic evaluation metrics are experimentally evaluated on two multi-parallel corpora using both monolingual and multilingual models.
Current intrinsic evaluation metrics are not universally comparable across multilingual setups.
The form-meaning debate is used to explain the lack of universal comparability of current intrinsic evaluation metrics.
A self-refinement strategy using large language models generates synthetic dialogue data that is more relevant to expert domains for meta-reviewing.
Synthetic data produced by the self-refinement strategy improves quality for training meta-reviewing assistants.
Dialogue agents trained on the self-refinement synthetic data outperform generic large language model assistants in meta-reviewing tasks.
Trained dialogue agents enhance the efficiency of meta-reviewing in real-world scenarios.
Activation steering modulates internal representations towards a cross-lingual reasoning space.
Activation steering techniques constructed for English syllogisms generalize effectively to multilingual syllogistic reasoning datasets.
Activation steering improves formal reasoning accuracy on multilingual datasets while minimally affecting language modeling performance.
Activation steering supports partial transfer to out-of-distribution reasoning tasks.
Ranke quantifies the effect of vision encoder prior knowledge on multi-modal large language model performance.
A positive correlation exists between vision encoder prior knowledge and multi-modal large language model performance.
Domain-specific fine-tuning using only end-to-end visual question answering data is insufficient for entities with low inherent visual prior knowledge.
VisPRE is a two-stage training framework that explicitly incorporates prior knowledge at the vision encoder level.
Augmenting the vision encoder’s prior knowledge substantially boosts the visual understanding capabilities of multi-modal large language models, especially for uncommon visual entities.
A model that predicts human gaze patterns is used to steer language model outputs toward desired reading behaviors.
The method generates texts with controllable reading ease.
Eye-tracking experiments with native and non-native English speakers demonstrate that the method can modulate reading ease of generated texts, as reflected in reading times and perceived difficulty.
Statistical analysis indicates that the modulation of reading behavior is primarily driven by lexical processing features.
ARQA is a benchmark for grounded table–text question answering in enterprise annual reports.
ARQA contains approximately 2.5K question-answer pairs spanning ten fiscal years of automotive enterprise annual reports and includes three reasoning families: Lookup, Arithmetic, and Insight.
ARQA data are generated using a planner–generator pipeline, deterministically verified and recomputed, and fully reviewed by domain experts.
State-of-the-art instruction-tuned language models achieve strong factual retrieval on ARQA but exhibit persistent weaknesses in grounded arithmetic and causal reasoning.
ARQA and its evaluation toolkit are released to facilitate research on auditable, evidence-first reasoning over enterprise documents.
HalluZig detects hallucinations in large language models by analyzing the dynamic topology of layer-wise attention evolution.
HalluZig models sequences of attention matrices as a zigzag graph filtration.
HalluZig extracts topological signatures from attention dynamics using zigzag persistence.
HalluZig outperforms strong baselines on multiple hallucination detection benchmarks.
Topological signatures identified by HalluZig generalize across different large language models and enable early detection of hallucinations.
Linguistic reformulations significantly affect both retrieval and generation stages of retrieval-augmented generation systems.
Less formal queries cause substantial reductions in retrieval recall for retrieval-augmented generation systems.
Queries containing grammatical errors cause notable decreases in answer match quality for retrieval-augmented generation systems.
Retrieval-augmented generation systems exhibit greater sensitivity to linguistic variations than language-model-only generation.
A lightweight (<10M parameter) privacy-preserving transformer performs entity extraction and entity linking in French medical prescriptions using only OCR text and normalized 2D word coordinates.
The model is pretrained on a large corpus of pseudonymised OCR outputs with prescription-structure-tailored objectives, including a novel Token-to-Line Alignment task.
Fine-tuning on the Rx-PAD dataset enables the model to achieve extraction performance comparable to or exceeding that of larger document-understanding models and multimodal large language models.
The model achieves sub-second CPU-level inference latency suitable for real-time deployment in pharmacy settings.
The system has been deployed in hundreds of pharmacies, demonstrating scalability and practical relevance for prescription verification.
PALETTE edits LLM personality by using adjustment queries that treat self-referential statements grounded in psychological constructs as factual knowledge.
PALETTE achieves substantial improvements in personality alignment across dimensions with only a few editing samples.
Automatic and human evaluations show that PALETTE provides more stable and well-balanced personality control in LLMs.
MobileCity is a lightweight generative-agent framework for city-scale simulation powered by cognitively-grounded generative agents.
MobileCity initializes agents from survey-based demographic data and models their behavior based on evolving needs, habits, and obligations.
MobileCity enables agents to navigate a realistic multimodal transportation network spanning multiple vehicle types.
MobileCity introduces asynchronous batched LLM inference combined with a low-token communication mechanism to improve scalability and computational efficiency.
MobileCity generates more human-like urban dynamics than baselines while maintaining high computational efficiency.
PROBES is a multi-task system powered by large language models for end-to-end evaluation of semantic search systems.

PROBES identifies context-aware relevance using a fine-grained scale (exact, substitute, complement, irrelevant) that incorporates query category, feature-level intent, and category-aware feature importance.
PROBES provides differentiated relevance assessment across diverse query categories.
PROBES diagnoses precision issues by checking product content conflicts and inaccuracies.
PROBES diagnoses missed recall by leveraging retrieval and relevance models to determine whether a missed recall stems from a selection issue or a ranking/retrieval system issue.
PROBES introduces the Actionable Error Rate metric to measure the proportion of actionable errors among flagged errors.
PROBES achieves a high actionable error rate, generating actionable insights across many product categories.
A proxy-based test-time alignment framework uses guidance from a small aligned model to align large language models at inference time.
The framework implements a token-specific cascading method where the deferral decision is formulated as a 0-1 knapsack problem.
Primal and dual approximation algorithms are derived for the optimal deferral decision in the knapsack formulation.
The method improves task performance compared to baseline alignment procedures.
The method accelerates speculative decoding speed.

Indic Jailbreak Robustness (IJR) provides a judge-free benchmark for evaluating adversarial safety of large language models in twelve Indic and South Asian languages across JSON (contract-bound) and Free (naturalistic) tracks.
IJR demonstrates that contract-bound prompts inflate refusal rates but do not prevent jailbreaks, with models achieving high jailbreak success rates in both JSON and Free tracks.
IJR shows that English-to-Indic jailbreak attacks transfer strongly, and format wrappers often outperform instruction wrappers.
IJR reveals that romanized or mixed orthography inputs reduce jailbreak success rates under JSON prompts, with systematic correlations to the proportion of romanization and tokenization behavior.
Human audits confirm the reliability of IJR's detection methodology, and lightweight evaluations preserve the same conclusions as full evaluations.
SymPyBench is a large-scale synthetic benchmark of university-level physics problems.
Problems in SymPyBench are fully parameterized, allowing an effectively infinite range of input configurations.
Each problem in SymPyBench includes structured step-by-step reasoning and executable Python code that generates the ground-truth solution.
SymPyBench comprises three question types: multiple-choice symbolic, multiple-choice numerical, and free-form open-ended responses.
SymPyBench introduces three metrics—Consistency Score, Failure Rate, and Confusion Rate—to quantify variability and uncertainty across problem variants.
State-of-the-art instruction-tuned language models display both strengths and limitations in scientific reasoning on SymPyBench.
A novel adversarial paraphrasing task is defined, requiring grammatically correct paraphrases that preserve query meaning while degrading segmentation performance.
A comprehensive automatic evaluation protocol for adversarial paraphrases is developed and validated with human studies.
SPARTA is a black-box sentence-level optimization method that operates in the low-dimensional semantic latent space of a text autoencoder and is guided by reinforcement learning.
SPARTA achieves higher success rates than prior methods on ReasonSeg and LLMSeg-40k datasets.
Advanced reasoning segmentation models remain vulnerable to adversarial paraphrasing even under strict semantic and grammatical constraints.
Vision-language models achieve higher alignment with human neural representations of concepts than language-only models in both visual and textual context conditions.
For LXMERT and IDEFICS2, brain alignment stems from learning more human-like concepts during pretraining.
For other vision-language models, brain alignment is highly sensitive to the visual or textual context provided at inference.
Vision-language encoders are more brain-aligned than recent generative vision-language models.
An evaluation framework built on hierarchical concept indices and novel metrics measures generalization in mention-agnostic biomedical concept recognition.
A task-specific pipeline generates LLM-based auto-labeled data for biomedical concept recognition.
LLM-generated auto-labeled data improves generalization of models for unseen biomedical concepts, though it does not fully replace manual annotations.
MALINT is a human-annotated English corpus that captures disinformation and its malicious intent.
MALINT enables benchmarking of twelve language models on binary and multilabel intent classification tasks.
Intent-based inoculation integrates intent analysis into LLM reasoning to mitigate the persuasive impact of disinformation.
Intent-augmented reasoning improves zero-shot disinformation detection across multiple datasets, models, and languages.
The MALINT dataset is released with annotations from each annotation step.
FAIDSet is a multilingual, multi-domain, multi-generator dataset for fine-grained AI-generated text detection.
FAID classifies text into human-written, LLM-generated, and human-LLM collaborative categories and identifies the LLM family of the generator.
FAID combines multi-level contrastive learning with multi-task auxiliary classification to learn subtle stylistic cues.
FAID models LLM families as distinct stylistic entities and incorporates adaptation to address distributional shifts without retraining for unseen data.
FAID outperforms several baselines in generalization accuracy on unseen domains and new LLMs.
Culture-sensitive neurons exist in vision-language models, showing preferential activation to inputs associated with particular cultural contexts.
Deactivating identified culture-sensitive neurons disproportionately harms performance on visual question answering for the corresponding cultures while having limited impact on other cultures.
Contrastive Activation Margin (ConAct) is a margin-based selector for identifying culture-selective neurons.
Contrastive Activation Margin outperforms probability-based and entropy-based methods in identifying culture-selective neurons.
Culture-sensitive neurons are not uniformly distributed across model layers but cluster in specific decoder layers, with the clustering pattern varying across models.
The CVQA benchmark can be used to identify culture-selective neurons in vision-language models.
Context-Enriched Agentic RAG (CARAG) is a retrieval-augmented framework that uses a team of cooperative LLM agents specialized in distinct analytical tasks for post-earnings price shock prediction.
CARAG agents retrieve structured evidence from a Causal-Temporal Knowledge Graph constructed from financial statements and earnings calls to enable grounded, context-rich reasoning.
The retrieval-augmented design of CARAG mitigates LLM hallucinations and yields more objective predictions for large stock price movements after earnings announcements.
CARAG achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on NASDAQ, NYSE, and MAEC datasets, surpassing larger LLMs and fine-tuned models on macro-F1, MCC, and Sharpe metrics.
CARAG’s predictions outperform market benchmarks such as the S&P 500 and Nasdaq for the same forecasting horizon.
MULSUM generates visually informed text summaries by conditioning a pre-trained LLM decoder with image-level embeddings projected into a shared space via a Cross-Vis Aligner.
MULSUM selects a compact set of images that are relevant to the generated summary while enforcing pairwise diversity using a Diversity-Aware Image Selector.
MULSUM outperforms strong baselines on automatic summarization metrics such as ROUGE on the MSMO benchmark.
Human evaluation indicates that the diverse set of images selected by MULSUM is more helpful than images selected solely based on similarity.
Qualitative analysis shows that images selected by MULSUM serve as explanatory evidence for the summary rather than ornamental add-ons.
FI (First order local Influence) is a stability measure rooted in information geometry.
FI quantifies the sensitivity of individual parameter and input dimensions in large language models and vision-language models.
A small subset of parameters or input dimensions with high FI values disproportionately contributes to model brittleness in large language models and vision-language models.
Mitigating the influence of vulnerable parameters during model merging improves performance of large language models and vision-language models.
BabyBabelLM provides a multilingual collection of datasets that model language exposure from birth to native acquisition.
BabyBabelLM curates developmentally plausible pretraining data covering roughly 100 million English-word equivalents for each of 45 languages.
BabyBabelLM includes evaluation suites for each of the 45 languages.
Baseline models are trained on the curated pretraining data for each language in BabyBabelLM.
MathEDU provides a dataset of student mathematical problem-solving processes together with teacher-written feedback.
Fine-tuning strategies enhance model accuracy in classifying answer correctness.
Fine-tuning strategies enhance model ability to identify erroneous steps in problem-solving processes.
Generated feedback from evaluated models is verbose and fails to deliver targeted explanations of student misconceptions, showing a substantial gap from teacher-written feedback.
A systematic evaluation of model reliability across answer correctness classification, error identification, and feedback generation is performed.
MetaLead provides a fully human-annotated machine learning leaderboard dataset that captures all experimental results.
MetaLead includes metadata indicating the experimental type of each result (baseline, proposed method, or variation of a proposed method).
MetaLead explicitly separates train and test datasets to support cross-domain assessment.
MetaLead enables more transparent and nuanced evaluations across machine learning research.
A novel taxonomy covering user granularity and simulation objectives for LLM-based conversational user simulation is introduced.
Core techniques and evaluation methodologies for LLM-based conversational user simulation are systematically analyzed.
Recent advancements in LLM-based conversational user simulation are surveyed.
A model automatically discovers fine-grained contextual word patterns that correlate with gender-based disagreement in politeness annotations.
Application of the model to a politeness-annotated email corpus shows that female annotators rate emails containing formal cues as more polite than male annotators.
Application of the model to a politeness-annotated email corpus shows that male annotators rate emails containing informal cues as more polite than female annotators.
Argument Representation Coverage (ARC) is a bottom-up evaluation framework that assesses how well summaries preserve structured salient arguments.
ARC distinguishes between different information types to be covered and separates omissions from factual errors.
ARC evaluates summaries from eight open-weight large language models on long legal opinions and scientific articles.
Summaries generated by open-weight large language models frequently omit critical information, especially when arguments are sparsely distributed across the input.
ARC reveals systematic patterns such as context window positional bias and role-specific preferences that shape argument coverage in summaries.

MAViS is an end-to-end multi-agent collaborative framework that orchestrates specialized agents for script writing, shot designing, character modeling, keyframe generation, video animation, and audio generation in long-sequence video storytelling.
MAViS applies a 3E Principle—Explore, Examine, Enhance—in each stage to ensure completeness of intermediate outputs.
MAViS introduces Script Writing Guidelines to improve compatibility between scripts and generative tools.
MAViS attains state-of-the-art performance in assistive capability, visual quality, and video expressiveness for long-sequence video generation.
MAViS’ modular architecture enables scalability with diverse generative models and tools.
MAViS can produce multimodal design output comprising videos with narratives and background music from a brief prompt.
A dataset for processing structured JSON tool responses is introduced.
Fifteen open- and closed-weight language models are evaluated on the tool response processing task with multiple prompting approaches.
JSON processing remains difficult for frontier language models across multiple prompting strategies.
The optimal response processing strategy depends on the nature and size of tool outputs and the complexity of required reasoning.
Variations in processing approaches can lead to substantial performance differences.
Gradient-aligned Sparse Tuning (GAST) performs selective fine-tuning simultaneously across data points and model layers within a unified optimization strategy.
GAST employs a layer-sparse strategy that adaptively selects the most impactful data points for each layer.
GAST targets redundancy in information during parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large language models.
Experiments demonstrate that GAST consistently outperforms baseline parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods.
Learning agentic policies models large language model behavior as a decision-making problem for sycophancy control.
The framework equips a single model with an explicit action space that includes answering directly, countering misleading signals, and asking for clarification.
The policy is trained with a multi-objective reward that balances task success, sycophancy resistance, and behavioral consistency.
Evaluation on multiple benchmarks shows reduction of sycophancy, improvement in performance, and robust generalization across languages.
Mitigating sycophancy requires moving beyond compliance-oriented generation toward agreement-agentic control.
Test-time scaling is evaluated on twelve reasoning models across a diverse suite of machine translation benchmarks covering multiple domains.
Direct translation with general-purpose reasoning models shows limited and inconsistent benefits from test-time scaling, with performance quickly plateauing.
Domain-specific fine-tuning enables test-time scaling to achieve consistent improvements up to an optimal, self-determined reasoning depth.
Forcing a reasoning model to continue reasoning beyond its natural stopping point consistently degrades translation quality.
Test-time scaling is highly effective in a post-editing context, reliably converting self-correction into a beneficial process.
Early prefix tokens of autoregressive audio generators implicitly encode global semantic attributes of the final output, such as event count and sound-object category.
Plan-Critic is a lightweight auxiliary model trained with a Generalized Advantage Estimation-inspired objective to predict final instruction-following quality from partial generations.
During inference, Plan-Critic evaluates candidate prefixes early, prunes low-fidelity trajectories, and reallocates computation to high-potential planning seeds.

Plan-Critic-guided sampling improves CLAP scores over the autoregressive baseline, establishing a new state of the art in autoregressive text-to-audio generation while maintaining computational parity with standard best-of-N decoding.
Autoregressive models are capable of planning ahead to achieve global semantic alignment with complex textual prompts.
NeuronMoE analyzes language-specific neurons across all transformer components to guide per-layer expert allocation based on measured cross-lingual neuron diversity.
NeuronMoE achieves substantial parameter reduction while matching the performance of the LayerMoE baseline on low-resource languages.
Low-resource language experts independently develop neuron specialization patterns that mirror those of high-resource languages, primarily in early and late layers.
The observed specialization patterns suggest universal architectural principles for organizing linguistic knowledge in multilingual models.
The expert allocation strategy influences performance more than the total number of experts, and the method generalizes to other architectures such as Qwen.
Prototype-Based Regularization learns soft prototypes through a distance-based auxiliary loss to structure the latent space for rhetorical role labeling.
Prototype-Conditioned Modulation constructs corpus-level prototypes and injects them during training and inference for rhetorical role labeling.
The two prototype-based methods integrate local context with global representations in a hierarchical architecture for rhetorical role labeling.
SCOTUS-Law is a dataset of U.S. Supreme Court opinions annotated with rhetorical roles at three levels of granularity: category, rhetorical function, and step.
Experiments on legal, medical, and scientific benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements over strong baselines, particularly on low-frequency rhetorical roles.
An analysis of the methods’ implications in the era of large language models is complemented by expert evaluation.
Controlled experiments that re-initialize subsets of layers with random weights before fine-tuning are used to assess transfer of pretrained representations to structured data tasks.
For structured data tasks, performance saturates after the first few pretrained layers, indicating limited benefit from deeper pretrained depth.
Unstructured tasks benefit more consistently from deeper pretrained representations compared to structured data tasks.
Pretraining provides notable advantages primarily in low-resource settings for structured data, with its impact diminishing as training data size increases.
The Multilingual European Value Survey (MEVS) is a corpus of human-translated survey questions aligned in eight European languages.
A subset of MEVS questions was administered to many multilingual large language models under comprehensive controlled prompt variations, including answer order, symbol type, and tail character.
Larger instruction-tuned multilingual models exhibit higher overall consistency in value-laden multiple-choice question responses.
Response robustness varies across questions, with some multiple-choice questions achieving total agreement among models and others resulting in split answers.
Language-specific behavior emerges in consistent instruction-fine-tuned models on certain questions, suggesting a selective effect of preference fine-tuning.
Causal tracing is employed to systematically analyze how pretrained causal transformers handle idiom ambiguity.
Early sublayers and specific attention heads retrieve figurative interpretations while suppressing literal interpretations of idioms.

When disambiguating context precedes an idiom, the model leverages the context from the earliest layer and later layers refine the interpretation if the context conflicts with the retrieved interpretation.

Selective competing pathways carry both figurative and literal interpretations, with an intermediate pathway prioritizing figurative meaning and a parallel direct route favoring literal meaning, keeping both readings available.
Mechanistic evidence for idiom comprehension in autoregressive transformers is provided.
Visual faithfulness of reasoning chains is defined as a distinct evaluation dimension that assesses whether perception steps are grounded in the image.
A training- and reference-free framework decomposes reasoning chains into perception and reasoning steps and uses off-the-shelf vision-language model judges to evaluate step-level faithfulness.
Human meta-evaluation validates the step-level faithfulness assessment framework.
A lightweight self-reflection procedure detects and locally regenerates unfaithful perception steps without any training.
The self-reflection procedure reduces the rate of unfaithful perception steps while preserving final-answer accuracy in reasoning-trained vision-language models.
A unified view for understanding emotion representation in large language models is introduced, encompassing experiments with diverse datasets and prompts.
Large language models possess a common emotion representation in their later layers.
Emotion direction vectors derived from later-layer representations can be interchanged across datasets with minimal performance degradation.
Chain-of-Thought prompting analysis demonstrates that large language models have limited reasoning ability on complex emotion identification tasks.
A framework automatically generates intention knowledge graphs that capture connections between user intentions.
The constructed intention graph exhibits high plausibility and acceptance.
The model predicts new session intentions.
The model enhances product recommendations.
The model outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods in intention prediction and recommendation tasks.
Martingale Foresight Sampling reformulates large language model decoding as identifying an optimal stochastic process.
Martingale Foresight Sampling derives step valuation from the Doob Decomposition Theorem to measure a reasoning path’s predictable advantage.
Martingale Foresight Sampling uses Optional Stopping Theory for principled pruning of suboptimal candidate paths.
Martingale Foresight Sampling employs an adaptive stopping rule based on the Martingale Convergence Theorem to terminate exploration when a path’s quality has provably converged.
Martingale Foresight Sampling surpasses state-of-the-art methods in accuracy on six reasoning benchmarks.
Martingale Foresight Sampling improves computational efficiency relative to state-of-the-art methods on six reasoning benchmarks.
Prompt-based use of pre-trained large language models can detect offensive language in Urdu and Roman Urdu.
Llama variants, Qwen, Lughaat, and GPT-4 are evaluated with prompting strategies for offensive language detection in under-resourced languages.
Pre-trained large language models achieve performance comparable to traditional fine-tuned benchmarks for hateful and offensive content detection.
AfriMTEB expands MMTEB to include 59 African languages, 14 tasks, and 38 datasets, adding six new datasets.
AfriMTEB introduces new tasks for African languages, including hate speech detection, intent detection, and emotion classification.
AfriE5 adapts the instruction-tuned mE5 model to African languages using cross-lingual contrastive distillation.
AfriE5 achieves state-of-the-art performance on African language text embedding benchmarks, outperforming Gemini-Embeddings and mE5.

ToxiPrompt is a two-stage red-teaming framework that separates exploration for prompt diversity from exploitation for toxicity and reunifies them with a single selection criterion to balance diversity and toxicity.
ToxiPrompt achieves higher adversarial prompt diversity and higher elicited toxicity than four state-of-the-art baselines.
ToxiPrompt improves the harmonic mean of toxicity and diversity relative to the best baseline.
ToxiPrompt generalizes to multiple instruction-tuned target language models without re-tuning, delivering substantial improvement in the harmonic mean of toxicity and diversity.
A comprehensive evaluation of how weight and activation quantization affect bias in large language models across stereotypes, fairness, toxicity, and sentiment.
Probability- and generated text-based metrics are applied across 13 benchmarks to measure bias under quantization.
Quantization reduces model toxicity without significantly impacting sentiment.
Quantization slightly increases stereotypes and unfairness in generative tasks, particularly under aggressive compression.
Bias trends induced by quantization are generally consistent across demographic subgroups and model architecture families, with effect magnitude varying by setting.
A comprehensive evaluation of conditional acceptability judgments by large language models across multiple model families, sizes, and prompting strategies is conducted.
Linear mixed-effects models and ANOVA tests are employed to assess the influence of conditional probability and semantic relevance on large language models' acceptability judgments.

Large language models exhibit sensitivity to conditional probability and semantic relevance when judging conditional acceptability, with the degree of sensitivity varying by architecture and prompting style.
Large language models incorporate probabilistic and semantic cues less consistently than humans in conditional acceptability judgments.
Model scale does not guarantee closer alignment with human conditional acceptability judgments.
AfriVox is an open-source benchmark that includes novel domain-specific and unscripted datasets covering 20 African languages, African-accented French, Arabic, and over 100 African English accents.
AfriVox enables comparison of leading multimodal speech large language models with traditional unimodal automatic speech transcription and translation models.
Analysis using AfriVox shows significant variation in language coverage among leading multimodal speech LLMs.
Analysis using AfriVox identifies robustness concerns of multimodal speech LLMs with unscripted speech.

Targeted fine-tuning of the speech LLM Qwen2.5-Omni on three Nigerian languages achieves state-of-the-art performance, reducing word error rate relative to prior models and improving translation BLEU scores.
HPTSA is a system of LLM agents that includes a planning agent capable of launching subagents.
The planning agent in HPTSA explores the system and determines which subagents to call, addressing long-term planning challenges in vulnerability exploitation.
A benchmark comprising 14 real-world zero-day vulnerabilities is constructed.
Teams of LLM agents can exploit real-world zero-day vulnerabilities.
Teams of LLM agents using HPTSA achieve higher exploitation success than prior agent frameworks.
FormGym provides a benchmark for evaluating end-to-end form filling accuracy on document-style forms.
FormGym is built by repurposing three existing datasets and adding a new dataset to create challenging, diverse, realistic test cases.
Baseline vision-language agents perform poorly on FormGym across scenarios due to inadequate field localization.
GUI agents achieve higher performance on FormGym but incur high latency and costs.
FieldFinder is a field localization tool that enables zero-shot vision-language agents to locate input fields and accurately place text.
Vision-language agents augmented with FieldFinder achieve better performance on FormGym than baseline vision-language agents.
TempViz is a dataset for holistically evaluating temporal knowledge in text-to-image generation, comprising thousands of prompts and hundreds of reference images.
Human evaluation of five text-to-image models across five temporal knowledge categories reveals generally weak temporal competence, with no model achieving high accuracy.
Comparison of established automated evaluation methods against human judgments shows that none reliably assess temporal cues in generated images.

LAILA is the largest publicly available Arabic AES dataset, comprising thousands of essays annotated with holistic and trait-specific scores on seven dimensions: relevance, organization, vocabulary, style, development, mechanics, and grammar.
Benchmark evaluations on LAILA are provided using state-of-the-art Arabic and English models in prompt-specific and cross-prompt settings.
LAILA enables development of robust Arabic automated essay scoring systems.
A paradigm leverages large language models to infer global reading order and inter-element layout relations without human supervision.
Incorporating reading order as structural guidance captures hierarchical, document-level dependencies beyond local adjacency.

LLM-inferred reading order provides richer global structural priors and yields superior downstream performance on semantic entity recognition, entity linking, and document question answering, even when differing from ground-truth adjacency.
Experiments show consistent improvements over baseline methods on semantic entity recognition, entity linking, and document question answering.
The paradigm demonstrates scalability and advances both large language models and lightweight layout-aware models for robust document understanding.
ToxiGAN is a class-aware text augmentation framework that combines adversarial generation with semantic guidance from large language models.
ToxiGAN introduces a two-step directional training strategy to mitigate mode collapse and semantic drift in GAN-based augmentation.
ToxiGAN leverages large language model‑generated neutral texts as semantic ballast to provide balanced guidance during augmentation.
ToxiGAN dynamically selects neutral exemplars rather than using static large language model generators.
ToxiGAN explicitly optimizes toxic samples to diverge from neutral exemplars, creating class‑specific contrastive signals.
ToxiGAN achieves the strongest average performance across multiple hate‑speech benchmarks, outperforming traditional and large language model‑based augmentation methods.
Ablation and sensitivity analyses confirm that semantic ballast and directional training enhance classifier robustness.
Amory constructs structured memory representations through agentic reasoning during offline time.
Amory organizes conversational fragments into episodic narratives.
Amory consolidates memories using momentum-aware consolidation.
Amory semanticizes peripheral facts into a semantic memory store.
Amory retrieves information by applying coherence-driven reasoning over narrative structures.
Evaluation on the LOCOMO benchmark shows that Amory attains performance comparable to full-context reasoning while substantially reducing response time.
Momentum-aware consolidation in Amory markedly improves response quality.
Coherence-driven retrieval in Amory provides superior memory coverage compared to embedding-based retrieval methods.
A comprehensive analysis of Surface-over-Semantics tendencies in multilingual text-to-image models is presented.
A dataset of prompts covering 171 cultural identities translated into 14 languages is created for evaluating text-to-image models.
A novel measure for quantifying Surface-over-Semantics tendencies across models, languages, and cultures is introduced.
All but one evaluated text-to-image model exhibits strong surface-level tendency in at least two languages, with the effect intensifying across layers of text encoders.
Surface-level tendencies in text-to-image models frequently correlate with stereotypical visual depictions.
MDL-based measures of regularity and processing complexity capture key differences between attested natural recursive numeral systems and theoretically possible ones.
Ad-hoc constraints previously used to exclude unnatural recursive numeral systems naturally follow from regularity as measured by the MDL-based approach.
Recursive numeral systems are efficient with respect to regularity and processing complexity.
Regularity plays an important role in explaining the shape of linguistic systems, as demonstrated through analysis of recursive numeral systems.

A framework comprising a game theory-based taxonomy of uncooperative agent behaviors and a structured multi-stage simulation pipeline that dynamically generates and refines uncooperative behaviors as agents' states evolve is introduced.
The framework is evaluated in a collaborative resource management setting, measuring system stability with metrics such as survival time and resource overuse rate.
The framework generates realistic uncooperative behaviors with high accuracy, as validated by human evaluations.
Cooperative agents maintain perfect system stability, whereas any uncooperative behavior can trigger rapid system collapse within a few rounds.
LLM-based defense methods detect some uncooperative behaviors, but many remain largely undetectable.
Discriminative Alignment Index (DALI) quantifies instance-level alignment between non-English inputs and English representations across multiple languages and natural language understanding tasks.
Lower representation alignment with English in middle layers is strongly associated with incorrect natural language understanding predictions for non-English inputs.

Patching middle-layer activations of non-English inputs with their parallel English activations corrects incorrect predictions, demonstrating a causal role of representation alignment in cross-lingual correctness.
A systematic investigation evaluates how retrieval augmentation modulates social biases in large language model generators across English, Japanese, and Chinese.
Evaluation of generator LLMs on the BBQ benchmark measures bias across gender, race, age, and religion categories.
Document collections with controlled stereotypical content are used to analyze the impact of retrieved information on generated text bias.
Biases present in retrieved documents are often amplified in the generated texts, even when the base LLM exhibits low intrinsic bias.
COREA cascades a small language model with a large language model to balance accuracy and cost in complex reasoning tasks.
COREA uses the small model’s answer and verbalized confidence score to decide whether to defer a question to the large model.
COREA incorporates a reinforcement learning training algorithm that adds a confidence calibration reward to align the small model’s confidence estimates.
COREA jointly improves the small model’s reasoning performance and confidence calibration across diverse datasets and model backbones.
COREA reduces inference cost compared to using only a large language model while maintaining comparable accuracy.
DocZSRE-SI performs document-level zero-shot relation extraction using entity side information instead of LLM-generated synthetic data.
DocZSRE-SI incorporates entity mention descriptions and entity mention hypernyms as side information for relation extraction.
DocZSRE-SI is a low-complexity model that achieves higher macro F1 performance than baseline models on zero-shot relation extraction.
DocZSRE-SI provides a robust and efficient alternative to LLM-based methods for zero-shot relation extraction in low-resource languages.
DocZSRE-SI demonstrates scalable and reliable zero-shot relation extraction for Malaysian English news articles.
MedEditBench provides a rigorous evaluation framework for assessing medical knowledge editing.
Self-Generated Rationale Editing (SGR-Edit) leverages model-generated rationales as editing targets to enable deeper knowledge integration.
SGR-Edit consistently improves editing efficacy and generalization across diverse large language models and knowledge editing methods.
Current knowledge editing approaches that directly edit simple answers often result in superficial updates with poor generalization.
Sequential edits affect in-domain medical knowledge, external-domain knowledge, and general model capabilities, providing practical insights for real-world medical applications.
AndroidBuildBench is a benchmark of 1,019 Android build failures with verified fixes curated from open-source project commit histories.
GradleFixer is an LLM agent equipped with domain-specific tools for inspecting and manipulating the Gradle build environment.
GradleFixer resolves a majority of Android build failures, outperforming a state-of-the-art coding agent that relies on a general-purpose shell.
Tool Bridging replaces general-purpose shell commands with domain-aware abstractions to improve LLM agent execution.
Tool Bridging provides API-like tools and constrains the action space, facilitating translation of high-level reasoning into effective low-level actions.
BeDiscovER provides a comprehensive suite for evaluating discourse-level knowledge of modern large language models.
BeDiscovER aggregates five discourse tasks spanning lexicon, (multi-)sentential, and documental levels, comprising 52 individual datasets.
BeDiscovER includes novel challenges such as discourse particle disambiguation and a shared task on multilingual, multi-framework discourse relation classification.
Evaluation of Qwen3 series, DeepSeek-R1, and GPT-5-mini on BeDiscovER reveals strong performance on the arithmetic aspect of temporal reasoning.
Evaluation of these models on BeDiscovER shows difficulty with long-dependency reasoning and subtle semantic and discourse phenomena, including rhetorical relation classification.
Discourse knowledge is encoded within large language models.
Quality-aware decoding, implemented as minimum Bayes risk decoding, extracts discourse knowledge from large language models.
Quality-aware decoding outperforms other decoding approaches for handling discourse phenomena in translation.
Quality-aware decoding enhances the semantic richness of translations.
Quality-aware decoding aligns translations more closely with human preferences.
Comprehensive analysis of large language models' performance on discourse phenomena in context-aware translation is provided.
Lightweight models trained on a few hundred to one thousand structured sentence completion examples learn English verb alternation rules with high accuracy.

Ablation studies indicate that analogical organization of inputs is the primary factor for sample-efficient rule learning, while contrastive distractors and minimal contextual cues provide additional benefits.
Zero- and few-shot large language models evaluated on the same tasks achieve lower performance than lightweight models given the same amount of task-specific data.
Input organization based on analogical structure, contrastive distractors, and minimal contextual cues enables sample-efficient learning of linguistic rules.
Distinct learning signatures are observed between trained lightweight models and prompted large language models in the controlled tasks.
ViGoEmotions is a Vietnamese emotion corpus of 20,664 social media comments annotated with 27 fine-grained emotions.

Eight pre-trained Transformer-based models were systematically evaluated on ViGoEmotions under three emoji preprocessing strategies: preserving original emojis with rule-based normalization, converting emojis into textual descriptions, and applying ViSoLex lexical normalization.
Converting emojis into text often improves the performance of several BERT-based models on Vietnamese emotion classification.
Preserving emojis yields the best results for ViSoBERT and CafeBERT on Vietnamese emotion classification.
Removing emojis generally leads to lower performance for Vietnamese emotion classification models.
ViSoBERT achieves the highest macro and weighted F1 scores among the evaluated models on ViGoEmotions.
A multi-task evaluation setup associates demographics with names, professions, and education levels to measure bias removal while preserving demographic detection.
Attribution-based and correlation-based methods are compared for locating bias features in language models.
Targeted sparse autoencoder feature ablations in Gemma-2-9B reduce demographic bias without degrading demographic recognition performance.
Attribution-based feature ablations mitigate race and gender profession stereotypes while preserving name recognition accuracy.
Correlation-based feature ablations are more effective for reducing education bias.
Removing attribution features in education tasks induces "prior collapse", increasing overall bias.
Demographic bias in language models arises from task-specific mechanisms rather than absolute demographic markers.
Mechanistic inference-time interventions enable surgical debiasing without compromising core model capabilities.
MHEL-LLaMo is an unsupervised ensemble approach that combines a small language model and a large language model for multilingual historical entity linking.
MHEL-LLaMo leverages a multilingual bi-encoder (BELA) to retrieve candidate entities.
MHEL-LLaMo uses an instruction-tuned large language model for NIL prediction and candidate selection via prompt chaining.

MHEL-LLaMo employs confidence scores from the small language model to distinguish easy from hard samples, applying the large language model only to hard cases, thereby reducing computational cost and preventing hallucinations on straightforward cases.
MHEL-LLaMo outperforms state-of-the-art models on historical entity linking benchmarks across six European languages without requiring fine-tuning.
Error analysis indicates that many false predictions are semantically proximate to the correct entities, demonstrating accurate disambiguation of historical references.
DITTO is a framework that spoofs watermarks on large language models via knowledge distillation.
Watermark spoofing enables a malicious model to generate text containing authentic-looking watermarks of a victim model.
Repurposing watermark radioactivity from fine-tuning allows stealing and replicating the watermarking signal of a victim model.
The assumption that a specific watermark proves authorship by a specific model is demonstrated to be flawed.
A critical security gap in text authorship verification is revealed.
An entropy-based dynamic early stopping strategy for query auto-completion in dialog systems is introduced.
Statistical n-gram models and trie-based methods achieve higher performance and faster inference than deep learning models for seen prefixes in ghosting.
Neural models such as T5 and Phi-2 achieve superior results for unseen queries in ghosting.
Adding conversational context substantially improves ghosting quality, particularly on Open-Assistant and ShareGPT data.
A benchmark evaluating query auto-completion methods on DailyDialog, DSTC7-Ubuntu, Open Assistant, and ShareGPT is released.
A two-layer multi-modal graph enables image-to-text and text-to-image reasoning for evidence retrieval.
Token- and evidence-level fusion integrates claim and evidence embeddings for multi-modal claim verification.
Multi-modal Fusion-in-Decoder generates textual explanations to justify verification results.
The model jointly performs evidence retrieval, multi-modal claim verification, and explanation generation.
AIChartClaim is a scientific dataset containing textual and chart image evidence for claim verification in the AI domain.
CenterBench is a dataset of thousands of comprehension questions on center-embedded sentences with syntactically identical but semantically implausible counterparts.
CenterBench provides a framework for identifying when language models shift from structural analysis to semantic pattern matching.
Evaluation of multiple language models on CenterBench shows that performance gaps between plausible and implausible sentences widen as syntactic complexity increases.
Semantic plausibility harms model performance on questions requiring causal reasoning about resulting actions.
Reasoning-augmented models improve accuracy on CenterBench but exhibit semantic shortcuts, overthinking, and answer refusal behaviors.
Human participants display variable semantic effects, unlike models whose plausibility advantage grows with complexity.
PRISM detects dataset-level non-membership using only grey-box access to model logits.
Two models that have not seen a dataset exhibit higher rank correlation in their normalized token log probabilities than when one model has been trained on that data.
PRISM reliably rules out membership in training data across all tested datasets while avoiding false positives.
PLAT is a benchmark for assessing large language models' ability to predict the legitimacy of additional tax penalties.
PLAT includes binary-choice, multiple-choice, and essay-type questions derived from Korean court precedents.
PLAT evaluates large language models' understanding of tax law and their performance on legal cases that require complex reasoning beyond straightforward statutory application.

Systematic experiments show that large language models have limited baseline capabilities, particularly on cases involving conflicting issues that require comprehensive understanding of statutes and taxpayer circumstances.
Large language models struggle with the "AC" stage of the IRAC reasoning framework even for advanced inference-time scaling models such as o3.

Emerging architectures such as xLSTMs, structured state-space models, diffusion models, and adversarial learning are evaluated on sequence labeling tasks with varying structural complexity, label space, and token dependencies across multiple languages.
Performance advantages of these emerging architectures observed in simple settings do not consistently generalize across languages, datasets, or more complex structured sequence labeling tasks.
RAGPPI provides a factual question-answer benchmark comprising thousands of pairs about potential biological impacts of protein–protein interactions.
A gold-standard dataset of several hundred question-answer pairs for protein–protein interaction biological impact was created through expert-driven annotation.

An ensemble auto-evaluation language model that combines expert labeling characteristics, average fact–abstract similarity, and low-similarity fact counts was developed to construct a larger silver-standard dataset of thousands of question-answer pairs.
RAGPPI serves as a resource for advancing retrieval-augmented generation systems for drug discovery question answering.
ToolDreamer conditions retriever models to fetch tools based on synthetic tool descriptions generated by a language model.
ToolDreamer aligns queries and tools within the language space of tool descriptions.
ToolDreamer improves the performance of sparse retrievers on the ToolRet dataset.
ToolDreamer improves the performance of dense retrievers on the ToolRet dataset.
ToolDreamer enables retrievers to operate effectively with or without additional training, demonstrating flexibility.
ToolDreamer offloads a portion of the reasoning burden from the language model to the retriever.
The Antibacterial Inorganic Nanoparticles Dataset (AINA) contains 7,910 curated articles that distinguish intrinsic antibacterial inorganic nanoparticles from drug carrier or surface-bound studies.
AINA enables reproducible large-scale identification of intrinsically bactericidal inorganic nanoparticles.
Fine-tuned BioBERT classifier outperforms other evaluated models for classifying abstracts in nanoparticle-based antimicrobial research.
A lightweight SVM model with TF-IDF features provides competitive performance for classifying abstracts in nanoparticle-based antimicrobial research, suitable for low-resource settings.
Traditional machine learning and deep learning methods are effective for classifying scientific abstracts in nanoparticle-based antimicrobial research.
CSPB is a benchmark for assessing the robustness of self-supervised speech models in realistic conversational scenarios.
CSPB is constructed from four multi‑party datasets: AMI, AliMeeting, MMCSG, and DiPCo.
CSPB supports both single‑channel and multi‑channel evaluation.
CSPB is released as an open‑source toolkit that provides a unified framework for evaluating robust, spatially‑aware self‑supervised speech models.
The study extends prior methodology to evaluate GPT-2 on a larger set of natural languages and corresponding impossible perturbations.
GPT-2 learns each natural language and its impossible counterpart equally easily across the expanded language set.
A lenient separation test based on cross-linguistic variance in learning‑curve metrics shows that GPT-2 does not provide any systematic separation between natural and impossible languages.
Overall, GPT-2 provides no systematic distinction between humanly possible and impossible languages.
PADRE aligns diffusion language models using a pseudo-likelihood based objective.
The pseudo-likelihood based objective has the same optima as policy-gradient reinforcement learning optimization for diffusion language models.
The pseudo-likelihood based objective does not require evaluating exact likelihood from diffusion language models.
The method matches or surpasses the performance of recent diffusion language model training baselines such as diffu-GRPO/d1 on coding and mathematical reasoning benchmarks.
The approach provides a stable and practical alternative to reinforcement learning based fine-tuning of reasoning-focused diffusion language models.
PANDORA is a framework for investigating bidirectional persuasion dynamics between large language models and humans exposed to misinformative content.
A multi-agent large language model framework is employed to analyze the spread of misinformation under persuasion among demographic-oriented LLM agents.
Demographic factors cause notable differences in LLM susceptibility to misinformation, leading to varying correctness across groups.
Multi-agent LLMs exhibit echo chamber behavior that mirrors human-like group polarization patterns.
VQA-Causal and VCR-Causal are benchmarks that isolate and rigorously evaluate causal reasoning abilities of vision-language models.
Vision-language models excel at object and activity recognition but perform poorly on causal reasoning tasks, barely surpassing random guessing.
The poor causal reasoning of vision-language models is attributed to a severe lack of explicit causal expressions in widely used training datasets.
Targeted fine-tuning with hard negative cases improves vision-language models' causal reasoning while preserving generalization and downstream performance.
A multi-granular rationale-annotated ICD coding dataset derived from MIMIC-IV and ICD-10 is created.
An evaluation of ICD coding rationales along faithfulness and plausibility dimensions is conducted for entity-level mentions, LLM-generated rationales, and attention-based rationales.
LLM-generated rationales are employed as distant supervision to train rationale learning methods for ICD coding.
Few-shot prompting of an LLM with human-annotated examples enhances plausibility of rationale generation in both teacher and student models.
Chandomitra is an English-to-structured Sanskrit poetry translation dataset that adheres to the Anushtubh meter.
Constrained decoding achieves higher syntactic accuracy than GPT-4o for generating metrically valid Sanskrit poetry.
Instruction fine-tuning improves semantic coherence with the English input for structured Sanskrit poetry generation, while slightly reducing syntactic accuracy.
Human evaluation shows that the instruction fine-tuned model better captures poetic aspects than other evaluated models.
Various open and closed language models are benchmarked on the task of generating structured Sanskrit poetry from natural language inputs.
