memTrace extracts informative signals from transformer hidden states and attention patterns to perform membership inference attacks on large language models.
memTrace detects memorization fingerprints that traditional loss-based membership inference approaches may miss.
memTrace achieves strong membership detection across several model families on popular membership inference benchmarks.
Internal model behaviors can reveal training data exposure even when output-based signals appear protected.
Argument replacement, adjunction rewriting, their combination, and annotation generation constitute LLM-based data augmentation strategies for event argument extraction.
Boundary-aware augmentation yields larger performance gains than boundary-agnostic augmentation for low-resource event argument extraction.
Experiments on four benchmark datasets using GPT-4o-Mini and DeepSeek-R1-7B as data generators validate the effectiveness of the proposed augmentation strategies.
Augmentation quality is evaluated in terms of uncertainty reduction, error type distribution, data quality, and data scale.
A large annotated dataset of social media posts about the Chinese and Japanese Jirai subculture is introduced.
JiraiBench is a bilingual benchmark for evaluating large language models' detection of risky health behavior content in the Jirai subculture.
The dataset enables evaluation of large language models on culturally specific content.
Japanese prompts handle Chinese content better than Chinese prompts, indicating that cultural context can outweigh linguistic similarity.
Fine-tuned models exhibit potential cross-lingual knowledge transfer for detecting risky health behavior content across the two languages.
Culturally informed cross-lingual datasets are indispensable for creating effective content moderation tools that protect vulnerable communities across linguistic borders.
An LLM-based annotation pipeline is introduced for translating classical Chinese into Japanese via annotation.
A new dataset of digitized open-source translation data is constructed for the annotation-based translation task.
In low-resource settings, incorporating auxiliary Chinese NLP tasks improves training of sequence tagging models for the annotation-based translation task.
Large Language Models are evaluated on the annotation-based translation task and achieve high scores on direct machine translation.
The annotation-based method can supplement Large Language Models to improve the quality of character annotations.
Partial YaRN extends audio token positions without altering text token positions, enabling context extension for large audio-language models while preserving text capabilities.
Partial YaRN improves performance of large audio-language models across a wide range of settings.
Virtual Longform Audio Training augments Partial YaRN during training by simulating diverse audio lengths, allowing models to generalize to inputs longer than seen in training.
Virtual Longform Audio Training yields substantial performance improvements on long audio of unseen lengths.
MERLIN is a model-stacking framework that iteratively refines in two stages using a curriculum from bilingual bitext to task-specific data and adapts only a small set of DoRA weights.
MERLIN improves exact-match accuracy on the AfriMGSM benchmark relative to MindMerger.
MERLIN outperforms GPT-4o-mini on exact-match accuracy on the AfriMGSM benchmark.
MERLIN yields consistent accuracy gains on the MGSM and MSVAMP benchmarks across both low- and high-resource settings.
DART uses disagreements between multiple visual agents to identify visual tools that can resolve inter-agent disagreement.
DART introduces tool-aligned agreement scores that highlight agents whose outputs align with expert tools.
An aggregator agent in DART selects the final answer by combining agent outputs with tool information.
DART achieves higher performance than multi-agent debate and single-agent tool-calling frameworks on several multimodal reasoning benchmarks.
DART adapts effectively to new tools in applied domains, improving results on a medical multimodal dataset.
DART measures text overlap across discussion rounds to demonstrate richer multi-agent discussion compared to existing methods.
DART analyzes the distribution of expert tool calls to ensure that each tool is reliably used to resolve disagreement.
Output formatting is a critical hyperparameter that influences fine-tuned large language model performance on information extraction tasks.
Changing only the output format can cause large performance shifts in F1 scores for the same model.
No single output format consistently dominates across different settings.
The optimal output format depends on factors such as model family and dataset characteristics.
Informationally equivalent output formats can produce substantial performance variation in information extraction systems.
DuwatBench comprises 1,272 curated Arabic calligraphy samples covering about 1,475 unique words across six classical and modern styles, each annotated with sentence-level detection labels.

When evaluated on DuwatBench, thirteen leading Arabic and multilingual multimodal models achieve strong performance on clean text but struggle with calligraphic variation, artistic distortions, and precise visual–text alignment.
DuwatBench and its annotations are publicly released to facilitate culturally grounded multimodal research on Arabic language and visual heritage.
Morphologically-guided tokenization improves performance on four downstream tasks for Latin encoder models.
Performance gains from morphologically-guided tokenization are most pronounced on out-of-domain texts, indicating improved generalization.
Incorporating linguistic resources into tokenization serves as a feasible alternative to improve language model performance for low-resource, morphologically complex languages.
RoSE is a proxy metric for selecting the best LLM generator without human test sets.
RoSE trains a small model on the outputs of a candidate generator and evaluates it on synthetic examples from other candidate generators, using the mean performance as the final score.
RoSE identifies the optimal generator more frequently than any other intrinsic heuristic across multiple languages, models, and tasks.
RoSE achieves downstream performance close to that of the optimal generator baseline while using only synthetic data.
RoSE is the only intrinsic metric that exhibits a positive correlation with performance on human-labelled test data.
ExStrucTiny is a benchmark dataset for schema-variable structured information extraction from document images.
ExStrucTiny unifies key entity extraction, relation extraction, and visual question answering tasks.
ExStrucTiny is constructed using a novel pipeline that combines manual and synthetic human-validated samples.
ExStrucTiny includes a broader variety of document types and extraction scenarios than existing datasets.
Evaluation of open and closed vision-language models on ExStrucTiny reveals challenges in schema adaptation, query under-specification, and answer localization.
Comparative analysis of language models trained under controlled pre-training dataset or tokenizer settings versus standard settings reveals mechanisms of character-level knowledge acquisition.
Contributing factors to character-level information in language models are categorized into tokenization-dependent and tokenization-independent groups.
Merge rules and orthographic constraints are identified as primary tokenization-dependent factors for character-level information in language models.
Semantic associations of substrings and syntactic information are identified as key tokenization-independent factors for character-level information in language models.
LISTEN is a controlled benchmark that disentangles lexical reliance from acoustic sensitivity in emotion understanding for large audio language models.
Evaluation of six state-of-the-art large audio language models on LISTEN reveals a consistent dominance of lexical cues over acoustic cues.
Large audio language models predict a neutral emotion label when lexical cues are neutral or absent, regardless of acoustic information.
Large audio language models exhibit limited performance gains when lexical and acoustic emotion cues are aligned.
Large audio language models fail to correctly classify distinct emotions when lexical and acoustic cues are in conflict.
In paralinguistic settings lacking lexical content, large audio language models perform near chance level.
Predicting replacements for sensitive spans is formulated as a research task with principled use-inspired evaluation criteria.
A multi-token completion method is introduced to predict replacements for sensitive spans while preserving consistency with low compute requirements.
The multi-token completion method enables low-compute, local anonymization of data before external sharing.
The method generates more realistic text than alternative infilling methods and differentially private mechanisms across multiple domains without retraining.
The method better preserves utility than alternative infilling methods and differentially private mechanisms across multiple domains without retraining.
Grounded evaluations capturing utility for the task of replacing redacted content are contributed.
MedQA-CS is an AI-SCE framework inspired by Objective Structured Clinical Examinations for evaluating clinical skills of large language models.
MedQA-CS provides a comprehensive evaluation framework with publicly available data and expert annotations.
MedQA-CS enables quantitative and qualitative assessment of large language models as reliable judges in clinical skills evaluation.
MedQA-CS is more challenging than traditional multiple-choice question answering benchmarks for evaluating clinical skills.
Combining MedQA-CS with existing benchmarks allows more comprehensive evaluation of both open- and closed-source large language models' clinical capabilities.
INSURE-Dial provides a public benchmark for developing and assessing compliance-aware voice agents for phase-aware call auditing with span-based compliance verification.

INSURE-Dial corpus comprises de-identified AI-initiated calls with live insurance representatives and synthetically generated calls, each annotated with a phase-structured JSON schema covering IVR navigation, patient identification, coverage status, medication checks, and agent identification.
Each phase in INSURE-Dial is labeled for information and procedural compliance under explicit ask/answer logic.

INSURE-Dial defines two evaluation tasks: Phase Boundary Detection (span segmentation under phase-specific acceptance rules) and Compliance Verification (information/procedural compliance decisions given fixed spans).

Experiments on INSURE-Dial show strong per-phase performance of small, low-latency baselines, while end-to-end reliability is limited by span-boundary errors and full-call exact segmentation on real calls is low, indicating a gap between conversational fluency and audit-grade evidence.
LLM internal representations can reliably identify incoherent events in narratives.
The ability of LLMs to separate coherent and incoherent narratives diminishes by the end of the narrative.
LLM-generated overall coherence ratings fail to satisfactorily distinguish coherent from incoherent narratives.
Reasoning models tested do not eliminate deficits in recognizing incoherence in narratives.
LLMs are more sensitive to incoherence caused by setting violations than to incoherence caused by character trait violations.
A novel task of extracting low-resourced and noisy Latin fragments from mixed-language historical documents with varied layouts is defined.
A multimodal dataset of annotated pages for Latin detection in historical books is released.
Large foundation models are benchmarked on the Latin detection dataset.
Contemporary zero-shot models can reliably detect Latin in mixed-language corpora.
Contemporary zero-shot models lack functional comprehension of Latin.
A comprehensive baseline for processing Latin within mixed-language corpora is established.
MIRAGE is a benchmark for measuring robustness of LLM agents under imperfect guidance.
MIRAGE includes procedurally generated navigation, cooking, and gaming environments where the fidelity and relevance of auxiliary guidance vary.
MIRAGE is extended to realistic web tasks via WebArena using noisy or underspecified instructions extracted from demonstrations.
Evaluation on MIRAGE reveals critical failure modes in current LLM agents when auxiliary guidance is imperfect.
Changing noun and adjective endings in Italian prompts (e.g., from masculine "-o" to feminine "-a") significantly shifts educational recommendations generated by LLM-based virtual assistants.
LLM-based virtual assistants recommend STEM disciplines less for prompts with feminine grammatical gender in Italian.
LLM-based virtual assistants narrow the set of recommended disciplines for prompts with masculine grammatical gender in Italian.
The gender bias effects persist across multiple commercial LLMs from OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google.
Grammatical gender cues alone account for up to 76% of the bias observed when using prompts with proper names.
Continual Neural Topic Model (CoNTM) continuously learns topic models at subsequent time steps without forgetting previously learned topics.
CoNTM uses a globally updated prior distribution to retain knowledge across time steps.
CoNTM captures topic changes online.
CoNTM achieves higher topic quality and lower predictive perplexity than dynamic topic models.
CoNTM learns more diverse topics and better captures temporal changes than existing methods.
ConText-VQA provides image-question pairs with systematically generated persuasive prompts that deliberately conflict with visual evidence.
A testing framework benchmarks the susceptibility of vision-language models to conflicting textual inputs.
State-of-the-art vision-language models are vulnerable to misleading prompts, often overriding clear visual evidence in favor of conflicting text.
Persuasive textual manipulation causes a substantial performance drop in vision-language models after a single round of conversation.
Supervised contrastive learning learns domain-invariant representations for low-resource language identification.
Supervised contrastive learning improves language identification performance on out-of-domain data for low-resource languages.
Supervised contrastive learning maintains language identification performance for high-resource languages.
KG-CRAFT constructs a knowledge graph from claims and associated reports for claim verification.
KG-CRAFT formulates contextually relevant contrastive questions based on the knowledge graph structure to guide evidence distillation.
KG-CRAFT synthesizes distilled evidence reports into concise summaries for veracity assessment.
KG-CRAFT achieves state-of-the-art predictive performance on the LIAR-RAW and RAWFC fact-checking datasets.
Analyses validate that knowledge graph-based contrastive reasoning improves large language models' fact-checking capabilities.
A baseline of attention regularization using randomly selected tokens is introduced for comparison with human-annotated rationales.
Attention regularization with randomly selected tokens often yields improvements comparable to those obtained with human-annotated rationales.
Human-annotated rationales reduce model sensitivity to strong spurious correlations more effectively than randomly selected tokens.
DIVINE jointly predicts diagnostic categories and severity levels for oro-facial neurological disorders using synchronized speech and facial video.
DIVINE employs hierarchical variational bottlenecks, sparse gated fusion, and learnable symptom tokens to disentangle shared and modality-specific representations.
DIVINE operates on embeddings extracted from state-of-the-art audio and video foundation models.
Experiments on the Toronto NeuroFace dataset show that DIVINE achieves state-of-the-art performance for both full-modality and single-modality (audio-only or video-only) test conditions.
DIVINE demonstrates superior generalization compared to unimodal models and baseline fusion techniques under modality-constrained scenarios.
Disentangling shared and modality-specific representations within multimodal foundation model embeddings enhances clinical interpretability and generalization.
A fully multilingual prompting strategy translates task prompts into each language for previously fact-checked claim detection.
Six open-source multilingual large language models are evaluated across twenty languages on the AMC-16K dataset for previously fact-checked claim detection.
Monolingual and cross-lingual performance disparities are observed, with trends associated with model family, size, and prompting strategy.
Recommendations are provided to improve equity in multilingual fact-checking.
Retrieval bias is analyzed using multilingual embedding models by examining the frequency distribution of retrieved claims.
Certain claims are retrieved disproportionately across posts, leading to inflated retrieval performance for popular claims and under-representation of less common claims.
Online difficulty-aware filtering has a theoretical lower bound on expected policy improvement that is proportional to the variance of task-level success probabilities.
Selecting tasks of intermediate difficulty maximizes learning efficiency according to the derived lower bound.
Balanced filtering maximizes the lower bound on expected policy improvement.
Balanced filtering improves convergence speed and final performance on multiple math reasoning benchmarks.
Extending the analysis to various reward distributions provides a principled foundation for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards curriculum strategies.
TReX is a regression-based framework that predicts the optimal data mixture for tokenizer training.
TReX trains small-scale proxy tokenizers on random mixtures and learns to predict compression performance from data mixtures.
TReX enables scalable mixture search before large-scale tokenizer training, mitigating the accuracy‑cost trade‑off in multilingual tokenizer design.

Tokenizers trained with mixtures predicted by TReX achieve higher compression efficiency than tokenizers trained with LLaMA3‑based or uniform mixtures, demonstrating scalability, robustness, and practical effectiveness.
Tight lower bounds on the runtime of any deterministic speculative generation algorithm are established.
A theoretical analysis linking speculative token generation to branching random walks is presented, enabling analysis of the optimal draft tree selection problem.

An upper bound on the expected number of tokens successfully predicted per speculative iteration is proved as a function of the verifier's batch size and the first and second moments of the verifier's output entropy.
Empirical evaluations on Llama models confirm that the derived theoretical bounds accurately predict practical performance, demonstrating their tightness.
An evaluation protocol that combines long persona dialogues (over 100 rounds) with evaluation datasets creates dialogue-conditioned benchmarks for robustly measuring long-context effects.
The protocol enables systematic measurement of persona fidelity, instruction-following, and safety in extended interactions.
Persona fidelity degrades over the course of long dialogues, especially in goal-oriented conversations.
A trade-off exists between persona fidelity and instruction-following, with non-persona baselines initially outperforming persona-assigned models.
As dialogues progress and persona fidelity fades, persona responses become increasingly similar to baseline responses.

A unified benchmark and evaluation protocol for abstention in medical multiple-choice question answering integrates conformal prediction, adversarial question perturbations, and explicit abstention options.
Systematic evaluation of open- and closed-source large language models shows that even high-accuracy models often fail to abstain when uncertain.
Providing explicit abstention options consistently increases model uncertainty and leads to safer abstention, more so than input perturbations.
Increasing model size or using advanced prompting yields little improvement in abstention behavior.
Abstention mechanisms are identified as central for trustworthy deployment of large language models in high-stakes medical applications.
An end-to-end reinforcement learning framework for robust and secure LLM watermarking is introduced.
The framework adopts an anchoring mechanism for reward terms to ensure stable training.
The framework introduces regularization terms to prevent reward hacking.
Experiments demonstrate a state-of-the-art trade-off across detectability, text quality, robustness, and security criteria.
Experiments demonstrate improved resistance to spoofing attacks without degrading other criteria.
Systematic analysis of lexical and temporal features reveals their impact on turn structure predictability in spoken dialogue.
Principal component analysis, mixed-effects modelling, and temporal surprisal analysis show that linguistic fluency induces overconfidence at intermediate completion points.
Temporal surprisal analysis demonstrates that turn‑shift overlap dominates boundary detection.
Findings delineate conditions where lexical‑temporal information suffices for turn‑taking prediction and where additional cues become necessary.
The study provides a framework for evaluating the distribution of turn‑taking cues in dialogue systems.
Query decomposition and document retrieval are modeled as an exploitation-exploration problem that updates belief about sub-query utility after each retrieved document.
Bandit learning methods are used to dynamically select the most informative sub-queries in retrieval-augmented generation systems.
Estimating document relevance with rank information and human judgments improves document-level precision, α-nDCG, and downstream long-form generation performance.
DeepInsert inserts multimodal tokens directly into the middle of multimodal language models, bypassing early layers.
DeepInsert reduces training and inference costs of multimodal language models.
DeepInsert preserves or surpasses the performance of existing baselines across vision, audio, and molecular modalities and model sizes from 350M to 13B parameters.

An iterative, prompt-based framework constructs a Cultural Commonsense Knowledge Graph (CCKG) by treating large language models as cultural archives and eliciting culture-specific entities, relations, and practices into multi-step inferential chains across languages.
Human judgments of cultural relevance, correctness, and path coherence are used to evaluate CCKG on five countries.
Cultural knowledge graphs generated by the framework are more accurately realized in English than in non-English target cultures, indicating uneven cultural encoding in current large language models.
Augmenting smaller large language models with CCKG improves performance on cultural reasoning tasks.
Augmenting smaller large language models with CCKG improves performance on story generation tasks, with the largest gains from English chains.
Chain-structured cultural knowledge serves as a practical substrate for culturally grounded natural language processing.
Nahw is a comprehensive benchmark for Arabic grammar that includes tasks of grammatical error detection, correction, and explanation, covering both theoretical knowledge and practical applications.
Evaluation of large language models on Nahw reveals that many models exhibit substantial deficiencies in Arabic grammar comprehension.
GPT-4o outperforms the best Arabic model on the Nahw benchmark.
Fine-tuning with synthetic data improves performance on Arabic grammar tasks, but training on natural, high-quality data yields greater effectiveness.
Training LLMs with context-augmented examples increases attention to provided knowledge and improves grounding compared to training without context.
Context-augmented training shifts LLMs to store and leverage less parametric knowledge and depend more on provided context.
Employing a context-augmented LLM as the backbone of vision-language models reduces hallucination and enhances visual grounding.
Maintaining separate context-augmented and context-free LLMs and routing inputs between them provides more robust overall performance than a single mixed model by preserving complementary strengths.
A three-dimensional specificity framework (general, control, robustness) for inference-time interventions is introduced.
Model steering methods reduce overrefusal while preserving general abilities and refusal behavior on harmful queries.
Model steering methods substantially increase vulnerability to jailbreaks, indicating a failure of robustness specificity.

Systematic evaluation of specificity in model steering across overrefusal and faithfulness hallucination use cases demonstrates that standard efficacy and specificity checks are insufficient without robustness assessment.
MixCal is a calibration method that improves in-domain performance of compressed language models in a post-training setting.
MixCal outperforms existing approaches on domain-specific tasks while preserving general performance.
MixCal reduces the computational cost of language model compression.
Novel divergence-based metrics that track learning trajectories using next-token distributions are introduced.
In GPT-2 small, abstract class-level linguistic behavior appears earlier in training than lexical item-specific behavior.
In GPT-2 small, distinct linguistic behaviors emerge abruptly in sequence at different training steps, indicating that abstraction drives language learning.
Transformer language models can serve as existence proofs for models of human language acquisition.
BLEnD-Vis is a multimodal, multicultural benchmark that evaluates the robustness of everyday cultural knowledge in vision-language models across linguistic rephrasings and visual modalities.

BLEnD-Vis constructs hundreds of culturally grounded question templates covering dozens of world regions and generates three aligned multiple-choice formats: a text-only Region→Entity query, an inverted text-only Entity→Region query, and a VQA-style version with generated images.
BLEnD-Vis comprises thousands of images and tens of thousands of multiple-choice question instances that have been validated through human annotation.
Evaluation with BLEnD-Vis demonstrates that current vision-language models exhibit significant performance drops when cultural questions are linguistically rephrased.
Evaluation with BLEnD-Vis shows that visual cues can improve model performance, yet cross-modal consistency remains low, especially for lower-resource regions.
BLEnD-Vis provides a systematic testbed for analyzing cultural robustness and multimodal grounding in vision-language models, facilitating the development of more culturally competent systems.
Datasets of human-aligned creative and uncreative examples for creative writing, unconventional problem-solving, and research ideation are compiled.

Four creativity measures—perplexity, LLM-as-a-Judge, the Creativity Index, and syntactic templates—are evaluated for their ability to discriminate creative from uncreative examples across multiple domains.
Metrics exhibit limited consistency across domains, with measures that distinguish creativity in one domain failing in others.
Metrics often disagree on the same data points, such as the Creativity Index and perplexity assigning opposite creativity rankings.
Perplexity primarily reflects fluency rather than novelty.
LLM-as-a-Judge produces inconsistent judgments under minor prompt variations and shows bias toward particular labels.
The Creativity Index mainly measures lexical diversity and is highly sensitive to implementation choices.
Syntactic templates are ineffective in settings dominated by formulaic language.
Datasets and evaluation code are released.
A methodology that assesses large language model alignment with the democracy–authoritarianism spectrum by integrating the F-scale, the FavScore metric, and role-model probing is presented.
FavScore, a metric for evaluating model favorability toward world leaders, is introduced.
Role-model probing is applied to determine which figures large language models cite as general role models.
Large language models generally favor democratic values and leaders.
Large language models show increased favorability toward authoritarian figures when prompted in Mandarin.
Large language models frequently cite authoritarian figures as role models even outside explicitly political contexts.
An LLM-as-a-judge framework classifies the type of the preceding adult utterance and scores child responses on Expansion and Independence axes.
The framework shows developmental validity by revealing age-related patterns in the Expansion and Independence scores.
The framework improves age estimation of children compared to common baseline metrics.
The framework detects semantic differences tied to discourse relations, confirming semantic sensitivity.
The framework’s metrics align with human judgments, supporting large-scale evaluation of child utterances.

Evidence Ranking is a task that prioritizes presenting sufficient information as early as possible in a ranked list to minimize user reading effort while keeping all evidence accessible for sequential verification.
A new evaluation framework inspired by information retrieval metrics is introduced for the Evidence Ranking task.
A unified benchmark for Evidence Ranking is constructed by aggregating existing fact verification datasets.
Incremental ranking strategies capture complementary evidence better than one-shot ranking for the Evidence Ranking task.
LLM-based methods outperform shallower baselines on the Evidence Ranking task.
Evidence ranking reduces user reading effort and improves verification compared to evidence selection, as demonstrated in a controlled user study.
An information-theoretic framework based on Partial Information Decomposition audits unlearning in language models.
The framework decomposes mutual information between model representations and forgotten data into components representing unlearned and residual knowledge.
Redundant information shared across pre- and post-unlearning models constitutes residual knowledge that persists after unlearning.
Residual knowledge correlates with susceptibility to adversarial reconstruction attacks.
A representation-based risk score enables abstention on sensitive inputs at inference time to mitigate privacy leakage.

SCENEBench is a benchmark suite for evaluating audio comprehension across four categories: background sound understanding, noise localization, cross-linguistic speech understanding, and vocal characterizer recognition.
SCENEBench includes a small human-recorded evaluation split for each category.
SCENEBench measures model latency in addition to performance.
SCENEBench provides a structured error taxonomy to characterize failure modes across audio understanding tasks.
Evaluation with SCENEBench reveals critical gaps in current Large Audio Language Models, with performance ranging from far below random chance to high accuracy across tasks.
A comprehensive benchmark covering six prompting strategies, seven large language models, and four domain datasets of human- and AI-generated texts is constructed.
Classification-based detectors are fine-tuned on various generation settings and evaluated for cross-prompt, cross-model, and cross-dataset generalization.
Correlations between generalization accuracies and shifts in eighty linguistic features between training and test conditions are computed to explain performance variance.
Generalization performance of detectors is significantly associated with linguistic features such as tense usage and pronoun frequency.
A systematic study explains generalization behavior of AI-text detectors through linguistic analysis.
LACE introduces a language-grounded attribute-controllable translation framework for multi-domain image-to-image translation.
GLIP-Adapter fuses global semantic information with local structural features to preserve consistency during translation.
Multi-Domain Control Guidance grounds the semantic delta between source and target prompts into per-attribute translation vectors that align linguistic semantics with visual changes.
LACE supports compositional multi-domain control with independently adjustable strength for each attribute.

Empirical evaluation on CelebA(Dialog) and BDD100K demonstrates that LACE achieves high visual fidelity, preserves structural content, provides interpretable domain-specific control, and outperforms previous baselines.
Audio Reasoning Tasks (ART) is a benchmark for assessing multimodal large language models' ability to solve problems that require reasoning over audio signals.
Image Corruption-Inspired Membership Inference Attacks (ICIMIA) exploit the differing sensitivity of large vision-language models to image corruption for member versus non-member images.
In a white-box setting, ICIMIA infers image membership by measuring similarity between the image embedding and the embedding of its corrupted version.
In a black-box setting, ICIMIA infers image membership by measuring similarity between output text embeddings generated from queries containing the image and a textual instruction.
Experiments on existing datasets demonstrate that ICIMIA is effective for membership inference under both white-box and black-box scenarios.
NG-Router formulates nutrition question answering as a supervised, knowledge-graph-guided multi-agent collaboration problem.
NG-Router integrates agent nodes into heterogeneous knowledge graphs.
NG-Router employs a graph neural network to learn task-aware routing distributions over agents using soft supervision derived from empirical agent performance.
NG-Router includes a gradient-based subgraph retrieval mechanism that identifies salient evidence during training to enhance multi-hop and relational reasoning.
NG-Router consistently outperforms single-agent and ensemble baselines across multiple benchmarks and backbone models for nutrition question answering.
A systematic comparison of model completions to original human responses is conducted across sixteen language models, three dialogue corpora, and multiple stylometric features.
Language models strongly converge to the conversation’s style, often overfitting relative to the human baseline.
Convergence patterns are often specific to individual stylometric features.
Instruction-tuned and larger language models converge less than pretrained and smaller counterparts.
The mechanisms driving convergence in language models are hypothesized to differ substantially from those underlying human convergence.
RotBench is a benchmark of 350 manually-filtered images (lifestyle, portrait, landscape) for evaluating multimodal large language models on image rotation identification.
State-of-the-art multimodal large language models, including GPT-5, o3, and Gemini-2.5-Pro, fail to reliably identify image rotation across 0°, 90°, 180°, and 270° orientations.
Providing auxiliary information such as captions or depth maps, or using chain-of-thought prompting, yields only small and inconsistent improvements in rotation identification performance.

Most multimodal large language models reliably identify right-side-up (0°) images, some models reliably identify upside-down (180°) images, but none reliably distinguish between 90° and 270° rotations.
Simultaneously presenting an image in multiple orientations yields moderate performance gains for reasoning models in rotation identification.
A voting-based evaluation setup improves the rotation identification performance of weaker multimodal large language models.
Fine-tuning multimodal large language models substantially improves identification of 180° rotated images but does not improve distinction between 90° and 270° rotations.
Deeper Self-Prediction is a multi-step training objective that teaches robust self-correction by denoising the model’s own intermediate outputs.
UCB-guided Decoding is an inference algorithm that treats token re-masking as a multi-armed bandit problem and uses the Upper Confidence Bound to balance exploration and exploitation.

The unified framework combining Deeper Self-Prediction and UCB-guided Decoding bridges the training‑inference discrepancy in discrete diffusion text generation and consistently improves faithfulness and coherence over existing diffusion baselines as measured by automatic metrics and LLM-as-a-Judge evaluations.
OD-Stega generates stego-texts using a large language model combined with arithmetic coding for coverless steganography.
Steganographic embedding is formulated as maximizing the entropy of a replacement probability distribution for the next token while constraining its divergence from the original LLM distribution.
Closed-form solutions are derived for the embedding optimization under both KL divergence and total variation constraints.
A simple prompt selection approach resolves tokenization mismatch between the LLM and the steganographic process.
The optimized distribution is combined with a vocabulary truncation technique to improve steganographic efficiency.
The proposed method can be incorporated with existing non‑arithmetic coding steganography techniques such as the Discop technique.
MAQuA is a multi-outcome adaptive question-asking framework for simultaneous multidimensional mental health screening.
MAQuA combines multi-outcome modeling on language responses with item response theory and factor analysis to select the most informative questions across multiple dimensions.
MAQuA reduces the number of assessment questions required for score stabilization compared to random question ordering.
MAQuA demonstrates robust performance across internalizing (depression, anxiety) and externalizing (substance use, eating disorder) mental health domains.
Early stopping strategies in MAQuA further reduce patient time and burden.
A dataset of 1,016 scenarios categorized as ethical, unethical, and neutral is introduced for analyzing subtle biases in large language models.
A permutation test is proposed to detect bias by comparing probability distributions of ethical and unethical scenarios with those of neutral scenarios across demographic groups.
A tendency measurement is introduced to quantify bias magnitude through the relative difference between probability distributions of ethical and unethical scenarios.

Evaluations of twelve prominent large language models reveal persistent and nuanced biases across residency status, political ideology, fitness status, educational attainment, and attitudes toward AI.
Llama models exhibit the most pronounced biases among the evaluated large language models.
BILLY extracts and blends multiple distinct persona vectors directly in a model’s activation space.
BILLY steers a large language model’s generation process with a merged persona vector during inference, enabling multi-perspective output without explicit multi-LLM communication.
BILLY surpasses single-model prompting and traditional multi-LLM approaches on creativity-oriented benchmarks while substantially reducing inference time and computational costs.
Blending distinct persona vectors with BILLY provides effective control over complementary aspects of generation and enhances interpretability.
MQ4CS generates multiple queries per user utterance using large language models to capture different aspects of the information need.
MQ4CS improves retrieval performance compared to state-of-the-art query rewriting methods on six conversational information seeking datasets.
MASQ is a dataset comprising multiple-aspect queries for six conversational information seeking datasets, constructed using MQ4CS.
Fine-tuning a model on MASQ yields significant improvements in retrieval performance.
A computational study investigates the interaction between emotional framing and logical fallacies using large language models.
Eight large language models are benchmarked for their ability to inject emotional appeal into fallacious arguments while preserving logical structure.
The best-performing language models are used to generate stimuli for a human experiment on fallacy detection.
LLM-driven emotional framing impairs human detection of logical fallacies.
Human fallacy detection performance is higher when the emotional framing conveys enjoyment rather than fear or sadness.
Enjoyment, fear, and sadness emotional framings are associated with higher convincingness compared to neutral or other emotion states.
A lookahead quality gate verifies speculative decoding by selecting the longest reliable prefix of each k-token lookahead draft.
The quality gate computes a geometry-based quality score for each prefix using only the base model’s hidden states.
Acceptance thresholds are determined via quantile calibration estimated from unlabeled prompts.
The method integrates with speculative blockwise decoding without requiring auxiliary heads, reward models, or finetuning.
The method improves accuracy over sampling baselines while providing faster generation.
RefusalBench provides a generative methodology that programmatically creates diagnostic test cases through controlled linguistic perturbation.
RefusalBench employs 176 distinct perturbation strategies across six categories of informational uncertainty and three intensity levels.
RefusalBench includes two benchmarks, RefusalBench-NQ for single-document tasks and RefusalBench-GaRAGe for multi-document tasks.
Evaluation across many models shows that neither model scale nor extended reasoning improves selective refusal performance.
Selective refusal performance comprises separable detection and categorization skills.
Selective refusal is identified as a trainable, alignment-sensitive capability.
Automatic detection of hype in biomedical research is defined as a task.
Formalized guidelines for identifying hype language are proposed.
A portion of the NIH grant application corpus is annotated for hype language using the formalized guidelines.
Traditional text classifiers and language models are evaluated on the hype detection task and compared to a human baseline.
Formalized annotation guidelines enable reliable human annotation of candidate hype adjectives.
Training machine learning models on the annotated dataset yields promising results for hype detection.
Hype detection exhibits linguistic complexity and may require domain knowledge.
The annotation guidelines and dataset for hype detection are released as a resource.
PromptAnatomy decomposes prompts into functional components.
ComPerturb selectively modifies prompt components to expose component-wise vulnerabilities while maintaining linguistic plausibility through perplexity-based filtering.
Four instruction-tuning datasets are structurally annotated and validated by human reviewers using PromptAnatomy.
ComPerturb attains state-of-the-art attack success rates against five advanced large language models.
Ablation analyses demonstrate that prompt dissection and perplexity filtering have complementary effects on adversarial attack performance.
USChainMains dataset is introduced to evaluate LLM-based meal recommendations across gender and racial groups.
Healthfulness of LLM-generated meal recommendations is measured against established dietary standards.
LLMs systematically recommend meals with higher levels of adverse nutrients for names associated with Black, Hispanic, or male individuals compared to other groups.
Model scaling improves overall healthfulness of LLM-generated meal recommendations but does not eliminate healthfulness gaps between demographic groups.
Augmented reasoning mitigates gender bias in LLM-generated meal recommendations but does not mitigate racial bias.
A benchmark for regex minimization containing over a million regexes paired with their minimal equivalents is introduced.
Two language models are trained on the regex minimization dataset.
Five open-source large language models are evaluated on regex minimization as a PSPACE-complete problem.
Experiments analyze the generalization capabilities and reasoning limitations of language models on regex minimization.
A foundation for solving PSPACE-complete problems with language models is established.
Correct-to-incorrect sycophancy signals are most linearly accessible within multi-head attention activations.
Linear probes across the residual stream and MLPs exhibit separability of sycophancy signals, but steering is most effective in a sparse subset of middle-layer attention heads.
Linear probes trained on TruthfulQA transfer effectively to other factual QA benchmarks.

The discovered sycophancy direction has limited overlap with previously identified truthful directions, indicating that factual accuracy and deference resistance arise from related but distinct mechanisms.
Influential attention heads attend disproportionately to expressions of user doubt, contributing to sycophantic shifts.
Simple targeted linear interventions exploiting the internal geometry of attention activations can mitigate sycophancy.
HintNavigator is a cognitive multi-agent collaboration framework for dynamic web navigation that enhances cyberspace exploration via In-Context Exploration.
HintNavigator categorizes interaction history into Declarative History (environment observations) and Procedural History (action trajectories) to improve historical reflection.
HintNavigator dynamically integrates dual-history streams through specialized cognitive agents to enable self-directed backtracking guided by working memory consolidation.
HintNavigator achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source LLM agents on the WebArena benchmark, surpassing the proprietary model Claude-3.5 Sonnet.
DEER probe uses embedding vectors extracted from generative language models to probe factual knowledge.
DEER probe operates with linear time complexity in the number of candidate entities.
DEER probe supports rank-based evaluation metrics such as Hit@k.
DEER probe handles multi-token entity names.
DEER probe enables probing while disambiguating homographic tail-entity names.
DEER probe's scores correlate with those of existing knowledge probes, validating its probing capability.
DEER probe provides practical benefits due to its improved scalability.
A systematic literature review synthesizes findings on multilingual safety of large language models.
The review explores methodologies used to assess multilingual safety of large language models.
The review identifies challenges such as dataset availability and evaluation biases in multilingual safety assessment.
The review highlights gaps in multilingual safety research and provides recommendations for future work.
An interactive Streamlit dashboard provides transparent access to the extracted data and supports continuous updates.
InfiGUIAgent is a multimodal generalist GUI agent that performs native reasoning and reflection.
InfiGUIAgent is trained with a two-stage supervised fine-tuning pipeline.
The first stage of the pipeline enhances GUI understanding and grounding skills.
The second stage integrates hierarchical reasoning and expectation‑reflection reasoning skills using synthesized data.
InfiGUIAgent achieves competitive performance on multiple GUI benchmarks, demonstrating that native reasoning skills improve GUI interaction for automation tasks.
A cloze-style surprisal method that conditions on full-sentence context is introduced for measuring surprisal of metaphoric words.
Surprisal of metaphoric words is analyzed across corpus-based and synthetic metaphor datasets using sixteen causal language model variants.
Surprisal scores from language models exhibit moderate correlations with human annotations of metaphor novelty.
On corpus-based metaphor data, the correlation between surprisal and novelty decreases as model size increases, indicating an inverse scaling effect.
On synthetic metaphor data, the correlation between surprisal and novelty increases as model size increases, supporting a quality–power hypothesis.
ReFACT provides a benchmark of expert-annotated question-answer pairs with span-level error annotations for scientific confabulation detection.
Evaluation of state-of-the-art LLMs on ReFACT reveals a salient distractor failure mode where many incorrect span predictions are semantically unrelated to actual errors.
The salient distractor failure mode persists across model scales from 1B to 70B, indicating a semantic grounding deficit not resolved by scaling.
Comparative judgment of answers side-by-side is more difficult for LLMs than independent detection, leading to lower performance.
These findings challenge the reliability of using LLMs as judges for scientific factuality.
Moral Dilemma Dataset is a benchmark of real-world moral dilemmas paired with binary human evaluations and free-text rationales.
The alignment of LLM and human moral judgments is framed as a pluralistic distributional alignment task that compares judgment distributions across dilemmas.
Large language models reproduce human moral judgments only under conditions of high human consensus, and alignment deteriorates sharply as human disagreement increases.
A taxonomy of 60 moral values extracted from rationales reveals that LLMs rely on a narrower set of moral values than humans.
Dynamic Moral Profiling is a Dirichlet-based sampling method that conditions model outputs on human-derived value profiles.
Dynamic Moral Profiling improves alignment between LLM and human moral judgments and enhances the diversity of expressed moral values.
Cross-encoder analysis, linguistic features, and sparse autoencoders are employed to ground intrinsic dimension of texts in interpretable properties.
Intrinsic dimension is uncorrelated with entropy-based metrics after controlling for length, capturing geometric complexity orthogonal to prediction quality.
Intrinsic dimension stratifies by genre, with scientific prose exhibiting low values, encyclopedic content medium values, and creative or opinion writing high values across tested models.
Sparse autoencoders identify that scientific signals such as formal tone, report templates, and statistics reduce intrinsic dimension, while personalization, emotion, and narrative increase it.
Steering experiments confirm that modifying scientific or humanized signals causally changes intrinsic dimension as identified by sparse autoencoders.
Few-shot meta-learning is applied to syllogistic reasoning to encourage extraction of abstract inference rules across tasks.
Controlled premise-selection datasets are constructed to isolate logical competence for syllogistic reasoning.
Meta-learning enables small language models (1.5B–7B) to achieve strong generalization gains on syllogistic reasoning, particularly in low-data regimes.
Meta-learned small language models outperform GPT-4o and o3-mini on a syllogistic reasoning task.
RL-Text2Vis is the first reinforcement learning framework for Text-to-Visualization generation.
RL-Text2Vis uses Group Relative Policy Optimization with a multi-objective reward that jointly optimizes textual accuracy, code validity, and visualization quality using post-execution feedback.
RL-Text2Vis improves chart quality over GPT-4o on the Text2Vis benchmark.
RL-Text2Vis increases code execution success relative to its zero-shot baseline.
RL-Text2Vis demonstrates robust generalization to out-of-domain datasets VIS-Eval and NVBench.
A formal framework for simulating social networks is introduced.
Different approaches for imitating user communication on social networks are empirically evaluated in English and German.
Empirical results indicate that validation of social simulations requires measuring empirical realism in the same setting where simulation components were fitted.
LitBench is a benchmark and dataset for reliable evaluation of creative writing, comprising a large training corpus of story pairs and a curated test set from Reddit.
Specialized Bradley-Terry and generative reward models trained on LitBench outperform off-the-shelf language model judges in agreement with human preferences for creative writing evaluation.
An online human study shows that the rankings produced by the trained reward models align more closely with human preferences than those produced by off-the-shelf judges.
CAIRE is an evaluation metric that assesses the cultural relevance of an image given a user-defined set of labels.
CAIRE grounds entities and concepts in an image to a knowledge base and uses factual information to provide independent graded judgments for each culture label.
A manually curated dataset of culturally salient but rare items was constructed using language models.
Two datasets of culturally universal concepts were created, one from text-to-image generated outputs and another from naturally occurring data.
CAIRE's scores correlate with human ratings on culturally universal concept datasets, demonstrating strong alignment with human judgment across diverse image sources.
RAFFLES is an offline evaluation architecture that incorporates iterative reasoning.

RAFFLES operates as an iterative multi-component pipeline with a central Judge that systematically identifies faults and specialized Evaluators that assess the quality of candidate faults and the Judge’s rationales.
RAFFLES can identify step-level faults in multi-agent systems using the Who&When dataset.
RAFFLES can diagnose step-level mathematical reasoning errors using the ReasonEval datasets.
RAFFLES achieves higher accuracy than strong baselines on the Who&When Hand-Crafted and Algorithmically-Generated datasets and on the ReasonEval datasets.
RAFFLES enables automated fault detection for autonomous systems, reducing reliance on labor-intensive manual review.
Sparsely Grounded Visual Navigation is introduced as a task to evaluate sequential decision-making abilities of multimodal large language models in knowledge-intensive real-world environments.

A comprehensive benchmark covering four diverse global cities is created to assess raw multimodal large language model agents in city navigation without additional environmental annotations or specialized architectural modifications.
Verbalization of Path (VoP) grounds the agent's internal reasoning by probing city-scale cognitive maps of key landmarks and directions, substantially enhancing navigation success.

State-of-the-art multimodal large language models, reasoning techniques such as GEPA, chain-of-thought, reflection, and the competitive baseline PReP significantly underperform on the Sparsely Grounded Visual Navigation benchmark.
Jailbreaks can be framed as inference-time alignment within a unified optimization framework.
LIAR extends best-of-N inference-time alignment to the adversarial setting.
Suboptimality bounds are derived that prove LIAR approaches an optimal jailbreak as compute scales.
Safety-Net is introduced as a measure of how vulnerable a language model is to jailbreaks.
LIAR generates natural, hard-to-detect prompts that achieve competitive attack success while running significantly faster than prior suffix-based jailbreaks.
ConGrad mitigates negative interference in multilingual preference alignment by filtering preference samples whose gradients align with a consensus direction.
ConGrad computes a consensus gradient direction that aggregates cross-lingual beneficial gradients.
ConGrad uses sublinear gradient compression to reduce memory overhead during gradient accumulation for large language models.
ConGrad is incorporated into a self-rewarding framework for multilingual preference alignment.
ConGrad consistently outperforms strong baselines on LLaMA3-8B and Gemma2-2B across ten languages, including unseen languages, with minimal alignment tax.
KidsArtBench provides a benchmark of over 1k children’s artworks annotated by expert educators across nine rubric-aligned dimensions with accompanying expert comments.
KidsArtBench pairs multi-dimensional annotations with comment supervision to enable ordinal assessment and formative feedback for children’s artwork evaluation.
Attribute-specific multi-LoRA approach assigns a distinct LoRA module to each evaluation dimension in the scoring rubric.
Regression-Aware Fine-Tuning (RAFT) aligns model predictions with ordinal scales for artwork evaluation.
Attribute-specific multi-LoRA approach with RAFT improves correlation between model predictions and ordinal scores on Qwen2.5-VL-7B.
The approach yields larger improvements on perceptual dimensions and reduces performance gaps on higher-order attributes.
Educator-aligned supervision and attribute-aware training produce pedagogically meaningful evaluations of children’s artwork.
KidsArtBench establishes a rigorous testbed for sustained progress in educational AI.
TimeMachine-bench is a benchmark for evaluating software migration tasks in real-world Python projects.
TimeMachine-bench comprises GitHub repositories whose tests fail after dependency updates.
The benchmark construction process is fully automated, enabling live updates of the benchmark.
A human-verified subset of the benchmark ensures problem solvability.
Agent-based baselines built on eleven models have been evaluated on the verified subset of the benchmark.
Large language models exhibit reliability challenges in migration tasks, including spurious solutions that exploit low test coverage.
Large language models produce unnecessary edits due to suboptimal tool-use strategies.
An LLM-based strategy for geocoding compositional location references is proposed.
The proposed strategy improves performance on geocoding compositional location references.
A relatively small fine-tuned LLM can achieve comparable performance to much larger off-the-shelf models on geocoding compositional location references.
LLMs' geospatial knowledge and reasoning skills relevant to geocoding compositional location references are evaluated.
An utterance-level detection framework for LLM-generated text in conversational settings is introduced.
The framework integrates features from individual and combined analysis of dialogue participants' responses.
The framework employs a transformer-based recurrent architecture.
A curated dataset comprising human‑human, human‑LLM, and LLM‑LLM dialogues is created for training and evaluation.
The framework attains high accuracy and fast inference in detecting LLM-generated text in real‑time conversations.
Concept Differentiation extends Word-in-Context to include inter-word scenarios.
A dataset for Concept Differentiation derived from SemCor is provided.
Concept-Aligned Embeddings are representation models fine-tuned on the Concept Differentiation dataset.
Concept-Aligned Embeddings provide efficient multi-purpose representations of lexical meaning that achieve best performance on various lexical semantic tasks.
Fine-tuning with Concept-Aligned Embeddings changes the spatial organization of embeddings.
ULPT optimizes prompts in a low-dimensional space and uses a frozen random matrix for up-projection.
ULPT reduces training parameters by orders of magnitude compared to vanilla prompt tuning while preserving performance.
ULPT consistently outperforms recent parameter-efficient tuning methods across a wide range of NLP tasks using significantly fewer parameters.
ULPT provides a storage-efficient framework for massive large language model customization.
A joint measurement methodology simultaneously quantifies humor, stereotypicality, and toxicity in language model-generated jokes.
Information-theoretic metrics are used to analyze incongruity signals in humor generation, revealing that harmful cues increase predictive uncertainty.
Harmful cues can make harmful punchlines more expected for some language models, indicating intrinsic structural embedding in learned humor distributions.
Under fixed neutral setups, harmful outputs receive higher humor scores than neutral outputs, evidencing a bias amplification loop between generators and evaluators.
Human evaluation on a satire-generation task shows that LLM-perceived funniness relies on increased stereotypicality and toxicity, including for closed models.
H3Fusion introduces a mixture-of-experts fusion mechanism that models alignment as a controllable drift in the representation subspace.
H3Fusion uses a drift-regularization loss to balance competing alignment dimensions.
H3Fusion formulates alignment with a dual objective that leverages distances between generated embeddings and alignment embeddings.
H3Fusion introduces a gating loss that canalizes activations on contributing experts.
Evaluations on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that H3Fusion yields higher helpfulness, lower harmfulness, and greater honesty than individually aligned models.
H3Fusion provides stronger robustness than state-of-the-art LLM ensemble and model-merging approaches.
MLRBench is a synthetic benchmark for multilingual long-context reasoning that includes bAbI-style tasks testing multi-hop inference, aggregation, and epistemic reasoning.
MLRBench spans seven languages, is parallel, resistant to leakage, and scalable to arbitrary context lengths.

Experiments with an open-weight large language model reveal a pronounced performance gap between high- and low-resource languages, especially on tasks requiring aggregation of multiple facts or prediction of absent information.
Large language models effectively utilize only a small fraction of their claimed context length in multilingual settings.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation alleviates but does not solve the long-context problem for multilingual large language models.
MLRBench is open-sourced to enable future research in the evaluation and training of multilingual large language models.
Self-Destruct replaces selected weights in pre-trained language model layers with values that trigger a system error when the model engages in targeted behaviors.
Self-Destruct leaves normal language model functionality unaffected.
Self-Destruct introduces no inference overhead and does not require auxiliary models.
Experiments with five language model families demonstrate that Self-Destruct protects against jailbreak attacks while preserving accuracy on standard benchmarks.
Self-Destruct mitigates biased text generation.
Self-Destruct enables model fingerprinting.
Cetvel is a benchmark that evaluates large language models in Turkish across discriminative and generative tasks.
Cetvel includes 23 tasks grouped into seven categories, covering grammatical error correction, machine translation, and question answering rooted in Turkish history and idiomatic language.
Evaluation of 33 open-weight large language models up to 70 B parameters shows that Turkish-centric instruction-tuned models generally underperform compared to multilingual or general-purpose models.
Grammatical error correction and extractive question answering tasks are particularly discriminative for differentiating model capabilities.
Cetvel provides a comprehensive and culturally grounded evaluation suite for advancing the development and assessment of Turkish large language models.

Agent-Testing Agent (ATA) automates testing and evaluation of conversational AI agents by integrating static code analysis, developer interrogation, literature mining, and persona-driven adversarial test generation with difficulty adaptation via judge feedback.
ATA scores each dialogue with an LLM-as-a-Judge rubric and uses the scores to direct subsequent tests toward the agent’s weakest capabilities.
ATA discovers more diverse and severe failures in a travel planner and a Wikipedia writer than expert annotators while achieving comparable severity assessment.
ATA completes testing in a fraction of the time required by multi-annotator rounds.
Removing code analysis and web search from ATA increases result variance and miscalibration, highlighting the value of evidence-grounded test generation.
ATA provides quantitative metrics and qualitative bug reports for developers.
An open-source implementation of ATA is released.
EM-MIA performs membership inference by iteratively refining prefix effectiveness and membership scores using an expectation‑maximization strategy without labeled non‑member examples.
OLMoMIA provides a benchmark that enables systematic analysis of membership inference attack robustness under varied distributional overlap and difficulty.
EM-MIA achieves higher membership inference performance than prior baselines, especially when training and test distributions are clearly separable.
EM-MIA succeeds in practical settings with partial distributional overlap between training and non‑member data.
Current membership inference methods fail under near‑identical distributional conditions, revealing fundamental limitations.
The DSC benchmark provides an evaluation framework that categorizes router performance across diverse query types and incorporates privacy and safety assessments.
Preference-based routers and commercial routers improve computational efficiency.
Routers frequently make suboptimal, category-driven routing decisions, such as directing all coding and mathematics queries to the most powerful LLM even when simpler models would suffice.
Routers route jailbreaking attempts to weaker models, thereby increasing safety risks.
A computational investigation of visual metonymy is presented.
A novel pipeline grounded in semiotic theory leverages large language models and text-to-image models to generate metonymic visual representations.
ViMET, a visual metonymy dataset of multiple-choice questions for evaluating cognitive reasoning in multimodal language models, is constructed.
A substantial performance gap is observed between humans and state-of-the-art vision-language models on visual metonymy tasks.
SCoPE VLM leverages a Chain of Scroll mechanism to selectively and recursively navigate documents, focusing on relevant segments.
A dedicated data generation pipeline constructs informative Chain of Scroll trajectories for training document navigation.
Episodic Group Relative Policy Optimization is a reinforcement learning method that bridges the gap between training and inference for document navigation.
SCoPE VLM substantially reduces memory usage compared to existing long-input processing approaches.
SCoPE VLM models human-like reading behaviors in multi-page document question answering.
SCoPE VLM is the first framework to explicitly model agentic reading patterns in multi-page document question answering.

S-MedQA is an English medical question-answering dataset for benchmarking large language models in fine-grained clinical specialties, containing over 24,000 examples across 15 specialties with multi-specialty annotations.
S-MedQA was constructed using both machine and expert verification to maximize data availability and reliability.
Training on data from a clinical specialty does not necessarily yield the best performance on that specialty in medical question answering.
Token probabilities of clinically relevant terms consistently increase across all specialties regardless of the specialty used for fine-tuning.
Improvement gains observed in the experiments are primarily derived from domain shifting (general to medical) rather than from injecting specialty-specific knowledge.
S-MedQA and the code required to reproduce the experiments are released for the research community.
Instant Confidence COMET is an uncertainty-aware quality estimation model that matches prior performance while using a fraction of the computational cost.
Early-Exit COMET computes quality scores and associated confidences at early model layers, enabling early exiting to reduce evaluation cost.
Early-Exit COMET combined with an upper confidence bound bandit algorithm can identify the best translation candidate without evaluating all candidates with the full model.
The proposed methods reduce required compute for evaluation and reranking while incurring minimal performance degradation.
Instant Confidence COMET can be used to prioritize which translations are sent to human evaluators instead of relying solely on COMET scores.
AUGMENT generates controlled paraphrases grounded in user behaviors.
AUGMENT leverages linguistically informed rules and enforces quality through checks on instruction adherence, semantic similarity, and realism.
Controlled paraphrases generated by AUGMENT uncover systematic weaknesses in the BBQ dataset.
Controlled paraphrases generated by AUGMENT uncover systematic weaknesses in the MMLU dataset.
STAMP is a framework for task-aware text privatization that selectively allocates privacy budgets across tokens based on token importance to downstream tasks and privacy sensitivity.
The polar mechanism perturbs only the direction of token embeddings on the unit sphere while preserving their magnitude.
Decoding in STAMP is performed via cosine nearest-neighbor search, aligning perturbation geometry with decoding geometry.
The polar mechanism maintains semantic neighborhoods in the embedding space and better preserves downstream utility compared to isotropic noise mechanisms.

Experimental evaluations on SQuAD, Yelp, and AG News demonstrate that STAMP combined with the normalized polar mechanism achieves superior privacy-utility trade-offs across varying per-token privacy budgets.
HiVisTask is a Hinglish multimodal, multidomain, persona-based task-oriented dialogue dataset.
G3 TOD enhances personalization by incorporating three structured knowledge graphs—entity context, user persona, and commonsense reasoning—extracted from conversation history.
G3 TOD outperforms standard and ablated baselines on task-oriented dialogue evaluation.
Structured and selective contextualization improves the generation of personalized and engaging multimodal responses.
A safety-aware layer swapping method transfers safety alignment from an English safety expert to low-resource language experts without additional training.
The method adaptively selects or blends modules based on their degree of specialization.
The method preserves performance on general language understanding benchmarks while enhancing safety in target languages.
The method achieves comparable performance to language experts on general benchmarks such as MMMLU, BELEBELE, and MGSM.
The method produces more aligned and less harmful responses on the MultiJail safety benchmark.
An existing benchmark is extended with knowledge extraction annotations for evaluating triple extraction in web-based question answering.
Commercial and open-source large language models of varying sizes are evaluated on the extended benchmark for knowledge extraction performance.
Knowledge extraction from semi-structured web content remains a challenging task for large language models.
Augmenting large language models with extracted triples improves their performance in web-based question answering.
Incorporating knowledge extraction through multi-task learning benefits large language models in web-based question answering.
Strategies are identified to maximize large language model effectiveness across different model sizes and resource settings in web-based question answering.
ChronoQA is a QA benchmark that measures temporal, causal, and character consistency understanding in narrative documents under retrieval-augmented generation.

Entity-Event RAG (E2RAG) is a dual-graph framework that maintains separate entity and event subgraphs linked by a bipartite mapping, preserving temporal and causal information for fine-grained reasoning.

Entity-Event RAG (E2RAG) achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art unstructured and knowledge-graph RAG baselines on ChronoQA, particularly on causal and character consistency queries.
The classify-and-count approach for evaluating attribute control success yields biased and inconsistent results, with estimates varying significantly across classifiers.
Control success estimation is framed as a quantification task and addressed with a hybrid Bayesian method that combines classifier predictions with a small number of human labels for calibration.
A test dataset containing both human-rated and automatically rated samples across two modalities is collected for evaluating control success.
The hybrid Bayesian method produces robust estimates of control success across both text and text-to-image generation tasks.
The hybrid Bayesian method provides a principled alternative to current evaluation practices for attribute control success.
Detoxifiability-Aware Detoxification (DID) adaptively selects filtering or paraphrasing for each toxic comment based on its detoxifiability.
DID incorporates an unsupervised detoxifiability discriminator.
DID incorporates a semantic purification module that extracts harmful intents and performs targeted paraphrasing on detoxifiable comments.
DID incorporates a feedback-adaptive refinement loop that processes remaining harmful contents only when they are detoxifiable.
DID outperforms existing comment detoxification approaches on academic and industrial data, establishing a novel practical modeling paradigm.
A contrastive learning framework models narrative salience by learning story embeddings from narrative twins, which are stories that share the same plot but differ in surface form.
The framework trains a model to distinguish a story from both its narrative twin and a distractor with similar surface features but a different plot.
Four narratologically motivated operations—deletion, shifting, disruption, and summarization—are evaluated for inferring salience using the learned story embeddings.
Contrastively learned story embeddings outperform a masked-language-model baseline on short narratives from the ROCStories corpus and longer Wikipedia plot summaries.
Summarization is the most reliable operation for identifying salient sentences among the evaluated operations.
Random dropout can generate narrative twins from a single story when narrative twins are not available.
Effective distractors can be obtained by prompting large language models or, for long-form narratives, by using different parts of the same story.
Surprisal estimates from 17 pre-trained transformer language models across three families were evaluated on two fMRI datasets.
An inverse scaling relationship between models' per-word estimated probability and model fit to fMRI data is observed across the evaluated models and datasets.
The inverse scaling trend is demonstrated to extend beyond latency-based measures to fMRI data.
CRADLE Bench is a benchmark for multi-faceted mental health crisis and safety risk detection covering seven clinically defined crisis types.
CRADLE Bench incorporates temporal labels for crisis detection.
CRADLE Bench provides 600 clinician-annotated evaluation examples and 420 development examples.
CRADLE Bench includes a training corpus of approximately 4,000 examples automatically labeled by a majority-vote ensemble of multiple language models, which outperforms single-model annotation.

Six crisis detection models are fine-tuned on subsets of the training data defined by consensus and unanimous ensemble agreement, yielding complementary models trained under different agreement criteria.
COMPACT models cross-clause dependencies through structured clause graphs.
COMPACT extracts deontic-temporal entities from clauses and constructs typed relationship graphs that capture definitional dependencies, exception hierarchies, and temporal sequences.
ACE is a benchmark containing 4,700 compliance scenarios derived from 633 real-world contracts covering 26 agreement types, each requiring multi-hop reasoning across multiple clauses.
Multi-clause reasoning is identified as a fundamental challenge for state-of-the-art models, which achieve low base accuracy on such tasks.
Training on ACE yields substantial improvements on compliance tasks and also enhances general legal reasoning performance on other benchmarks.
A statistically grounded criterion directly identifies the optimal set of embedding dimensions for each query at inference time.
The criterion improves retrieval effectiveness while reducing embedding size by roughly half at inference time across multiple models and datasets.
LingGen enables fine-grained control over a large number of real-valued linguistic attributes in text generation.
LingGen conditions a language model by injecting attribute representations into the beginning-of-sequence embeddings.
P-MASKING samples per-example attribute masking rates from a truncated Pareto distribution during training to improve robustness across attribute subsets.
LingGen attains lower average control error than evaluated baselines while remaining efficient at inference.
Human evaluation rates LingGen as having higher fluency than compared methods.
Ablation studies demonstrate that Pareto-sampled masking and BOS-based injection outperform alternative masking and integration strategies.

Analysis on two multilingual benchmarks covering dozens of languages reveals that instruction-tuning on high-resource language supervised fine-tuning datasets significantly increases model confidence in low-resource languages while yielding marginal or no accuracy improvements, leading to mis-calibration.
Label smoothing alleviates mis-calibration in multilingual language models, maintaining better calibration across all languages without requiring low-resource supervised fine-tuning data.
A connector-sharing strategy based on linguistic family membership enables using one connector per language family in LLM-powered automatic speech recognition systems.
The connector-sharing strategy reduces the parameter count compared to training separate connectors for each language.
The connector-sharing strategy improves generalization across domains in multilingual ASR deployment.
The connector-sharing strategy is evaluated across two multilingual large language models and two real-world speech corpora, including curated and crowd-sourced data.
DDAIR mitigates ambiguous class-guided augmented examples generated by large language models for intent recognition.
DDAIR uses Sentence Transformers to detect synthetic examples that are semantically more similar to an unintended intent than to their target intent.
DDAIR includes an iterative re-generation method that reduces ambiguity in augmented examples.
Sentence embeddings effectively help to (re)generate less ambiguous examples for intent recognition.
DDAIR improves classification performance in low-resource scenarios with loosely or broadly defined intents.
SCALAR is a benchmark that assesses citation-grounded long-context reasoning in academic writing.
SCALAR automatically generates high-quality ground-truth labels from academic papers and their citation structure without human annotation.
SCALAR features controllable difficulty levels and a dynamic updating mechanism that mitigates data contamination.
SCALAR includes a multiple-choice question answering task and a cloze-style citation prediction task for evaluating citation-grounded long-context reasoning.
The multiple-choice task of SCALAR distinguishes model capabilities, with human experts achieving substantially higher accuracy than state-of-the-art LLMs.
The cloze-style citation prediction task of SCALAR is more challenging, with no model attaining high accuracy.
Perplexity of texts written by non-native speakers of Czech is not lower than that of native speakers.
Detectors from three separate families exhibit no systematic bias against non-native speakers of Czech.
Contemporary GPT detectors operate effectively without relying on perplexity.
