Last-token pooling without a special end-of-sequence token makes vision-language encoders highly sensitive to end-of-input formatting artifacts such as punctuation or newline characters.
Introducing a dedicated end-of-sequence token into vision-language encoders removes sensitivity to input formatting artifacts and increases robustness.
Mean pooling avoids the sensitivity to end-of-input artifacts that is observed with last-token pooling lacking a special sequence-end token.
A search corpus of 16 million biomedical paper abstracts is released.
A factoid QA dataset named PaperSearchQA containing 60,000 samples answerable from the corpus is constructed.
Benchmarks for the PaperSearchQA dataset are provided.
Search agents trained in the PaperSearchQA environment outperform non-reinforcement-learning retrieval baselines.
Quantitative analysis shows that trained search agents exhibit planning, reasoning, and self-verification behaviors.
The data creation methods for the corpus and dataset are scalable and can be extended to other scientific domains.
A method learns subword correspondences in parallel sentence pairs using the EM algorithm.
Direct modeling of subword correspondences in parallel corpora improves segmentation consistency across languages.
The method improves translation accuracy across multiple machine translation tasks.
A new Wason Selection Task dataset encodes deontic modality to distinguish deontic from descriptive conditionals.
The dataset is used to evaluate large language models' conditional reasoning under deontic rules.
Error patterns of large language models on the dataset are analyzed to compare confirmation bias and matching bias explanations.
Large language models reason more effectively with deontic rules than with descriptive rules.
Large language models exhibit matching-bias-like errors on the dataset.
Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is applied as a critic-free reinforcement learning method to improve dialogue state tracking accuracy under low-resource conditions.
The GRPO-based reinforcement learning approach achieves superior performance on MultiWOZ 2.1 and 2.4 compared to existing methods while using significantly reduced out-of-domain training data.
Models pretrained for tool-use tasks serve as a better starting point for dialogue state tracking, particularly at small scales.
A unified account based on circuit complexity, approximation theory, and communication complexity explains why transformer architectures struggle to implement exact discrete algorithms.
Depth constraints, difficulty approximating discontinuities, and inter-token communication bottlenecks constitute key challenges for transformers in discrete reasoning.
Recent theoretical advances are synthesized to clarify structural and computational limitations of transformers on symbolic computations.
Implications for model design and promising directions for overcoming foundational transformer limitations in discrete reasoning are identified.
MiSCHiEF provides two benchmarking datasets, MiC and MiS, that use minimally differing image-caption pairs to assess fine-grained safety and cultural alignment in vision-language models.
MiSCHiEF employs a contrastive pair design where each sample contains minimally differing captions and corresponding minimally differing images.
Evaluation of four vision-language models on MiSCHiEF shows that models are more accurate at confirming the correct image-caption pair than at rejecting an incorrect one.
Evaluation shows that models achieve higher accuracy when selecting the correct caption for a given image than when selecting the correct image for a given caption.
Results highlight persistent modality misalignment challenges in current vision-language models for tasks requiring precise cross-modal grounding of subtle semantic and visual distinctions.
Korean Canonical Legal Benchmark (KCL) assesses language models’ legal reasoning capabilities independently of domain-specific knowledge.
KCL provides question-level supporting precedents to disentangle reasoning ability from parameterized knowledge.

KCL comprises KCL-MCQA with multiple-choice questions and aligned precedents, and KCL-Essay with open-ended generation problems, aligned precedents, and instance-level rubrics for automated evaluation.
Systematic evaluation of more than 30 models reveals large performance gaps, particularly on KCL-Essay.
Reasoning-specialized models consistently outperform general-purpose models on KCL.
A domain-adapted image encoder training method for manga is proposed.
The method trains image encoders to acquire visual features that consider the structural and sequential characteristics of manga.
The trained image encoders are used as visual processors in a multimodal Japanese‑English machine translation model.
The method improves translation evaluation metrics such as BLEU and xCOMET compared to conventional approaches.
Learning While Evaluating (LWE) enables evaluators to improve sequentially at inference time without additional training or external signals.
LWE maintains an evolving meta-prompt that stores evaluation insights derived from self-generated feedback during sequential testing.
LWE uses the evolving meta-prompt to generate sample-specific evaluation instructions for accurate and consistent judgments.
Selective LWE updates the meta-prompt only on self-inconsistent cases, thereby focusing computation where it matters most.
Selective LWE outperforms strong baselines on pairwise comparison benchmarks, demonstrating that evaluators can improve during sequential testing with a simple selective update.
A summary and analysis of the EACL 2026 Student Research Workshop Mentorship Program is provided using structured exit surveys collected from mentors and mentees.
Overall satisfaction with the mentorship program is evaluated.
Systematic strengths and weaknesses of the mentorship process are identified.
Recommendations to improve alignment of expectations and program logistics are offered.
Empirical data for future workshop organizers and meta-research on early-career support within the NLP community are supplied.
The proposed tokenization method emphasizes Korean morphological structures in eojeol and supports both inter- and intra-eojeol segmentation to select subwords based on morphemes.
A language model pretrained with the morpheme-based tokenization method outperforms existing approaches on Korean benchmark tasks.
The morpheme-based tokenization method produces fewer tokens per input sequence than existing tokenizers, indicating higher efficiency for Korean language modeling.

Divide, Generate, Recombine, and Compare (DGRC) divides a dialogue prompt, generates continuations for subparts using language models, recombines the dialogue and continuations, and compares the likelihoods of the recombined sequences.
DGRC mitigates bias in linguistic analyses of language models.
DGRC enables systematic testing of discourse-sensitive behavior in language models.
Language models prefer to continue dialogue on at-issue content.
The preference for at-issue continuation is stronger in instruction-tuned language models.
Language models reduce their at-issue preference when relevant cues such as "Hey, wait a minute" are present.
Instruction-tuning does not further amplify the modulation of at-issue preference by relevant cues.
T2-RAGBench is a benchmark for evaluating retrieval-augmented generation on real-world text-and-table data.
T2-RAGBench includes a large collection of question-context-answer triples for assessing RAG methods.
T2-RAGBench converts existing state-of-the-art datasets into a context-independent format validated by experts.
Hybrid BM25, which combines dense and sparse vectors, is identified as the most effective retrieval technique for text-and-table data.
Ablation studies reveal that the choice of embedding model and the size of the corpus influence retrieval performance on text-and-table data.
T2-RAGBench presents a difficult evaluation scenario for state-of-the-art large language models and retrieval-augmented generation methods.
Persuasion tokens are special tokens trained to replicate the effect of in-context knowledge editing demonstrations, enabling knowledge editing without fact-specific demonstrations.
Persuasion tokens achieve editing performance comparable to, and often exceeding, in-context knowledge editing across two editing datasets and three large language models.
Editing performance with persuasion tokens is robust to distractors that have small negative effects on neighboring facts.
Increasing the number of persuasion tokens improves editing performance.
Persuasion tokens provide a more practical and scalable alternative for editing large language models.
Semantic token clustering groups tokens into semantically consistent clusters using embedding clustering and prefix matching.
A cluster-based score computed at each decoding step quantifies uncertainty in large language model outputs.
The uncertainty quantification method operates with a single generation and without auxiliary models.
The method attains uncertainty quantification performance comparable to existing baselines while markedly lowering computational overhead.
The approach leverages semantic information inherently encoded in large language models to achieve efficient uncertainty quantification.
A synthetic IPA OCR dataset generated from Wiktionary is introduced.
Fine-tuning Tesseract, Calamari, and Qwen2.5-VL-7B with the synthetic dataset improves recognition of IPA characters.
Off-the-shelf OCR frameworks Tesseract, Calamari, and Qwen2.5-VL-7B exhibit poor zero-shot performance on IPA characters due to character set mismatch.
OCR performance is evaluated on IPA text extracted from grammar book PDFs for Japhug and Kagayanen languages.
WebRollback introduces an explicit rollback mechanism that allows a web agent to revert to a previous navigation state.
The explicit rollback mechanism gives web agents flexibility to directly control the search process.
Direct control of the search process improves the effectiveness and efficiency of web navigation.
WebRollback demonstrates effectiveness on two live web navigation benchmarks in both zero-shot and fine-tuning settings.
A causal loop diagram models how credibility, trust, and information processing co‑evolve in public understanding of scientific information.
The diagram identifies a trust‑centered feedback loop that can foster social cohesion or accelerate truth decay.
The diagram identifies a feedback loop linking individual trust dynamics to broader patterns of polarization and unity.
A shared set of source, message, channel, and receiver variables is synthesized from a scoping literature review of communication models.
Distilling step-by-step formal reasoning into language models improves their pronominal reasoning abilities.
DeepSeek-distilled models achieve statistically significant performance gains on all evaluated English pronoun resolution and pronoun fidelity datasets compared to their original counterparts.
Performance improvements are observed across granular error types and arise from plausible-looking reasoning chains that employ a variety of reasoning strategies.
Even after improvement, the models perform only slightly above random chance on the pronoun resolution and fidelity datasets, indicating considerable remaining headroom.
Process Reward Models improve performance for instruct mathematical models but do not improve and can degrade reasoning model performance.
Linear probe analysis identifies reward prediction patterns that distinguish reasoning model outputs from non-reasoning model outputs.
Sparse Autoencoders trained on Qwen2.5-Math-PRM reveal that most learned features respond to formatting artifacts such as whitespace, Unicode tokens, and punctuation rather than mathematical content.
Reasoning model outputs display distinct metacognitive patterns that are absent from standard mathematical solutions.
Trained Sparse Autoencoders are released as resources for reward model interpretability research.
An evaluation framework for fine-grained controllability of single- and dual-concept scenarios in large language models is introduced.
The evaluation framework focuses on linguistically distinct concept pairs such as persuasiveness versus humor.
Performance often drops in dual-concept settings across multiple large language models and generative tasks.
Naive prompting-based control struggles with compositionality even when concepts are intuitively independent.
The framework offers a principled approach for measuring the ability of future methods to achieve multi-concept control.
CAPID fine-tunes a locally owned small language model to filter sensitive information before it is passed to large language models for question answering.
A synthetic data generation pipeline leverages large language models to create a diverse, domain-rich dataset covering multiple personally identifiable information types and relevance levels.
The fine-tuned small language model detects personally identifiable information spans, classifies their types, and estimates contextual relevance.

Relevance-aware personally identifiable information detection with the fine-tuned small language model outperforms existing baselines in span, relevance, and type accuracy while preserving higher downstream utility under anonymization.
A dataset of lengthy simplified English texts paired with first-order logic representations spanning thousands of sentences is generated.
An evidence retrieval task for contradiction detection in long-context texts is introduced.
Evidence retrieval evaluation shows that LLMs' effective context window collapses with realistic distractors, deteriorating after a relatively small number of sentences.
lrnnx implements several modern linear recurrent neural network architectures under a common interface.
lrnnx provides multiple levels of control, allowing direct work with core components or higher-level model abstractions.
lrnnx improves accessibility, reproducibility, and extensibility of linear recurrent neural network research and applications.
Luth is a family of French-specialized small language models obtained via targeted post-training on curated French data.
Luth models outperform all open-source small language models of comparable size on multiple French benchmarks while retaining English capabilities.
Strategic model merging improves performance of Luth models in both French and English.
Luth establishes a new state of the art for French small language models.

A systematic investigation of temporal misalignment in factuality benchmarks is conducted by examining five popular factuality benchmarks and eight large language models released across different years.
An up-to-date fact retrieval pipeline is developed to quantify benchmark aging for large language model factuality evaluation.
Three metrics are introduced to quantify benchmark aging and its impact on large language model factuality evaluation.
A considerable portion of samples in widely used factuality benchmarks are found to be outdated, leading to unreliable assessments of large language model factuality.
A testbed is provided to assess the reliability of benchmarks for large language model factuality evaluation.
A single linear projection of an appropriate hidden layer encodes the log-magnitudes of numerals, enabling recovery of numbers with low relative error.
The hidden state after reading a pair of numerals encodes their ranking, allowing a linear classifier to achieve high accuracy in predicting the order.
LLMs exhibit substantially lower accuracy when explicitly asked to rank numeral pairs than the accuracy achieved by the internal ranking probe.
Incorporating the classifier probe’s log-loss as an auxiliary objective during finetuning improves verbalized numerical reasoning accuracy.
An object-aligned auxiliary view is constructed to improve visual contrastive decoding for mitigating object hallucinations in multimodal large language models.
Object-centric attention from self-supervised Vision Transformers is leveraged to identify and remove the most salient visual evidence for auxiliary view creation.
The auxiliary view disrupts unsupported tokens and yields a stronger contrast signal in visual contrastive decoding.
The method is prompt-agnostic, model-agnostic, and can be integrated into existing visual contrastive decoding pipelines with minimal computational overhead.
The method achieves consistent performance improvements on two object hallucination benchmarks across two multimodal large language models.
MEET-MR provides fine-grained human judgments of English–Thai translation quality using the MQM annotation framework.
MEET-MR includes human preference rankings and reference translations for English–Thai machine translation.
MEET-MR enables both absolute and relative assessment of English–Thai translation quality.
MEET-MR covers nine diverse domains, providing linguistic and contextual diversity for English–Thai translation evaluation.
MEET-MR can be used to study translation quality estimation for English–Thai machine translation.
MEET-MR can be used to study model alignment with human evaluation for English–Thai machine translation.
MEET-MR can be used to study cross-domain performance for English–Thai machine translation.
MWE-aware linguistic tokenization is formulated as a character-level and token-level sequence labeling problem.
A systematic evaluation of MWE-aware tokenization methods is performed on English, German, Italian, and Dutch data.
A two-stage pipeline consisting of UDPipe pretokenization followed by MaChAmp postprocessing consistently achieves the highest tokenization accuracy.
Error pattern analysis reveals how different tokenization architectures trade off oversegmentation and undersegmentation.
Transformer-based postprocessing pipelines are identified as a strong and general strategy for non-standard tokenization.
Leaf-guided computation pruning strategy accelerates inference of Graph-Generating State Space Models without altering the graph topology.
The pruning strategy selectively scales or bypasses secondary refinement computations for high-dissimilarity leaf nodes while preserving the low-weight minimum spanning tree backbone.

Experiments on multiple long-term time series forecasting benchmarks show that the pruning strategy yields consistent throughput improvements with controlled accuracy degradation across various pruning ratios.
Structure-aware computation pruning is demonstrated as an effective mechanism for improving the scalability of graph-based state space models.

∞-MoE selects a portion of parameters of large feed-forward networks using continuous values sampled per token, enabling an infinite number of experts in a continuous space while preserving computational efficiency.
A GPT-2 Small-based ∞-MoE model attains performance comparable to a dense GPT-2 Medium model despite having fewer total parameters.
Adjusting the number of sampled experts at inference time provides a flexible trade‑off between accuracy and speed, yielding higher accuracy than conventional MoE.
Large Reasoning Models' reasoning in English versus the language of the question is systematically compared on MGSM and GPQA Diamond tasks.
Cognitive attributes in reasoning traces are analyzed to contrast English reasoning with reasoning in the question's language.
English reasoning traces exhibit a higher presence of cognitive behaviors than reasoning in the question's language.
Reasoning in English generally yields higher final-answer accuracy than reasoning in the question's language, with the accuracy gap increasing as task complexity grows.
A failure mode called “Lost in Translation” is identified, where translation steps introduce errors that would be avoided by reasoning in the question's language.
ClinSQL is a benchmark of 633 expert-annotated clinical text-to-SQL tasks on MIMIC-IV v3.1 that require multi-table joins, clinically meaningful filters, and executable SQL.
Solving ClinSQL requires navigating schema metadata and clinical coding systems, handling long contexts, and composing multi-step queries beyond traditional text-to-SQL.

Evaluation of 20 proprietary and open-source models on ClinSQL is performed using Chain-of-Thought self-refinement and rubric-based SQL analysis with execution checks that prioritize critical clinical requirements.
Current state-of-the-art models achieve execution performance on ClinSQL that remains far from clinical reliability.
DeepSeek-R1 leads open-source models on ClinSQL execution performance.
Gemini-2.5-Pro shows a substantial performance drop on harder ClinSQL tasks compared to easier ones.
TimeRes is a Turkish benchmark for evaluating temporal understanding of large language models.
TimeRes investigates comprehension of Reichenbach’s temporal points and reported speech via date arithmetic.
TimeRes includes a paired question formulation to separate temporal discourse understanding from temporal arithmetic capabilities.
Evaluation of six large language models shows that they struggle to resolve reported speech.
Evaluation shows that large language models fail to generalize across word order variations in temporal tasks.
Vacuous Neutrality Framework (VaNeu) provides a multi-dimensional evaluation paradigm to assess fairness of small language models across biases, utility, ambiguity handling, and positional bias.
A large-scale audit evaluates nine small language models from four families in the 0.5–5B parameter range under both ambiguous and disambiguated contexts.
Small language models that exhibit low bias in early evaluation stages often fail subsequent stages, revealing hidden vulnerabilities and unreliable reasoning.
Vacuous Neutrality Framework serves as a principled tool for responsible deployment of small language models in socially sensitive settings.
Black-box prompt optimization techniques are repurposed to systematically search for safety failures in large language models.
Three black-box optimizers are applied via DSPy to prompts from HarmfulQA and JailbreakBench, optimizing toward a continuous danger score provided by an independent evaluator model.
Adaptive prompt optimization substantially reduces the effectiveness of safety safeguards in large language models, with a pronounced effect on open-source small models.
Static safety benchmark collections underestimate residual risk because they do not account for adaptive adversarial prompt refinement.
Automated adaptive red-teaming is identified as a necessary component of robust safety evaluation for large language models.
LEMUR provides a large-scale multilingual corpus of EU environmental legislation covering 25 languages.
Lexical Content Score (LCS) quantifies the fidelity of PDF-to-text conversion by measuring lexical consistency against authoritative HTML versions in a language-agnostic manner.
Fine-tuning multilingual embedding models with contrastive objectives in monolingual and bilingual settings improves Top‑k retrieval accuracy for legal documents.
Fine-tuned models achieve especially large retrieval gains for low‑resource languages.
Cross‑lingual evaluation demonstrates that retrieval improvements transfer to unseen languages, suggesting that fine‑tuning enhances language‑independent, content‑level legal representations.
The Clinical Fingerprint framework compares structural and ethical integrity of clinical advice from human physicians and language models.
Physicians employ efficient, action-oriented structures to provide clear clinical guidance.
Generic language models often embed critical advice within complex linguistic recursion, increasing patient cognitive load.
Generic language models frequently adopt an unearned authoritative tone and can produce contradictory advice in complex multi-symptom cases.
Identifying rhetorical gaps demonstrates that domain-specific fine-tuning is required for AI assistants to maintain epistemic humility and logical cohesion in medical practice.

The Reasoning Model Confidence estimation Benchmark (RMCB) provides a public dataset of 347,496 reasoning traces from six large reasoning models across clinical, financial, legal, mathematical, and general reasoning domains, with correctness annotations for all samples.

An empirical evaluation of more than ten representation-based confidence estimation methods for large reasoning models reveals a persistent trade-off between discrimination (AUROC) and calibration (ECE).
Text-based encoders achieve the highest discrimination (AUROC) among the evaluated confidence estimation methods.
Structurally-aware models achieve the lowest calibration error (ECE) among the evaluated confidence estimation methods.
No confidence estimation method simultaneously attains the best discrimination and calibration performance.

Increased architectural complexity does not reliably outperform simpler sequential baselines for confidence estimation, indicating a performance ceiling for methods relying solely on chunk-level hidden states.
A set of Polish vision-language models is created by reproducing and adapting the LLaVA-Next methodology.
An automated pipeline translates and filters existing multimodal datasets and adds synthetic Polish data for OCR and culturally specific tasks to adapt vision-language models to Polish.
The adapted Polish vision-language models achieve a significant improvement over LLaVA-1.6-Vicuna-13B on a Polish-adapted MMBench benchmark.
The adapted Polish vision-language models generate higher-quality captions as judged by human annotators for linguistic correctness.
Large-scale automated translation combined with lightweight filtering can effectively bootstrap high-quality multimodal models for low-resource languages.
Smaller fine-tuned models consistently outperform larger LLMs in both fine-tuning and in-context learning settings for Hindi post-ASR correction.
Zero-shot in-context learning exhibits an n-shaped inverse scaling trend where mid-sized LLMs degrade performance before marginal recovery at extreme scales, yet still lag behind fine-tuned models.
ByT5 is more effective for character-level corrections such as transliteration and word segmentation in Hindi post-ASR correction.
mT5 is more effective for addressing broader semantic inconsistencies in Hindi post-ASR correction.
Mitigation strategies are introduced to preserve domain fidelity in out-of-domain post-ASR correction, and similar performance trends are observed in Marathi and Telugu.
A semantically controlled, stylistically diverse text dataset is created for analyzing writing style perception under controlled perturbations.
Human evaluation experiments compare writing style recognition accuracy between computational models and human readers.
A semantic stability framework is introduced for constructing explainable biomedical knowledge graphs.
The framework is applied to extract Substance Use Disorders and Social Determinants of Health information from the PubMed corpus.
Multiple large language models perform extraction under shared semantic constraints, with disagreements resolved through human-in-the-loop validation.
Semantic stability is defined through named entity recognition and relation extraction metrics, and stabilized gold data are used for model training and evaluation.
A claim-centered knowledge graph is developed where edges encode evidence, provenance, relation type, directionality, polarity, and stability indicators.
The benchmark and pipeline enable multi-hop reasoning, triadic SUD–SDOH–SUD mediation patterns, and feedback loop analysis.
EmoSocInflu dataset comprises 238 Polish dialogues annotated with span-level labels for 11 emotional social influence techniques.
Current large language models demonstrate limited effectiveness in accurately detecting fine-grained emotional social influence techniques in text dialogues.
Human pairwise preference evaluation of LLM-generated explanations reveals that completeness and soundness most strongly influence overall preference.
A human evaluation framework using four criteria—comprehensibility, cognitive coherence, completeness, and soundness—assesses LLM-generated explanations of emotional social influence techniques.
PICQ-drama is a benchmark of context-aware choice questions annotated with missing persona dimensions whose absence leads to ambiguous user choices.
A set of diverse evaluation criteria for missing persona identification is designed.
Leading large language models are benchmarked on PICQ-drama, demonstrating the feasibility of identifying missing relevant personas for faithful user simulation.
Analyses of benchmark results reveal cognitive differences between large language models and humans and highlight distinct roles of different persona categories in shaping responses.
EMBR reformulates KL-regularized reinforcement learning as an energy-based distribution matching problem for diffusion language models.
EMBR provides a scalable off-policy framework that avoids the overhead of on-policy learning and the variance of importance weighting.
A principled upper bound for the energy matching objective is derived and can be used to fine-tune diffusion language models.
EMBR achieves comparable or superior performance to diffu-GRPO and related baselines in online settings and to DPO in offline settings.
A comprehensive empirical study compares vanilla and advanced retrieval-augmented generation methods across eight diverse multi-domain conversational QA datasets.
Retrieval quality and answer generation are evaluated using both generator metrics and retrieval metrics within a unified experimental setup.
The study analyzes how RAG performance evolves across conversation turns.
Robust yet straightforward methods such as reranking, hybrid BM25, and HyDE consistently outperform vanilla RAG in multi-turn conversational QA.
Several advanced RAG techniques fail to provide gains and can degrade performance below a No-RAG baseline.
Dataset characteristics and dialogue length strongly influence retrieval effectiveness in conversational QA.
Effective conversational RAG depends more on alignment between the retrieval strategy and dataset structure than on method complexity.
A lightweight, interpretable framework for multimodal sarcasm detection combines frozen text, audio, and visual embeddings from pretrained encoders through compact fusion heads.
Early fusion of textual and acoustic features outperforms the best unimodal baseline on the MUStARD++Balanced dataset.
Sarcasm expressed through overt prosodic and visual cues is easier to detect than monotone, context-dependent sarcasm, as shown by character-specific evaluation.
Leave-one-speaker-out experiments demonstrate that the framework generalizes to different characters.

Ablation-style transfer experiments on two speakers with similar sarcasm distributions reveal that effective multimodal sarcasm understanding can emerge from frozen, resource-efficient representations without large-scale fine-tuning.
RECIPE-TKG is a parameter-efficient framework for temporal knowledge graph completion that incorporates rule-based multi-hop sampling, contrastive fine-tuning, and test-time semantic filtering.

Systematic analysis under unified evaluation reveals that retrieval strategies in existing LLM-based temporal knowledge graph completion miss multi-hop dependencies when target entities are not directly observed in history.
Systematic analysis under unified evaluation reveals that standard fine-tuning of LLMs for temporal knowledge graph completion reinforces memorization over relational generalization.
Systematic analysis under unified evaluation reveals that uncalibrated generation in LLM-based temporal knowledge graph completion produces contextually implausible entities.
RECIPE-TKG outperforms prior LLM-based methods for temporal knowledge graph completion across input regimes.
RECIPE-TKG achieves particularly strong gains when historical evidence is sparse or indirect.
Multi-step knowledge distillation uses staged, size-aware transfer paths to preserve teacher knowledge across students of varying scales.
Multi-step knowledge distillation improves ROUGE-L and perplexity compared to single-step distillation.
Multi-step knowledge distillation does not require specialized fine-tuning.
Multi-step knowledge distillation provides a simple yet powerful framework for progressively compressing large language models into efficient, high-performing small language models.
Fine-tuned encoder‑transformer classifiers are evaluated for classifying LLM‑generated text into multiple‑choice answer options.
A rule‑based pattern‑matching baseline is evaluated for classifying LLM‑generated text into multiple‑choice answer options.
The classifiers are evaluated on three datasets that differ in answer‑option complexity.
The rule‑based baseline outperforms the best neural encoder‑transformer classifier for mapping LLM responses to answer options.
Simple pattern‑matching of answer options against LLM outputs is identified as the most competitive solution for cleaning LLM responses to multiple‑choice questionnaires.
A phrase-level protagonist detection and classification task for moral discourse is defined using the Moralization Corpus.
The task is decomposed into identifying protagonist mentions and classifying them by actor type and argumentative function.
Fine-tuned NER models achieve competitive detection performance at substantially lower computational cost than prompting-based large language models.
Contextualized prompting substantially improves role classification performance compared to non-contextualized prompting.
Top-performing models reach or exceed human-level performance on both protagonist detection and role classification in moral discourse.
Human baselines for protagonist detection and classification are established.
The impact of contextual information on human and model decisions is analyzed.
Code, model predictions, and supplementary material are released publicly.
Funnel pooling is a lightweight trainable in-model readout that constructs routing vectors directly from token-level hidden states without a separate embedding encoder.
Representation choice for the routing vector systematically reshapes the available routing partitions.
In multilingual routing settings, single-vector inputs often encode only shallow features such as language or format, leading to domains organized by these features rather than by topic.
Funnel pooling reduces language- and source-dataset-driven clustering of routing domains.
Funnel pooling results in routing domains that are more aligned with topic semantics.
Funnel pooling maintains competitive downstream routing performance while adding only minor inference overhead.
A computational forensic linguistic profiling of Italian police interrogation transcripts is presented.
Automated extraction of 147 linguistic features is employed to analyze police interrogation transcripts.
Narrative monologues exhibit deeper syntactic embedding, higher past-tense usage, and more first-person singular verbs compared to question‑answer transcripts.
Question‑answer transcripts exhibit longer subordinate chains, more clausal complements, and higher pronoun frequency compared to narrative monologues.
Structurally and legally informed linguistic features reliably distinguish narrative monologue and question‑answer transcription formats.
Computational models capture genre‑specific cues in Italian police interrogation transcripts, providing scalable insights into transcription practices and evidential reliability.
A benchmark for automated language-of-study extraction from computational linguistics publications is introduced, comprising 431 ACL Anthology papers annotated for 62 languages.
SciBERT and four large language models (GPT-4o mini, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Claude 3.5 Haiku, DeepSeek 3.2) are evaluated on the benchmark using different parts of the papers.
GPT-4o mini attains the highest exact match and Jaccard agreement among the evaluated models.
Gemini 2.5 Flash attains the highest micro F1 among the evaluated models.
Extraction using only abstracts achieves competitive performance compared to using full texts, indicating that accurate language-of-study extraction can be achieved without high computational costs.
Large language models can accurately identify languages of study in computational linguistics papers, reducing the time and cost of analyses.
A neuro-symbolic approach generates entailment trees as structured proof trees using large language models.
An inference-guided prompting approach improves entailment tree generation.
A comprehensive evaluation of diverse prompting strategies across multiple models is conducted on the EntailmentBank dataset.
Fine-tuning large language models with data augmentation, curriculum learning, and reinforcement-guided optimization enhances proof generation performance.
The fine-tuned model outperforms all prompting strategies on structural and semantic metrics for proof generation.
Proof tree generation is presented as a benchmark for evaluating structured reasoning in large language models.
CHiRPE predicts psychosis risk from transcribed semi-structured clinical interviews.
CHiRPE generates novel SHAP explanation formats co-developed with clinicians.
CHiRPE integrates symptom-domain mapping, large language model summarisation, and BERT classification.
Clinicians strongly prefer the novel concept-guided explanations, especially hybrid graph-and-text summary formats.
Clinically-guided model development yields accurate and interpretable results.
A systematic review of automatic story generation research from the past four years is conducted.
Major trends and key limitations in story generation methods, model architectures, datasets, and evaluation methodologies are analyzed based on 57 papers.
Developing new evaluation metrics for automatic story generation is proposed.
Creating more suitable datasets for automatic story generation is proposed.
Improving narrative coherence and consistency in story generation and exploring practical applications are advocated.
Active learning with a transformer-based classifier iteratively refines corpora by excluding irrelevant documents.
Active learning improves construct validity of corpora used for quantitative text analysis.
Active learning reduces the labeling effort required for document relevance classification.
Active learning achieves high relevance classification performance with a small number of labeled snippets and outperforms random sampling and weakly supervised sampling.
Filtering non-medical articles has little effect on downstream depression stigmatization measures but increases schizophrenia stigmatization.
Sparsity-Exploiting Backward Pass (SEBP) reduces backward computation in linear layers of Transformer models by exploiting structured sparsity in output gradients.
Output gradients of BERT-based and LLaMA models exhibit pronounced sparsity for short input sequences.
Optimizing the autograd function in linear layers significantly reduces FLOPs during the backward pass.

Sparsity-Exploiting Backward Pass (SEBP) provides substantial backward pass speedup for BERT-base on GLUE tasks and for a 3B LLaMA model on reasoning benchmarks while keeping memory usage comparable to standard fine-tuning.
Sparsity-Exploiting Backward Pass (SEBP) matches standard convergence rates, offering a memory-efficient acceleration of large language model fine-tuning.
A controlled evaluation of tokenizer transfer on monolingually pretrained base models is performed across six languages and multiple finetuning regimes.
Orthogonal Mapping Pursuit and Fast Vocabulary Transfer are implemented as tokenizer transfer methods.
Orthogonal Mapping Pursuit combined with monolingual target finetuning achieves the lowest target-language log-perplexity and highest MultiBlimp accuracy among evaluated conditions.

Tokenizer transfer is demonstrated as a compute-efficient alternative for low-resource language model training by adapting a larger pre-trained model with a target-language tokenizer and fine-tuning on target data.

Byte-normalized log-perplexity and MultiBlimp accuracy are used to assess target-language adaptability, source-language retention, and the interaction between tokenizer transfer and finetuning strategies.
The first annotated dataset of 1,404 word use–to–sign ID mappings between German and German Sign Language is released.
Three correspondence types—one-to-many, many-to-one, and one-to-one—plus No Match cases are defined for lexical sense mapping between spoken German and German Sign Language.
Semantic Similarity using SBERT embeddings outperforms Exact Match for cross-modal sense correspondence.
Semantic Similarity yields substantial performance gains for the one-to-many correspondence type.
Language-specific SAE feature steering is applied to three large language models from two model families and evaluated on translation and multilingual question answering tasks.
SAE feature steering achieves higher target-language accuracy than language neuron-based steering on translation.
SAE feature steering slightly underperforms prompting on translation language accuracy and output quality.
On multilingual question answering, SAE-based steering provides stronger language control than prompting.
Combining SAE-based steering with prompting yields the best overall language control and task performance.
SAE features are demonstrated as a tool for controllable multilingual generation.
A dataset of Polish Reddit comments with bot-comment labels is constructed for comment-level bot detection.
Language composition of a comment is used as a feature for bot detection in multilingual Polish Reddit.
Novel platform-specific, language-specific, and culturally informed features are developed for comment-level bot detection.
Comment-level classifiers trained on the annotated dataset achieve strong performance and generalize temporally to future data.

Cross-level interaction features, Bottiquette compliance signals, formatting markers, language indicators, repetition and randomness measures—especially entropy of non-alphabetic characters—are identified as among the most decisive features for bot detection.
A qualitative characterization of the Polish Reddit bot ecosystem is provided.
A baseline for comment-level bot detection in Polish Reddit is established.
A benchmark of small vision-language models and human annotators on the Monk Skin Tone scale using the MST-E dataset is presented.
A large-scale text-to-image generation study systematically varies income framing in prompts describing occupations, producing thousands of portraits across multiple large vision-language models.
High-income prompts consistently generate lighter-skinned faces in text-to-image generation, revealing strong color bias.
Prompt constraints only modestly reduce the color bias induced by income framing.
The magnitude of color bias varies across vision-language models, with some models exhibiting more pronounced shifts in skin tone than others.
A systematic comparison of single-agent and multi-agent pipelines is performed on four diagram-grounded geometry problem-solving benchmarks (Geometry3K, MathVerse, OlympiadBench, We-Math).
Multi-agent pipelines consistently improve the performance of open-source multimodal language models on diagram-grounded geometry problem-solving benchmarks.
Single-agent pipelines generally outperform multi-agent pipelines for the closed-source Gemini-2.0-Flash model on classic geometry benchmarks.
Multi-agent pipelines yield only modest performance gains for Gemini-2.0-Flash on newer geometry benchmarks.
Agentic decomposition is not universally optimal across all models and benchmarks.
Low-rank adapters (rank 8) fail to capture the complex optimization dynamics required for reasoning under micro-budget training.
High-rank adapters (rank 256) unlock significant plasticity in standard instruction-tuned models under micro-budget training.
Instruction-tuned models under micro-budget RLVR with high-rank adapters extend their chain-of-thought to maximize reward.
Heavily math-aligned models experience performance collapse under noisy low-budget RL updates, indicating destructive interference near a task-specific optimum.
A substantial improvement in Pass@1 on the AIME 24 benchmark is achieved relative to baseline under the micro-budget training regime.
